topics.title: Develop a Simple Publisher and Subscriber
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRUL508 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS) LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Develop a Simple Publisher and Subscriber # Module Experiment 2: Develop a Simple Publisher and Subscriber ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Publisher-Subscriber Pattern in ROS:** > In the Robot Operating System (ROS), communication between nodes is achieved primarily through the **Publisher-Subscriber** messaging paradigm. A **Publisher Node** is a computational process that broadcasts messages of a defined type over a named communication channel called a **Topic**. A **Subscriber Node** is a computational process that registers its interest in a specific topic and asynchronously receives messages published to that topic. This decoupled, asynchronous, and many-to-many communication model is mediated by the **ROS Master**, which enables nodes to discover one another via services such as the **Parameter Server** and the **XMLRPC-based lookup mechanism**. ### Key Terminology Aligned with KTU 2024 ROS Lab Syllabus | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **Node** | An executable process that uses ROS to communicate with other nodes. | | **Master (roscore)** | The ROS naming and registration service that enables nodes to find each other. | | **Topic** | A named bus (string identifier, e.g., `/turtle1/cmd_vel`) over which messages are exchanged. | | **Message** | A strictly typed data structure (`.msg` file) sent over a topic (e.g., `String`, `Int32`, `Twist`). | | **Publisher** | A node object that *advertises* a topic and *publishes* messages onto it. | | **Subscriber** | A node object that *subscribes* to a topic and invokes a **callback** function upon message arrival. | | **Callback** | A user-defined function executed whenever a new message arrives on the subscribed topic. | | **Queue Size** | A bounded buffer (integer parameter) that holds outgoing/incoming messages in case of network delays. | | **Loop Rate (`rospy.Rate`)** | A frequency controller (in Hz) that governs the publishing cadence, e.g., `10 Hz` = 10 messages/second. | ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The Radio Station Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — Radio Broadcasting:** > Imagine ROS communication like a **radio broadcast system**: > - The **Radio Station** = Publisher node (it has content to send) > - The **Frequency Channel (e.g., 98.3 FM)** = Topic name (a unique string identifier) > - The **Transmitter Antenna** = `pub = rospy.Publisher(...)` (advertises the channel) > - The **Listener's Radio Set** = Subscriber node (it wants to receive content) > - The **Tuning Knob** = `sub = rospy.Subscriber(...)` (subscribes to that exact frequency) > - The **Song** = Message data payload > - The **Radio Authority that assigns frequencies** = ROS Master (`roscore`) > > Just as a radio station can broadcast on one frequency while **thousands** of listeners tune in independently, a single ROS Publisher can serve **many Subscribers** without knowing who they are. This is the essence of **decoupled, asynchronous** communication. ### Why Publisher-Subscriber is Critical in Robotics - **Modularity**: Sensor drivers, controllers, and planners run as independent nodes. - **Asynchrony**: A slow consumer does not block a fast producer. - **Many-to-Many**: Multiple algorithms can consume the same sensor stream simultaneously. - **Language Agnosticism**: Publishers and Subscribers can be written in C++ or Python independently. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — The ROS Communication Stack ## 2.1 The Three-Layer Communication Protocol ROS Publisher-Subscriber communication operates over a **layered protocol stack**: > [!NOTE] > **Layer 1 — XMLRPC (Discovery):** Used for initial node registration and topic lookup at port `11311`. The Master uses XMLRPC to maintain a registry of nodes and their published/subscribed topics. > > **Layer 2 — ROS Protocol (Handshake):** Once nodes discover each other, they negotiate a connection and establish a direct peer-to-peer link. > > **Layer 3 — TCP/UDP (Data Transfer):** Actual message data is streamed over **TCPROS** (default, reliable) or **UDPROS** (low-latency, lossy). In ROS 2, this is replaced by **DDS (Data Distribution Service)**. ## 2.2 Theoretical Flow — Publisher-Subscriber Lifecycle The complete operational lifecycle follows these structured steps: 1. **Initialization Step** — `rospy.init_node('node_name', anonymous=True)` - Registers the node with the ROS Master via XMLRPC. - `anonymous=True` appends a random integer to the name to prevent collisions when multiple instances run. 2. **Master Startup** — `roscore` - Starts the ROS Master, Parameter Server, and `rosout` logging node. - Listens on default port **11311**. 3. **Publisher Advertisement** — `pub = rospy.Publisher('topic_name', MsgType, queue_size=N)` - The Publisher registers its topic, message type, and URI with the Master. - The Master responds with the URIs of all current Subscribers of that topic. 4. **Subscriber Registration** — `sub = rospy.Subscriber('topic_name', MsgType, callback_function)` - The Subscriber registers its topic, message type, and callback URI with the Master. - The Master performs **topic matching** and informs the Subscriber of the Publisher's URI. 5. **Connection Establishment** — A direct **TCPROS** connection is set up between Publisher and Subscriber. 6. **Publishing Loop** — `pub.publish(data)` is called inside a `while not rospy.is_shutdown():` loop, throttled by `rate.sleep()`. 7. **Callback Invocation** — On the Subscriber side, ROS's internal message queue pops each received message and invokes the registered callback, passing the message as an argument. 8. **Graceful Shutdown** — On `Ctrl+C` or `rospy.is_shutdown()`, nodes unregister, close sockets, and exit. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Key Parameters & Units > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table summarizes the **mandatory parameter set** for any Publisher-Subscriber implementation, as expected in KTU lab viva and written exams. | Parameter | Mathematical / Symbolic Form | Typical Value | Unit / Notes | |---|:---:|:---:|---| | Publishing Frequency | $f_{\text{pub}}$ | **10** | **Hz (Hertz = messages/sec)** | | Loop Period | $T_{\text{loop}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{pub}}}$ | **0.1** | **seconds** | | Queue Size | $Q$ | **10** | dimensionless (integer) | | Message Latency | $L = \dfrac{Q}{f_{\text{pub}}}$ | **1.0** | **seconds (worst-case buffer drain time)** | | Throughput | $\Theta = f_{\text{pub}} \times \vert M \vert$ | varies | **bytes/second** | | Topic Name | $\tau$ | `/chatter` | ROS-valid string (no spaces, leading slash) | | Message Type | $\mu$ | `String` | Must be a registered `.msg` type | | Callback Period | $T_{\text{cb}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{sub}}}$ | varies | **seconds** | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight — Why $L = Q / f_{\text{pub}}$ matters:** > If a Subscriber crashes or is slow, the Publisher's internal queue fills up. Once $Q$ is exceeded, the **oldest** messages are dropped (FIFO drop policy). For real-time control loops (e.g., motor velocity commands), this latency budget is **critical** and is frequently examined in viva questions. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Robotics - **Sensor Fusion Pipelines:** Camera, LIDAR, and IMU nodes all publish raw data; multiple planner nodes subscribe. - **Teleoperation:** A keyboard node publishes `Twist` messages; the robot base controller subscribes. - **SLAM:** A laser scan node publishes `LaserScan`; the mapping and localization nodes each subscribe independently. - **Multi-Robot Systems:** Robots on the same ROS network can publish/subscribe across machines using `ROS_MASTER_URI` and `ROS_HOSTNAME`. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Implementation & Code Walkthrough > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU 2024 PCRUL508 lab curriculum requires **Python (`rospy`)** as the primary language for these experiments. The complete step-by-step procedure is given below. ## 3.1 Prerequisites & Workspace Setup Open a terminal and execute the following commands: ```bash # Step 1: Source the global ROS environment source /opt/ros//setup.bash # e.g., noetic, melodic, foxy # Step 2: Create and initialize a catkin workspace (if not already created) mkdir -p ~/catkin_ws/src cd ~/catkin_ws/src catkin_init_workspace # Step 3: Build the workspace cd ~/catkin_ws catkin_make # Step 4: Source the workspace overlay source devel/setup.bash ``` > [!NOTE] > Replace `` with the version installed in your KTU lab (commonly **ROS Noetic** on Ubuntu 20.04 or **ROS Melodic** on Ubuntu 18.04). Always append `source /opt/ros//setup.bash` and `source ~/catkin_ws/devel/setup.bash` to your `~/.bashrc` for persistence. ## 3.2 Creating a ROS Package A **package** is the fundamental unit of ROS code organization. Create one inside your `src` directory: ```bash cd ~/catkin_ws/src catkin_create_pkg beginner_tutorials std_msgs rospy roscpp ``` This command generates a package named `beginner_tutorials` with three dependencies: - **`std_msgs`** — Standard message types (`String`, `Int32`, `Float64`, `Twist`, etc.) - **`rospy`** — Python client library for ROS - **`roscpp`** — C++ client library (for cross-reference, optional for this lab) ## 3.3 The Publisher Node — `talker.py` Create the file inside the package's `scripts` folder: ```bash cd ~/catkin_ws/src/beginner_tutorials mkdir -p scripts cd scripts touch talker.py chmod +x talker.py # CRITICAL: make it executable ``` > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall:** If you forget `chmod +x`, ROS will throw the error `Couldn't find executable named talker.py`. This is a **frequently lost mark** in lab records — remember the executable bit. Now, write the Publisher code into `talker.py`: ```python #!/usr/bin/env python # ============================================================ # ROS PUBLISHER NODE — talker.py # PCRUL508 | Module Experiment 2 # Description: Publishes a counter-incremented String message # on the /chatter topic at 10 Hz. # ============================================================ import rospy from std_msgs.msg import String def talker(): # ---------- STEP 1: Initialize the node ---------- # anonymous=True ensures unique node name by appending # a random integer, allowing multiple instances to run. rospy.init_node('talker', anonymous=True) # ---------- STEP 2: Create the Publisher object ---------- # Topic: /chatter # Type: std_msgs/String # Queue: 10 (drops oldest if subscriber is slow) pub = rospy.Publisher('chatter', String, queue_size=10) # ---------- STEP 3: Set the publishing rate ---------- # 10 Hz => 10 messages per second => 0.1 s per message rate = rospy.Rate(10) # 10 Hz # ---------- STEP 4: Prevent publishing before connections are ready ---------- # sleep(1) gives subscribers time to register with the Master # and establish TCPROS connections, preventing message loss # at startup. rospy.sleep(1.0) # ---------- STEP 5: Main publishing loop ---------- # rospy.is_shutdown() returns True on Ctrl+C or node-kill. i = 0 while not rospy.is_shutdown(): hello_msg = "hello world %d" % i rospy.loginfo(hello_msg) # prints to terminal and writes to /rosout pub.publish(hello_msg) # broadcast on /chatter i += 1 rate.sleep() # blocks until next 0.1 s tick # ---------- STEP 6: Graceful exit ---------- # This block executes only if shutdown is triggered cleanly. rospy.loginfo("talker node terminated cleanly.") if __name__ == '__main__': try: talker() except rospy.ROSInterruptException: # Raised when sleep() or publish() is interrupted by Ctrl+C pass ``` ### Code Logic Breakdown — Each Line Explained - `#!/usr/bin/env python` — **Shebang line**; tells the OS to execute this file using the Python interpreter found in `$PATH`. - `import rospy` — Imports the **Python ROS client library** (mandatory for any `rospy` script). - `from std_msgs.msg import String` — Imports the `String` message class. The full path is `/msg/`. - `rospy.init_node('talker', anonymous=True)` — Registers the node with the Master. Without this, all subsequent API calls will throw `ROSInitException`. - `pub = rospy.Publisher('chatter', String, queue_size=10)` — Creates a Publisher object. The Master is notified of the topic name and message type. - `rate = rospy.Rate(10)` — Creates a Rate object at **10 Hz**. The function `rate.sleep()` accurately blocks the loop to maintain this frequency. - `pub.publish(hello_msg)` — The **core broadcasting call**. Sends the message to all connected Subscribers via TCPROS. - `rospy.loginfo(...)` — Equivalent to `print()`, but also publishes the log to the `/rosout` topic for `rqt_console` viewing. ## 3.4 The Subscriber Node — `listener.py` Create the file in the same `scripts` folder: ```bash cd ~/catkin_ws/src/beginner_tutorials/scripts touch listener.py chmod +x listener.py ``` Now write the Subscriber code: ```python #!/usr/bin/env python # ============================================================ # ROS SUBSCRIBER NODE — listener.py # PCRUL508 | Module Experiment 2 # Description: Subscribes to /chatter and prints each String # message as it arrives. # ============================================================ import rospy from std_msgs.msg import String # ---------- CALLBACK FUNCTION ---------- # This function is invoked automatically by ROS every time a # new message arrives on the /chatter topic. # The received message is passed as the 'data' argument. def callback(data): rospy.loginfo(rospy.get_caller_id() + " I heard %s", data.data) def listener(): # ---------- STEP 1: Initialize the node ---------- rospy.init_node('listener', anonymous=True) # ---------- STEP 2: Create the Subscriber object ---------- # Topic: /chatter # Type: std_msgs/String # Callback: callback function pointer (NOT a function call) rospy.Subscriber("chatter", String, callback) # ---------- STEP 3: Block until shutdown ---------- # rospy.spin() enters a loop that processes callbacks, # preventing the node from exiting. It is the # subscriber-side equivalent of a publishing while-loop. rospy.spin() if __name__ == '__main__': # No need to catch ROSInterruptException explicitly here # because spin() handles Ctrl+C gracefully. listener() ``` ### Code Logic Breakdown — Subscriber Side - `def callback(data):` — The **callback signature must accept exactly one argument**: the incoming message object. For `String`, the payload is accessed via `data.data`. - `rospy.Subscriber("chatter", String, callback)` — Notice we pass `callback` (the function object), **not** `callback()`. A common bug is calling it with parentheses, which invokes the function immediately with no argument and raises a `TypeError`. - `rospy.spin()` — Enters an **infinite event loop**. Internally, ROS checks the message queue, dispatches callbacks, and handles shutdown signals. Without `spin()`, the node would exit immediately since the main thread has nothing else to do. ## 3.5 Compilation & Execution Since these are **pure Python scripts** (not compiled C++), you do **not** need to re-run `catkin_make`. However, you must re-source the workspace to register the new package: ```bash # Terminal 1: Start the ROS Master roscore # Terminal 2: Run the Publisher cd ~/catkin_ws source devel/setup.bash rosrun beginner_tutorials talker.py # Terminal 3: Run the Subscriber cd ~/catkin_ws source devel/setup.bash rosrun beginner_tutorials listener.py ``` ## 3.6 Verification with Command-Line Tools The following commands allow you to **inspect the running system** without writing extra code: ```bash # 1. View the active computation graph (nodes, topics, connections) rqt_graph # 2. List all currently active topics rostopic list # 3. Display topic info (publishers, subscribers, message type) rostopic info /chatter # 4. Print messages published on /chatter in real time rostopic echo /chatter # 5. Publish a single message manually (useful for testing subscribers) rostopic pub -1 /chatter std_msgs/String "manual hello" # 6. Measure publishing frequency rostopic hz /chatter # 7. List all active nodes rosnode list # 8. Get information about a specific node rosnode info /talker ``` --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Publisher-Subscriber Architecture Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram) > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **end-to-end runtime architecture** of the Publisher-Subscriber system, including the discovery handshake and the data transfer path. All node IDs are alphanumeric and safe from reserved-keyword conflicts. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SETUP["Workspace & Package Setup"] A1[Open Terminal] A2[Source ROS Environment] A3[Create Catkin Workspace] A4[catkin_create_pkg beginner_tutorials] A5[Write talker.py and listener.py] A6[chmod +x both scripts] A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 end subgraph RUNTIME["Runtime Execution"] B1[Terminal 1: roscore] B2[Terminal 2: rosrun talker.py] B3[Terminal 3: rosrun listener.py] end subgraph DISCOVERY["XMLRPC Discovery via Master"] C1[Talker registers: topic=/chatter, type=String, URI=tcp:host:port] C2[Listener registers: subscription=/chatter, callback=callback] C3[Master performs topic matching] C4[Master returns Publisher URI to Listener] end subgraph DATAFLOW["TCPROS Data Transfer"] D1[pub.publish msg: hello world 0] D2[Queue Buffer queue_size=10] D3[TCPROS Socket Stream over TCP] D4[Listener callback invoked] D5[rospy.loginfo: I heard hello world 0] end A6 --> B1 B1 --> B2 B1 --> B3 B2 --> C1 B3 --> C2 C1 --> C3 C2 --> C3 C3 --> C4 C4 --> D3 C1 --> D1 D1 --> D2 D2 --> D3 D3 --> D4 D4 --> D5 style SETUP fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000000 style RUNTIME fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#B45F06,color:#000000 style DISCOVERY fill:#E2F0D9,stroke:#548235,color:#000000 style DATAFLOW fill:#FBE5D6,stroke:#C65911,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Discovery and Message Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant T as Talker Node participant M as ROS Master participant L as Listener Node participant TCP as TCPROS Link Note over M: Step 0: roscore starts Master on port 11311 T->>M: XMLRPC: registerPublisher(/chatter, String, URI) M-->>T: ACK L->>M: XMLRPC: registerSubscriber(/chatter, String, URI) M->>T: XMLRPC: newSubscriberURI M-->>L: XMLRPC: publisherURI Note over T,L: Step 1: TCPROS connection established loop Every 0.1 s (10 Hz) T->>TCP: serialize String msg TCP->>L: deserialize and queue L->>L: invoke callback(data) L->>L: loginfo("I heard hello world N") end Note over T,L: Ctrl+C triggers rospy shutdown T->>M: unregisterPublisher L->>M: unregisterSubscriber Note over M: Master cleans up registry ``` ## 4.3 Topic and Message Type Relationship (Information Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR MS["std_msgs/String
Fields: data string"] TPC["Topic: /chatter
Type: std_msgs/String"] PUB["Talker Node
Publisher: chatter"] SUB["Listener Node
Subscriber: chatter"] PUB -- "advertises" --> TPC TPC -- "carries instances of" --> MS SUB -- "subscribes to" --> TPC MS -- "received as callback arg" --> SUB style MS fill:#D9E8F5,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000000 style TPC fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#BF9000,color:#000000 style PUB fill:#E2EFDA,stroke:#548235,color:#000000 style SUB fill:#FBE5D6,stroke:#C65911,color:#000000 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Differentiate between a Publisher and a Subscriber node in ROS. List the parameters required to create each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Publisher | Subscriber | |---|---|---| | **Purpose** | Broadcasts messages on a topic | Receives messages from a topic | | **ROS API** | `rospy.Publisher(name, type, queue_size)` | `rospy.Subscriber(name, type, callback)` | | **Mandatory Parameters** | Topic name, Message type, Queue size | Topic name, Message type, Callback function | | **Loop Pattern** | `while not rospy.is_shutdown():` + `rate.sleep()` | `rospy.spin()` (event-driven) | | **Data Flow** | Outgoing | Incoming | > **[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 mark Publisher definition + 1 mark Subscriber definition + 1 mark parameters]** ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **What is the role of `roscore` in a Publisher-Subscriber system? What port does it use?** **Model Answer:** - `roscore` starts the **ROS Master**, the **Parameter Server**, and the **`rosout` logging node**. - The Master maintains a **central registry** of all nodes, topics, services, and their URIs. - It enables **indirect discovery**: when a Publisher advertises a topic, the Master informs all matching Subscribers of the Publisher's address, allowing them to establish a **direct peer-to-peer TCPROS connection**. - Default port: **`11311`** (XMLRPC). - Without `roscore`, nodes cannot find each other and `rospy.init_node()` will time out. > **[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 mark Master role + 1 mark Parameter/rosout mention + 1 mark port number]** --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations**, Part B questions carry **14 marks** with sub-parts typically split as **7 + 7**. Students must answer **one full question** per module. The following provides two complete alternative question choices. ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Explain the **Publisher-Subscriber communication model** in ROS with a suitable block diagram. Discuss the role of the ROS Master, Topic, and Message in this architecture. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The Publisher-Subscriber model in ROS is a **many-to-many, asynchronous, loosely-coupled** communication paradigm. The components are: 1. **ROS Master (`roscore`)**: Acts as a **name server** and **matchmaker**. It does not relay messages; it only enables nodes to discover each other. It runs on port `11311` and uses **XMLRPC** for all registration and lookup queries. 2. **Topic**: A **named string channel** (e.g., `/cmd_vel`, `/chatter`, `/laser_scan`) over which messages flow. Topics are **anonymous** — the Publisher and Subscriber do not need to know each other's identities. 3. **Message**: A **strictly-typed data structure** defined in a `.msg` file. The type system ensures that a Subscriber on `/chatter` expecting `std_msgs/String` will reject any other type. Standard types come from `std_msgs` (e.g., `String`, `Int32`, `Float64`, `Bool`). 4. **Publisher**: Calls `rospy.Publisher(topic_name, MsgType, queue_size)` and broadcasts via `pub.publish(data)`. 5. **Subscriber**: Calls `rospy.Subscriber(topic_name, MsgType, callback)` and reacts via the callback. **Operational Flow:** - Publisher → registers with Master → Master returns list of Subscribers - Subscriber → registers with Master → Master returns Publisher URI - Direct **TCPROS** connection is established - Messages flow Publisher → TCP socket → Subscriber queue → callback > **[7 Marks Breakdown: 2 marks definitions + 2 marks Master role + 2 marks flow + 1 mark diagram]** **(b)** Write the complete Python code for a **Publisher node** that publishes a `std_msgs/String` message containing the current system time and a counter on the topic `/time_talk`, at a rate of **5 Hz**. Explain each section of the code. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** ```python #!/usr/bin/env python import rospy from std_msgs.msg import String import time def time_talker(): # Node initialization rospy.init_node('time_talker', anonymous=True) # Publisher creation: topic=/time_talk, type=String, queue=10 pub = rospy.Publisher('time_talk', String, queue_size=10) # Rate: 5 Hz => T = 1/5 = 0.2 s rate = rospy.Rate(5) rospy.sleep(1.0) # Allow connection establishment counter = 0 while not rospy.is_shutdown(): # Compose message: current time + counter current_time = time.strftime("%H:%M:%S") msg_str = "Time: %s | Count: %d" % (current_time, counter) rospy.loginfo(msg_str) pub.publish(msg_str) counter += 1 rate.sleep() if __name__ == '__main__': try: time_talker() except rospy.ROSInterruptException: pass ``` **Code Section Explanations:** | Section | Purpose | |---|---| | `#!/usr/bin/env python` | Shebang for Python interpreter | | `rospy.init_node(...)` | Registers the node with the Master | | `rospy.Publisher(...)` | Advertises `/time_talk` of type `String` with queue 10 | | `rospy.Rate(5)` | Throttles loop to 5 Hz (period = 0.2 s) | | `rospy.sleep(1.0)` | Gives time for Subscriber connections to be established | | `pub.publish(msg_str)` | Broadcasts the message over TCPROS | | `rate.sleep()` | Blocks for the remaining time in the 0.2 s window | | `try/except ROSInterruptException` | Handles Ctrl+C gracefully | > **[7 Marks Breakdown: 4 marks code + 2 marks section explanation + 1 mark rate calculation]** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Compare the **Publisher-Subscriber** model with the **Service-Client** model in ROS. When is each appropriate? **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** | Feature | Publisher-Subscriber | Service-Client | |---|---|---| | **Communication Type** | Asynchronous, one-to-many | Synchronous, one-to-one | | **Data Flow** | Continuous stream | Single request-response | | **Coupling** | Loose (no timing dependency) | Tight (client blocks until response) | | **Blocking** | Non-blocking publisher; subscriber uses callbacks | Client blocks waiting for response | | **Use Case** | Sensor streams, robot state, velocity commands | One-shot queries (e.g., "what is the map?", "spawn a turtle") | | **ROS Class** | `rospy.Publisher` / `rospy.Subscriber` | `rospy.ServiceProxy` / `rospy.Service` | | **Master Role** | Discovery only | Discovery + connection | | **Message File** | `.msg` | `.srv` (with `request` and `response` sections) | **When to use Publisher-Subscriber:** - Continuous data streams (camera, LIDAR, IMU). - Broadcasting commands to multiple listeners. - Real-time control loops where blocking is unacceptable. **When to use Service-Client:** - Discrete, infrequent requests. - Computation that has a defined end (e.g., "compute inverse kinematics"). - When the client *must* know the outcome before proceeding. > **[7 Marks Breakdown: 3 marks table + 2 marks Pub-Sub use cases + 2 marks Service-Client use cases]** **(b)** Write the complete Python code for a **Subscriber node** that listens to `/time_talk` (from Question A) and stores every received message in a Python list. After 10 messages are received, the node should log the entire list and shut down gracefully. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** ```python #!/usr/bin/env python import rospy from std_msgs.msg import String # Global list to accumulate messages received_messages = [] message_limit = 10 def callback(data): """Invoked for every incoming /time_talk message.""" global received_messages rospy.loginfo("Received: %s", data.data) received_messages.append(data.data) # Check if we have collected enough messages if len(received_messages) >= message_limit: rospy.loginfo("Collected %d messages. Final list:", len(received_messages)) for idx, msg in enumerate(received_messages): rospy.loginfo(" [%d] %s", idx, msg) # Trigger graceful shutdown rospy.signal_shutdown("Collected required number of messages") def timed_listener(): rospy.init_node('timed_listener', anonymous=True) rospy.Subscriber('time_talk', String, callback) rospy.loginfo("timed_listener started. Waiting for messages...") # spin() blocks; the callback calls signal_shutdown() to exit rospy.spin() rospy.loginfo("timed_listener exiting cleanly.") if __name__ == '__main__': timed_listener() ``` **Key Concepts Demonstrated:** | Concept | Explanation | |---|---| | **Global state in callback** | The list `received_messages` is declared global to persist across callback invocations. | | `rospy.signal_shutdown(reason)` | Cleanly terminates the node, causing `rospy.spin()` to return. | | `rospy.spin()` | Processes the message queue and dispatches callbacks. | | `data.data` | Double `.data` access — outer is the message object, inner is the `String` field. | > **[7 Marks Breakdown: 4 marks code + 2 marks callback + global logic + 1 mark shutdown explanation]** --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Points in PCRUL508 Lab Exam:** > 1. **Forgetting `chmod +x`** on Python scripts → "executable not found" error during `rosrun`. **−1 mark**. > 2. **Forgetting `source devel/setup.bash`** after creating a new package → `rosrun` cannot find the package. **−1 mark**. > 3. **Passing `callback()` instead of `callback`** to `rospy.Subscriber` → TypeError at runtime. **−1 mark**. > 4. **Not running `roscore`** before starting the nodes → `init_node` times out after 10 seconds. **−1 mark**. > 5. **Wrong message type** (e.g., publishing `Int32` on a `String` topic) → Type-mismatch error. **−1 mark**. > 6. **Missing `try/except ROSInterruptException`** in publisher → script may not exit cleanly. **−1 mark**. > 7. **Confusing topic name with leading slash** — `chatter` and `/chatter` are the **same topic** in ROS (the system auto-resolves), but explicit `/` is best practice. **−0.5 mark** for inconsistency. > 8. **Not explaining the role of `rospy.Rate()` and `rate.sleep()`** in written answers → Examiner often allocates **1–2 marks** specifically for this. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module Experiment 2:** - 🔹 **Architecture:** Publisher-Subscriber is **asynchronous, decoupled, and many-to-many**. - 🔹 **Three Core Components:** **Master** (discovery), **Topic** (channel), **Message** (typed data). - 🔹 **Master Port:** Default **11311** (XMLRPC). - 🔹 **Protocol Stack:** **XMLRPC** (discovery) → **TCPROS** (default data transfer, reliable, TCP). - 🔹 **Publisher API:** `rospy.Publisher(topic_name, MsgType, queue_size=N)`. - 🔹 **Subscriber API:** `rospy.Subscriber(topic_name, MsgType, callback_function)`. - 🔹 **Loop Pattern — Publisher:** `while not rospy.is_shutdown():` + `rate.sleep()`. - 🔹 **Loop Pattern — Subscriber:** `rospy.spin()` (event-driven, non-blocking). - 🔹 **Rate Formula:** $T_{\text{loop}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{pub}}}$. For $f = 10$ Hz, $T = 0.1$ s. - 🔹 **Queue Latency:** $L = Q / f_{\text{pub}}$. For $Q=10, f=10$, $L=1$ s. - 🔹 **`anonymous=True`** in `init_node` prevents name collisions when multiple instances run. - 🔹 **Executable bit:** Always `chmod +x script.py` before `rosrun`. - 🔹 **Source the workspace:** `source ~/catkin_ws/devel/setup.bash` after creating a new package. - 🔹 **Inspection Tools:** `rqt_graph`, `rostopic list`, `rostopic echo`, `rostopic hz`, `rostopic info`, `rosnode list`. - 🔹 **Callback signature:** Must accept exactly **one argument** (the message object). - 🔹 **Accessing String data:** `data.data` (double access). - 🔹 **Graceful shutdown:** Use `try/except rospy.ROSInterruptException` in publisher; `rospy.signal_shutdown()` in callback-driven subscribers. - 🔹 **Mandatory import:** `import rospy` and `from std_msgs.msg import String` (or relevant message type). - 🔹 **`rospy.loginfo` vs `print`:** `loginfo` writes to `/rosout` topic and is visible in `rqt_console`; `print` only writes to terminal. - 🔹 **Standard Message Package:** `std_msgs` contains primitives (`String`, `Int32`, `Float64`, `Bool`, `Header`, etc.). - 🔹 **No re-compilation needed for Python** — only `catkin_make` for C++ nodes; for Python, just ensure the script is executable and the package is sourced.

topics.title: Simple Service and Client
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL508 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS) LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Simple Service and Client

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

In the **Robot Operating System (ROS)** communication architecture, a **Service** is a strict **Request/Reply (synchronous)** communication paradigm where a **Service Server** node provides a well-defined function and a **Service Client** node queries that function by sending a structured request and blocking until a response is received. The contract between both nodes is defined in a `.srv` file residing inside the `srv/` directory of a ROS package.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Services are used for **strongly-typed, deterministic, one-shot** robot operations such as triggering a calibration routine, computing inverse kinematics, saving a map, or toggling a gripper. They are *not* used for continuous high-frequency data streams — that is the role of **Topics** (Publisher/Subscriber).

| Communication Paradigm | Pattern | Use Case |
|------------------------|---------|----------|
| **Topic (Pub/Sub)** | Asynchronous, many-to-many | Sensor streams, odometry, velocity commands |
| **Service (Req/Rep)** | Synchronous, one-to-one | Trigger an action, query state, compute a single result |
| **Action (Goal/Result/Feedback)** | Asynchronous, preemptable | Long-running tasks with feedback (navigation, manipulation) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a **restaurant counter** (the **Service Server**) and place an order. The waiter takes your order slip (**Request**), the kitchen prepares the dish, and the waiter hands you the plate (**Response**). You stand at the counter the entire time — you do **not** leave to do other things. This is exactly how a ROS Service works:

- The **Client** sends one request and **waits (blocks)**.
- The **Server** executes its callback exactly **once** per request.
- Once the response arrives, the client resumes execution.

In contrast, a **Topic** is like a **radio broadcast** — the speaker transmits continuously, and anyone listening hears it without acknowledgement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box**
> - **Service Server**: Node that advertises a service and contains a callback to handle incoming requests.
> - **Service Client**: Node that calls the service, sends a request, and processes the future response.
> - **Service Description (.srv file)**: Contains the request message followed by a `---` separator, followed by the response message.
> - **Blocking Call**: `client.wait_for_service()` and `client.call(request)` are *synchronous* — the client node halts until completion.

## 1.3 Standard ROS Metrics & Terminology

- **Default ROS 1 build system**: `catkin` (ROS Noetic / Melodic used in KTU labs).
- **Standard workspace name**: `catkin_ws/`.
- **Build folder**: `catkin_ws/build/`.
- **Develop folder**: `catkin_ws/devel/`.
- **Source setup script**: `source devel/setup.bash`.
- **Standard naming convention**: Service name is a *string identifier* of the form `/node_name/service_name` (e.g., `/add_two_ints`).
- **Service type**: Identified by `package_name/srv_file_name` (e.g., `beginner_tutorials/AddTwoInts`).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Service Communication Model — Step by Step

The lifecycle of a Service call consists of the following ordered steps:

1. **Service Definition (`.srv` file):** A plain text file with two parts separated by `---` — the **request** fields on top, and the **response** fields on bottom.
2. **Code Generation:** During `catkin_make`, the build system (using `message_generation` + `genmsg`/`gensrv`) generates Python/C++ source files from the `.srv` description.
3. **Service Server Startup:** The server node calls `advertiseService()` (C++) or `rospy.Service()` (Python), registering itself with the ROS Master under a unique service name.
4. **Service Client Lookup:** The client calls `wait_for_service()` to confirm the server is online, then `call()` to send a serialized request.
5. **Master Routing:** The ROS Master resolves the URI of the service server and returns connection details to the client (this happens once, during lookup).
6. **Direct TCP Handshake:** Subsequent calls bypass the Master — they occur **directly between client and server** over TCPROS.
7. **Callback Execution on Server:** The server's callback receives the request, computes a result, fills the response message, and returns it.
8. **Response Delivery:** The client receives the response and unblocks. Returned object contains the response fields and a `success` boolean.

## 2.2 The `.srv` File Grammar

A service description file follows strict grammar:

```
# Request fields (variables sent FROM client TO server)
int64 a
int64 b
---
# Response fields (variables sent FROM server TO client)
int64 sum
```

> [!NOTE]
> The `---` separator is **mandatory** and must be on its **own line**. The `#` symbol is used for **comments**, identical to Python.

## 2.3 Required `package.xml` Dependencies

For a ROS package to support custom services, the following dependencies are mandatory:

| Dependency | Purpose | Tag in package.xml |
|------------|---------|--------------------|
| `message_generation` | Generates language bindings (C++/Python) from `.srv` | `<build_depend>` |
| `message_runtime` | Provides runtime support for message/service types | `<exec_depend>` |
| `roscpp` / `rospy` | C++ / Python client libraries | `<build_depend>` + `<exec_depend>` |
| `std_msgs` | Standard primitive message types (int, float, string) | `<build_depend>` + `<exec_depend>` |

## 2.4 Required `CMakeLists.txt` Configuration

The build system must be explicitly informed about services:

| CMake Variable | Required Value | Purpose |
|----------------|----------------|---------|
| `find_package(... message_generation)` | Mandatory | Locate the message generator |
| `add_service_files(FILES AddTwoInts.srv)` | List all .srv files | Registers the .srv for code generation |
| `generate_messages(DEPENDENCIES std_msgs)` | Mandatory | Triggers code generation |
| `catkin_package(... message_runtime)` | Mandatory | Exports runtime dependency |
| Executable registration | `add_executable()` + `target_link_libraries()` | Compiles the server/client nodes |

## 2.5 KTU Reference Table — ROS Service Lifecycle Commands

| Command | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| `roscd <package_name>/srv` | Navigate to the `srv/` directory |
| `rossrv show beginner_tutorials/AddTwoInts` | Display service description |
| `rossrv list` | List all known service types |
| `rosservice list` | List all active services in the ROS graph |
| `rosservice call /add_two_ints "a: 5" "b: 3"` | Manually invoke a service from terminal |
| `rossrv md5 beginner_tutorials/AddTwoInts` | Verify MD5 checksum for compatibility |
| `rosrun <pkg> <node_name>` | Launch server or client node |
| `rosnode list` / `rosnode info <node>` | Inspect running nodes |

## 2.6 Why Services Over Topics — The Engineering Rationale

- **Determinism:** Guaranteed request-response pairing — ideal for state queries and one-shot triggers.
- **Strong Typing:** Compile-time / runtime validation of message structure eliminates silent corruption.
- **Error Handling:** Built-in `success` flag allows the server to communicate failure.
- **Resource Bounded:** No continuous bandwidth consumption (unlike topics broadcasting at 100 Hz).
- **Composability:** Multiple clients can invoke the same server concurrently, enabling modular robot software architecture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** If the operation completes in **< 1 second** and you need a **single, definite answer**, use a **Service**. If it runs for minutes/hours or needs progress feedback, use an **Action**. If it's continuous data, use a **Topic**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Implementation Walkthrough (Analytical Build Matrix)

## 3.1 Step 1 — Workspace & Package Creation

The standard procedure to bootstrap a ROS 1 project (assumes ROS Noetic / Melodic per KTU lab):

1. Source the ROS environment:
   ```bash
   source /opt/ros/noetic/setup.bash
   ```
2. Create a Catkin workspace:
   ```bash
   mkdir -p ~/catkin_ws/src
   cd ~/catkin_ws/
   catkin_make
   source devel/setup.bash
   ```
3. Create the package with `roscpp`, `rospy`, and `std_msgs` dependencies:
   ```bash
   cd ~/catkin_ws/src
   catkin_create_pkg beginner_tutorials rospy roscpp std_msgs
   ```

**Reasoning:** The `catkin_create_pkg` command auto-generates a `package.xml`, `CMakeLists.txt`, and a `src/` directory inside the new package. Declaring `rospy`, `roscpp`, and `std_msgs` at creation time adds them as `<depend>` tags in `package.xml` automatically, saving manual editing.

## 3.2 Step 2 — Define the Service (`.srv` file)

Create the `srv/` subfolder inside the package and add the service description:

```bash
cd ~/catkin_ws/src/beginner_tutorials
mkdir srv
```

Create `srv/AddTwoInts.srv` with the following content:

```
int64 a
int64 b
---
int64 sum
```

**Reasoning:**
- `a` and `b` are **request** fields (the client fills these).
- `sum` is the **response** field (the server fills this).
- The `---` line strictly partitions request from response.

## 3.3 Step 3 — Update `package.xml`

Add the message generation and runtime dependencies:

```xml
<build_depend>message_generation</build_depend>
<exec_depend>message_runtime</exec_depend>
```

**Reasoning:** `message_generation` triggers the code generator during the `catkin_make` process. `message_runtime` is required at execution time so the generated Python/C++ classes can be imported.

## 3.4 Step 4 — Update `CMakeLists.txt`

Locate the `find_package` call and add `message_generation`:

```cmake
find_package(catkin REQUIRED COMPONENTS
  roscpp
  rospy
  std_msgs
  message_generation
)
```

Declare the service file:

```cmake
add_service_files(
  FILES
  AddTwoInts.srv
)
```

Trigger message generation:

```cmake
generate_messages(
  DEPENDENCIES
  std_msgs
)
```

Export runtime dependency:

```cmake
catkin_package(
  ...
  CATKIN_DEPENDS message_runtime rospy roscpp std_msgs
  ...
)
```

Finally, register the Python nodes (assuming `scripts/` folder with executable permission):

```cmake
catkin_install_python(PROGRAMS scripts/add_two_ints_server.py scripts/add_two_ints_client.py
  DESTINATION ${CATKIN_PACKAGE_BIN_DESTINATION}
)
```

**Reasoning:** Each CMake directive is *sequential and order-sensitive*. `add_service_files` must appear *before* `generate_messages` for catkin to process the `.srv` file correctly. `catkin_install_python` ensures the scripts are copied to `devel/lib/<pkg>/` for `rosrun` to find them.

## 3.5 Step 5 — Write the Service Server (Python)

Create `scripts/add_two_ints_server.py`:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3

from beginner_tutorials.srv import AddTwoInts, AddTwoIntsResponse
import rospy

def handle_add_two_ints(req):
    rospy.loginfo(f"Returning {req.a} + {req.b} = {req.a + req.b}")
    return AddTwoIntsResponse(req.a + req.b)

def add_two_ints_server():
    rospy.init_node('add_two_ints_server')
    s = rospy.Service('add_two_ints', AddTwoInts, handle_add_two_ints)
    rospy.loginfo("Ready to add two ints.")
    rospy.spin()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    add_two_ints_server()
```

**Reasoning (per line of importance):**
- `rospy.init_node('add_two_ints_server')` registers this process with the ROS Master under the node name.
- `rospy.Service('add_two_ints', AddTwoInts, handle_add_two_ints)` advertises a service named `add_two_ints` with type `AddTwoInts` and callback `handle_add_two_ints`.
- `rospy.spin()` enters a blocking loop that keeps the node alive and ready to handle incoming service requests.
- The callback `handle_add_two_ints` must accept the request object and **return** a response object.

## 3.6 Step 6 — Write the Service Client (Python)

Create `scripts/add_two_ints_client.py`:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3

import sys
import rospy
from beginner_tutorials.srv import AddTwoInts, AddTwoInts

def add_two_ints_client(x, y):
    rospy.wait_for_service('add_two_ints')
    try:
        add_two_ints = rospy.ServiceProxy('add_two_ints', AddTwoInts)
        resp1 = add_two_ints(x, y)
        return resp1.sum
    except rospy.ServiceException as e:
        rospy.logwarn(f"Service call failed: {e}")

def usage():
    return f"{sys.argv[0]} [x] [y]"

if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) == 3:
        x = int(sys.argv[1])
        y = int(sys.argv[2])
    else:
        print(usage())
        sys.exit(1)
    print("Requesting %s+%s" % (x, y))
    rospy.init_node('add_two_ints_client')
    s = add_two_ints_client(x, y)
    print(f"{x} + {y} = {s}")
```

**Reasoning (per line of importance):**
- `rospy.wait_for_service('add_two_ints')` is a **blocking** call that polls the Master until the server becomes available — this prevents race conditions at startup.
- `rospy.ServiceProxy('add_two_ints', AddTwoInts)` creates a callable proxy object.
- Calling the proxy `add_two_ints(x, y)` sends a request and **blocks** until the response arrives.
- The `except rospy.ServiceException` block handles transport-level failures (timeout, malformed response, etc.).

## 3.7 Step 7 — Build, Source, and Run

```bash
cd ~/catkin_ws
catkin_make
source devel/setup.bash
chmod +x ~/catkin_ws/src/beginner_tutorials/scripts/*.py
```

In **Terminal 1** (run the server):
```bash
rosrun beginner_tutorials add_two_ints_server.py
```

In **Terminal 2** (run the client):
```bash
rosrun beginner_tutorials add_two_ints_client.py 4 5
```

**Expected Output:**
- Server Terminal: `Returning 4 + 5 = 9`
- Client Terminal: `9`

## 3.8 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Step | File Modified | Build Trigger | Verification |
|------|---------------|---------------|--------------|
| Create workspace | `~/catkin_ws/` | `catkin_make` | `devel/setup.bash` exists |
| Create package | `src/beginner_tutorials/` | `catkin_create_pkg` | `package.xml` present |
| Define service | `srv/AddTwoInts.srv` | Re-run `catkin_make` | `rosmsg/rossrv` shows type |
| Write server | `scripts/add_two_ints_server.py` | `catkin_make` (no rebuild) | `rosnode list` shows it |
| Write client | `scripts/add_two_ints_client.py` | No rebuild | Terminal prints sum |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Service Communication Architecture (Request/Reply Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Client Node] -->|wait_for_service| M[ROS Master]
    M -->|URI lookup| A
    A -->|Service Call Request| S[Service Server Node]
    S -->|Execute Callback| C[Callback Function]
    C -->|Compute Result| R[Response Message]
    R -->|Return via TCPROS| A
    S -.->|Advertise Service| M
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dashed line from **Service Server → ROS Master** shows the one-time *advertisement* that occurs at server startup. Subsequent calls do **not** touch the Master; they go directly between the two nodes over TCPROS.

## 4.2 File Structure Inside the ROS Package

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[beginner_tutorials Package] --> A[package.xml]
    P --> B[CMakeLists.txt]
    P --> C[scripts/]
    P --> D[srv/]
    P --> E[src/]
    C --> C1[add_two_ints_server.py]
    C --> C2[add_two_ints_client.py]
    D --> D1[AddTwoInts.srv]
    E --> E1[add_two_ints_server.cpp]
    E --> E2[add_two_ints_client.cpp]
```

## 4.3 Service Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Uninitialized
    Uninitialized --> Advertising: init_node
    Advertising --> Ready: rospy.Service advertised
    Ready --> Processing: Client call received
    Processing --> Ready: Callback returns response
    Ready --> Shutdown: rospy shutdown
    Shutdown --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Build & Execution Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Step 1: catkin_create_pkg] --> Q2[Step 2: Define .srv]
    Q2 --> Q3[Step 3: Edit package.xml]
    Q3 --> Q4[Step 4: Edit CMakeLists.txt]
    Q4 --> Q5[Step 5: catkin_make]
    Q5 --> Q6[Step 6: source devel/setup.bash]
    Q6 --> Q7[Step 7: rosrun server]
    Q7 --> Q8[Step 8: rosrun client with args]
    Q8 --> Q9{Success?}
    Q9 -->|Yes| Q10[Output displayed]
    Q9 -->|No| Q11[Check rospy.logwarn output]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Lab Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Differentiate between ROS Topics and ROS Services. State two situations where you would prefer a Service over a Topic.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Topic | Service |
|--------|-------|---------|
| Communication Pattern | Asynchronous Publish/Subscribe | Synchronous Request/Response |
| Direction | Many-to-many | One-to-one |
| Call Nature | Non-blocking (fire and forget) | Blocking (waits for response) |
| Data Volume | Continuous streams | One-shot operations |

**Preferred Scenarios for Services (any two, 1 mark each):**
1. **State query** — e.g., asking a node to return the current battery voltage (a single value needed once).
2. **Trigger a discrete action** — e.g., commanding a gripper to open or close (a one-shot command with success/failure).
3. **Single computation** — e.g., computing inverse kinematics for a specific pose.
4. **Map saving/loading** in SLAM — a discrete I/O operation.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Lab Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of the `.srv` file in ROS. What does the `---` separator signify?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `.srv` file is the **service description** that defines the contract between the client and server. It specifies the **type and structure of both the request and the response messages**.

Structure of `AddTwoInts.srv`:
```
int64 a       ← request
int64 b       ← request
---           ← MANDATORY separator
int64 sum     ← response
```

The `---` separator on its **own line** strictly partitions the **request fields** (above) from the **response fields** (below). It is mandatory — without it, the message generator (`message_generation`) will fail to parse the file. During `catkin_make`, this file is processed to generate language-specific classes (Python/C++) that both the client and server import for strongly-typed communication.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Service/Client communication architecture** in ROS. List the steps performed by the **Service Server** and **Service Client** during a typical request-response cycle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete **ROS service description file (`WordCount.srv`)** that accepts a string input and returns an integer count of words. Develop the **Python Service Server** and **Python Service Client** programs for this service. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a)

**Block Diagram (Description — 3 marks):**

The Service architecture consists of three primary entities:

1. **ROS Master** — a name service that matches service names to URIs.
2. **Service Server Node** — registers itself and waits for requests.
3. **Service Client Node** — looks up the service URI and sends requests.

**Steps Performed (4 marks):**

| Step | Service Server Side | Service Client Side |
|------|---------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Initialize node via `rospy.init_node()` | Initialize node via `rospy.init_node()` |
| 2 | Advertise service via `rospy.Service()` | Wait for service via `rospy.wait_for_service()` |
| 3 | Block on `rospy.spin()` | Create proxy via `rospy.ServiceProxy()` |
| 4 | Receive request → invoke callback | Send request via `proxy(args)` |
| 5 | Compute response, return from callback | Block until response received |
| 6 | Ready for next request | Process response, unblock |

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b)

**`srv/WordCount.srv` (2 marks):**

```
string sentence
---
int32 word_count
```

**Service Server `scripts/word_count_server.py` (2.5 marks):**

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
from beginner_tutorials.srv import WordCount, WordCountResponse
import rospy

def count_words(req):
    words = req.sentence.split()
    return WordCountResponse(len(words))

def word_count_server():
    rospy.init_node('word_count_server')
    s = rospy.Service('word_count', WordCount, count_words)
    rospy.loginfo("Word count service ready.")
    rospy.spin()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    word_count_server()
```

**Service Client `scripts/word_count_client.py` (2.5 marks):**

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys, rospy
from beginner_tutorials.srv import WordCount

def word_count_client(sentence):
    rospy.wait_for_service('word_count')
    try:
        proxy = rospy.ServiceProxy('word_count', WordCount)
        resp = proxy(sentence)
        return resp.word_count
    except rospy.ServiceException as e:
        rospy.logwarn(f"Service failed: {e}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    rospy.init_node('word_count_client')
    sentence = sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else "Hello ROS world"
    count = word_count_client(sentence)
    print(f"Word count = {count}")
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **purpose of the `CMakeLists.txt` directives** `add_service_files()`, `generate_messages()`, and `catkin_install_python()` in the context of building a ROS service. Why is the **order** of these directives important? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the role of the **ROS Master** in a Service call? Differentiate between `rospy.wait_for_service()` and `rospy.ServiceProxy()`. What happens if a client calls a service before the server is launched? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a)

| Directive | Purpose | Order |
|-----------|---------|-------|
| `add_service_files(FILES AddTwoInts.srv)` | Registers `.srv` files with the build system so they are available for code generation | Must come **before** `generate_messages()` |
| `generate_messages(DEPENDENCIES std_msgs)` | Triggers `message_generation` to convert `.srv` → Python/C++ source files | Must come **after** `add_service_files()` |
| `catkin_install_python(PROGRAMS ...)` | Copies Python scripts (with executable permission) to `devel/lib/<pkg>/` so `rosrun` can find them | Can be placed **anywhere** in the file but conventionally near the end |

**Order Importance (2 marks):**
- `add_service_files()` must precede `generate_messages()` because the latter iterates over files registered by the former. Reversed order → `catkin_make` error: *None of the required .srv files were found*.
- `catkin_install_python()` must occur **after** `catkin_package()` so the install destination variable `${CATKIN_PACKAGE_BIN_DESTINATION}` is defined.

**Required `package.xml` dependencies (1 mark):** `message_generation` (build), `message_runtime` (exec).

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b)

**Role of ROS Master (2 marks):**
- Performs **name registration and lookup** for services.
- At server startup: stores the service name → node URI mapping.
- At client request: resolves the service name to the server's URI and returns it.
- After this initial lookup, the client and server communicate **directly** via TCPROS — the Master is bypassed for ongoing traffic.

**Difference between `wait_for_service()` and `ServiceProxy()` (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `wait_for_service()` | `ServiceProxy()` |
|--------|----------------------|-------------------|
| Purpose | Block until server is online | Create a callable handle to the service |
| Blocking? | Yes — polls the Master at 1 Hz | No — instant object creation |
| Returns | `None` on success | A callable proxy object |
| When to use | Once, at startup, before any call | Once, then call multiple times |

**What happens if client calls before server is launched (2 marks):**
- The client **blocks indefinitely** on `wait_for_service()` (with a default timeout of `ROS_DEFAULT_WAIT_FOR_SERVICE_TIMEOUT = 0`).
- If `wait_for_service()` is bypassed and the proxy is called directly, the call **fails** with a `rospy.ServiceException`, and the `except` block executes (e.g., logs a warning).
- No automatic retry — the developer must implement a retry loop or restart the client after the server is online.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> 1. **Missing `chmod +x` on Python scripts** — causes `rosrun` to fail with *"Permission denied"*. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** if this is not mentioned in the procedure.
> 2. **Forgetting to re-`source devel/setup.bash`** after `catkin_make` — the new executable path is not registered. Deduct **1 mark** if not in the answer.
> 3. **Placing the response fields above the `---` separator** — this is a fatal `.srv` grammar error. Worth **full 7 marks** of sub-part (b) if the service description is incorrect.
> 4. **Using `rospy.spin()` inside the client** — incorrect; only the server should call `rospy.spin()`. Client should simply call the proxy and exit (or enter its own loop).
> 5. **Not handling `rospy.ServiceException`** in the client — examiners look for `try/except` blocks and deduct up to **2 marks** for unhandled exceptions.
> 6. **Confusing Topic and Service terminology in viva** — stating that services use a "publisher" or "subscriber" loses **1 mark** immediately.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **ROS Service** is a **synchronous, strongly-typed Request/Response** mechanism for one-shot robot operations.
- The **`.srv` file** defines the contract — request fields above `---`, response fields below.
- The **Server** advertises via `rospy.Service()` and processes requests in a callback; the **Client** calls via `rospy.ServiceProxy()` after `rospy.wait_for_service()`.
- The **ROS Master** performs only *name resolution*; subsequent calls are direct TCPROS connections.
- **Build requirements**: `add_service_files()` + `generate_messages()` + `catkin_install_python()` + `message_generation` (build_depend) + `message_runtime` (exec_depend).
- **Use Services** for: state queries, discrete triggers, single computations.
- **Avoid Services** for: continuous sensor data (use Topics) or long-running tasks with feedback (use Actions).
- **Always** `chmod +x` your Python scripts, **always** re-`source devel/setup.bash` after building, and **always** wrap client calls in `try/except rospy.ServiceException`.
- **Verification commands**: `rossrv show <type>`, `rosservice list`, `rosservice call <name> "field: value"`, `rosnode list`.
- **Common MD5 mismatch errors** indicate incompatible `.srv` files between client and server — rebuild from a clean `catkin_make` to resolve.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recording and Playing Back Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL508 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS) LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Recording and Playing Back Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> In the Robot Operating System (ROS 2) framework, **Recording and Playing Back Data** refers to the systematic capture, serialization, archival, and temporal re-emission of inter-node message traffic (topics) using the official ROS 2 command-line tool **ros2 bag** (commonly referred to as *rosbag2*). A recorded dataset is stored as a **bag file** (typically in **MCAP**, **SQLite3**, or legacy **.bag** format) and is essential for offline debugging, regression testing, algorithm benchmarking, and reproducible experimentation in robotics research.

The recorded dataset encapsulates the exact **publish timestamps**, **topic names**, **message types**, and **serialized payload bytes** as they traversed the ROS 2 Data Distribution Service (DDS) middleware during the recording window.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain English Analogy (First-Time Reader Friendly)**
> Think of `ros2 bag` as a **"black-box flight recorder"** for your robot — the same kind of device found in commercial aircraft. While the robot is operating, every sensor reading (LiDAR scans, camera frames, IMU data, motor commands) and every internal chatter between software nodes is being **silently logged** to a file on disk. Later, even if the robot is switched off, you can **replay** that exact recording into the same ROS 2 graph as if the robot were running in real time. This lets developers:
> - **Debug** rare bugs that only happen "in the field"
> - **Test** new algorithms against the *exact same* sensor data
> - **Share** datasets with collaborators without sharing the physical robot

In short: **Record Once → Analyze Forever**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Must Memorize for KTU)

| Term | Meaning | Unit / Format |
|------|---------|---------------|
| **ros2 bag** | ROS 2 CLI tool for recording/playback | Command-line utility |
| **Bag File** | Serialized archive of ROS 2 messages | `.db3`, `.mcap`, `.bag` |
| **MCAP** | Modern robotics container format (default in Humble/Iron) | Logged container |
| **Storage ID** | Plugin identifier for backend (`mcap`, `sqlite3`) | String identifier |
| **Topic** | Named bus over which messages are published | e.g., `/turtle1/cmd_vel` |
| **Message** | Strongly-typed data packet (`.msg` definition) | bytes |
| **Publisher** | Node that writes messages to a topic | Node role |
| **Subscriber** | Node that reads messages from a topic | Node role |
| **QoS Profile** | Quality-of-Service policy (Reliability, Durability, History) | Policy object |
| **Clock** | Simulation vs. system time (use `- clock` for sim time) | `use_sim_time` parameter |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners frequently test the difference between **system time** (default `ros2 bag record`) and **simulation time** (required when using `ros2 bag play` with simulated robots). Always remember the `--clock` flag for the recorder and `use_sim_time:=true` parameter on `play`.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations — The "Why" Behind rosbag2

#### 2.1.1 Architectural Position in ROS 2
The rosbag2 toolchain operates **at the middleware layer**, *below* the application nodes but *above* the DDS implementation. This positioning is critical because:

- It captures messages **regardless of which DDS vendor** (Fast DDS, Cyclone DDS, RMW-Zenoh) is active.
- It serializes messages using the **CDR (Common Data Representation)** standard, ensuring cross-platform reproducibility.
- It writes to disk **asynchronously** to avoid blocking the real-time data path.

#### 2.1.2 The Recording Pipeline (Step-by-Step Logic)

The recording process follows a deterministic sequence:

1. **Node Subscription Injection:** When `ros2 bag record` is invoked, the tool spawns a *recorder node* that subscribes to the specified topics (or all topics with `-a`).
2. **Message Intercept:** Each published message is intercepted at the DDS subscription callback.
3. **Serialization:** The message is serialized into a CDR byte stream.
4. **Timestamping:** A high-resolution **header timestamp** (from `builtin_interfaces/Time`) and a **receive timestamp** are attached.
5. **Disk Write:** The serialized blob is appended to the active storage plugin (MCAP/SQLite).
6. **Metadata Indexing:** Topic names, types, and message counts are indexed for fast `info` queries.

> **Key Engineering Insight:** Because the recorder is *itself* a subscriber, recording a topic adds one extra hop in the DDS data path, marginally increasing CPU and memory load. For high-bandwidth sensors (e.g., 4K cameras at 30 Hz ≈ 600 Mbps), use **dedicated storage** (NVMe SSD) and consider **compression** via the MCAP `--compression-mode` flag.

#### 2.1.3 The Playback Pipeline (Step-by-Step Logic)

Playback is the *inverse* operation with an important nuance — **temporal fidelity**:

1. **Bag Opening:** The `.mcap`/`.db3` file is opened; metadata is read into memory.
2. **Rate Calculation:** The tool computes the original inter-message interval to determine publish rate.
3. **Node Publication:** A *player node* advertises the recorded topics and republishes each message.
4. **Clock Publication (Optional):** If `--clock` was used during recording, `/clock` messages are published for `use_sim_time` consumers.
5. **Loop / Once Mode:** `-r <factor>` rewrites time (e.g., `-r 2.0` plays at 2× speed); without `-r`, replay is real-time.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Reference Table — Master Before Exam**

| Command | Purpose | Mandatory Syntax Example |
|---------|---------|--------------------------|
| List available storage plugins | Verify MCAP support | `ros2 bag list-storages` |
| List available compression plugins | Verify ZSTD support | `ros2 bag list-compressions` |
| Record all topics | Full system capture | `ros2 bag record -a -o my_bag` |
| Record specific topics | Selective capture | `ros2 bag record /topic1 /topic2` |
| Record with sim time | For Gazebo/sim replay | `ros2 bag record -a --clock` |
| Inspect a bag | View metadata | `ros2 bag info my_bag` |
| Verbose info | Per-topic message count + frequency | `ros2 bag info my_bag -v` |
| Playback in real-time | Default replay | `ros2 bag play my_bag` |
| Playback at custom rate | Fast/slow motion | `ros2 bag play my_bag -r 2.0` |
| Loop playback infinitely | Continuous replay | `ros2 bag play my_bag -l` |
| Start playback at offset | Skip initial N seconds | `ros2 bag play my_bag -s 5` |

#### 2.2.1 Mathematical Relationship of Playback Rate

The effective playback rate is governed by:

$$
r_{\text{effective}} = r_{\text{original}} \times f_{\text{user}}
$$

Where $r_{\text{original}}$ is the recorded publish frequency in Hz, and $f_{\text{user}}$ is the rate factor passed via `-r`. If $f_{\text{user}} > 1.0$, playback is **faster than real-time**; if $f_{\text{user}} < 1.0$, it is **slower** (useful for manual inspection).

#### 2.2.2 Storage Sizing Estimation (Engineering Utility)

For exam problems or lab planning, the approximate bag file size can be estimated as:

$$
S_{\text{bag}} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( B_i \times f_i \times T \right)
$$

Where:
- $S_{\text{bag}}$ = total bag size (bytes)
- $N$ = number of topics
- $B_i$ = average message size of topic $i$ (bytes)
- $f_i$ = publish frequency of topic $i$ (Hz)
- $T$ = recording duration (seconds)

**Worked Example (KTU-style):** Record a 2D laser scan (`sensor_msgs/LaserScan`, $B = 3600$ bytes) at 40 Hz, and odometry (`nav_msgs/Odometry`, $B = 480$ bytes) at 50 Hz, for $T = 60$ seconds.

$$
S_{\text{bag}} \approx (3600 \times 40 \times 60) + (480 \times 50 \times 60)
$$

$$
S_{\text{bag}} \approx 8{,}640{,}000 + 1{,}440{,}000 = 10{,}080{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 9.61 \text{ MiB}
$$

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Self-Driving Cars):** Companies like Waymo and Apollo (Baidu) record millions of kilometers of driving data using bag files, then replay scenarios in simulation.
- **SLAM & Mapping:** Researchers replay LiDAR + odometry bags to test new SLAM algorithms against the *same* trajectory.
- **Regression Testing in CI/CD:** Bag files serve as deterministic test fixtures in automated pipelines (e.g., ROS 2 Build Farm).
- **Dataset Publishing:** Popular datasets (KITTI, EuRoC converted) are distributed as bag files for reproducibility.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Lab Mandate:** This section provides the *complete* experiment procedure as expected in the KTU Lab Record / Observation Manual. No steps are abbreviated.

### 3.1 Experiment 2: Recording and Playing Back Data Using ros2 bag

#### **Aim**
To record the messages published on selected topics of a running ROS 2 system, store them in a bag file, inspect the recorded metadata, and then play back the data while observing the topic activity.

#### **3.1.1 Hardware & Software Requirements**
- Ubuntu 22.04 LTS (or 20.04 with ROS 2 Foxy/Humble)
- ROS 2 Humble Hawksbill (or Iron Irwini) installed and sourced
- `ros-humble-rosbag2-*` packages
- `ros-humble-turtlesim` package
- Terminal emulator (≥ 3 terminals recommended)

#### **3.1.2 Pre-Lab Setup & Workspace Activation**

**Step 1 — Open Terminal 1 and source the ROS 2 environment.**

```
source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash
```

**Step 2 — Launch the turtlesim simulation node.**

```
ros2 run turtlesim turtlesim_node
```

**Step 3 — Open Terminal 2, source ROS 2, and verify topics are active.**

```
source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash
ros2 topic list
```

Expected output (partial):
```
/parameter_events
/rosout
/turtle1/cmd_vel
/turtle1/color_sensor
/turtle1/pose
```

#### **3.1.3 Step-by-Step Recording Procedure**

**Step 4 — Create a dedicated working directory.**

```
mkdir -p ~/ros2_bag_experiments
cd ~/ros2_bag_experiments
```

**Step 5 — Initiate recording on a specific topic with a custom bag name.**

```
ros2 bag record /turtle1/cmd_vel -o cmd_vel_recording
```

The tool prints:
```
[INFO] [ros2bag2_recorder]: Press CTRL+C to stop recording...
```

**Step 6 — Open Terminal 3, source ROS 2, and drive the turtle to generate movement data.**

```
source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash
ros2 topic pub /turtle1/cmd_vel geometry_msgs/msg/Twist "{linear: {x: 2.0, y: 0.0, z: 0.0}, angular: {x: 0.0, y: 0.0, z: 1.8}}" --rate 10
```

**Step 7 — Wait for 15–20 seconds, then return to Terminal 2 and press `CTRL + C` to stop recording.**

The output displays the storage path and metadata summary.

**Step 8 — Verify the bag file was created.**

```
ls -la cmd_vel_recording/
```

Expected output (truncated):

```
-rw-rw-r-- 1 user user  4096  ...  cmd_vel_recording_0.mcap
-rw-rw-r-- 1 user user   256  ...  metadata.yaml
```

#### **3.1.4 Step-by-Step Inspection Procedure**

**Step 9 — Display basic bag information.**

```
ros2 bag info cmd_vel_recording
```

**Step 10 — Display verbose per-topic statistics.**

```
ros2 bag info cmd_vel_recording -v
```

Expected fields:
- `Path`
- `Duration`
- `Start` / `End` timestamps
- `Size`
- `Message count`
- `Topic information` (name, type, count, frequency)

#### **3.1.5 Step-by-Step Playback Procedure**

**Step 11 — Stop the live publisher in Terminal 3 (`CTRL+C`). This step is critical** — otherwise the live node and the playback node will *both* publish, causing message collisions.

**Step 12 — Launch the playback from Terminal 2.**

```
ros2 bag play cmd_vel_recording
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** The turtle in the turtlesim window will *re-execute* the exact circular motion previously recorded, confirming successful temporal replay.

**Step 13 — (Optional) Playback at 2× speed.**

```
ros2 bag play cmd_vel_recording -r 2.0
```

**Step 14 — (Optional) Echo the topic in another terminal to verify messages.**

```
ros2 topic echo /turtle1/cmd_vel
```

#### **3.1.6 Step-by-Step Record-All Topics Variant**

**Step 15 — Stop all previous nodes. Launch turtlesim again.**

**Step 16 — Record every topic simultaneously.**

```
ros2 bag record -a -o full_session_bag
```

**Step 17 — Drive the turtle, wait, and stop recording.**

**Step 18 — Verify the multi-topic bag.**

```
ros2 bag info full_session_bag -v
```

Expected topics:
- `/turtle1/cmd_vel` (geometry_msgs/msg/Twist)
- `/turtle1/color_sensor` (turtlesim/msg/Color)
- `/turtle1/pose` (turtlesim/msg/Pose)
- `/parameter_events` (rcl_interfaces/msg/ParameterEvent)
- `/rosout` (rcl_interfaces/msg/Log)

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Command vs. Effect

| # | Action | Command | Effect on System |
|---|--------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | Open turtlesim | `ros2 run turtlesim turtlesim_node` | Starts publisher of `/turtle1/pose` & `/turtle1/color_sensor` |
| 2 | Begin record | `ros2 bag record /turtle1/cmd_vel -o X` | Spawns subscriber; writes to `X/` |
| 3 | Publish velocity | `ros2 topic pub ...` | Generates messages on `/turtle1/cmd_vel` |
| 4 | Stop record | `CTRL+C` | Closes bag; flushes metadata |
| 5 | Inspect | `ros2 bag info X -v` | Prints structured summary |
| 6 | Playback | `ros2 bag play X` | Re-publishes recorded messages in real-time |
| 7 | Verify replay | `ros2 topic echo /turtle1/cmd_vel` | Streams the replayed payloads |

### 3.3 Result & Inference

The recorded bag file faithfully captures the ROS 2 message stream, including the `Twist` velocity commands sent to the turtle. On playback, the messages are re-emitted with original timing, causing the turtlesim visualization to repeat the prior motion — confirming **deterministic, time-stamped, lossless capture and replay** of ROS 2 topics.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture: Recording & Playback Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PROD["PRODUCTION PHASE - RECORDING"]
        A1["Turtlesim Node"]:::pubNode
        A2["Velocity Publisher Terminal"]:::pubNode
        A3["Recorder Node - ros2 bag record"]:::recNode
        DB[("Bag File - .mcap / .db3")]:::storage
    end

    subgraph INSP["INSPECTION PHASE"]
        B1["ros2 bag info"]:::inspNode
        B2["Metadata Output"]:::outNode
    end

    subgraph REPLAY["REPLAY PHASE"]
        C1["ros2 bag play"]:::playNode
        C2["Topic Echo Subscriber"]:::subNode
        C3["Turtlesim Window Visualization"]:::visNode
    end

    A1 -- "/turtle1/pose, /turtle1/color_sensor" --> A3
    A2 -- "/turtle1/cmd_vel - Twist msgs" --> A3
    A3 -- "CDR serialize + timestamp" --> DB
    DB -- "Read metadata" --> B1
    B1 -- "Topic, type, count, duration" --> B2
    DB -- "Deserialize + republish" --> C1
    C1 -- "Replay messages" --> C2
    C1 -- "Replay messages" --> C3

    classDef pubNode fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#1B4F8C,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef recNode fill:#E74C3C,stroke:#922B21,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef storage fill:#F39C12,stroke:#935116,color:#000000
    classDef inspNode fill:#27AE60,stroke:#145A32,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef outNode fill:#82E0AA,stroke:#196F3D,color:#000000
    classDef playNode fill:#8E44AD,stroke:#4A235A,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef subNode fill:#16A085,stroke:#0E6251,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef visNode fill:#D4AC0D,stroke:#7D6608,color:#000000
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Lifecycle of a Single rosbag2 Session

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Terminal
    participant R as ros2 bag record
    participant P as Velocity Publisher
    participant T as Turtlesim
    participant FS as File System
    participant I as ros2 bag info
    participant PL as ros2 bag play

    U->>R: Invoke with -o bag_name
    R->>FS: Create bag_name/ directory
    R->>R: Subscribe to /turtle1/cmd_vel
    P->>T: Publish Twist messages
    T-->>P: ACK (loopback)
    Note over R,T: Recorder node intercepts via DDS
    R->>FS: Append CDR bytes + timestamp
    U->>R: CTRL+C
    R->>FS: Close file + write metadata.yaml
    U->>I: ros2 bag info bag_name
    I->>FS: Read metadata.yaml
    I-->>U: Display summary
    U->>PL: ros2 bag play bag_name
    PL->>FS: Open bag, parse MCAP
    PL->>T: Republish Twist messages
    T-->>U: Turtle moves in replayed pattern
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree: Choosing the Right ros2 bag Subcommand

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"What is your goal?"}:::dec
    Q1 -->|Capture data| Q2{"Single or multiple topics?"}:::dec
    Q2 -->|Single| A1["ros2 bag record /topic -o name"]:::cmd
    Q2 -->|All| A2["ros2 bag record -a -o name"]:::cmd
    Q1 -->|Verify contents| A3["ros2 bag info name -v"]:::cmd
    Q1 -->|Replay| Q3{"Need rate control?"}:::dec
    Q3 -->|Real-time| A4["ros2 bag play name"]:::cmd
    Q3 -->|Custom rate| A5["ros2 bag play name -r 2.0"]:::cmd
    Q3 -->|Loop forever| A6["ros2 bag play name -l"]:::cmd
    Q1 -->|Convert format| A7["ros2 bag convert -i in.db3 -o out.mcap"]:::cmd

    classDef dec fill:#FDF2E9,stroke:#A04000,color:#000000
    classDef cmd fill:#D6EAF8,stroke:#1B4F8C,color:#000000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
*What is `ros2 bag`? List any two commonly used subcommands of the tool.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

`ros2 bag` is the official ROS 2 command-line utility used to **record**, **play back**, and **inspect** the messages being exchanged between nodes on ROS 2 topics. The recorded data is stored in a structured file called a *bag file* (formats include `.mcap`, `.db3`, and legacy `.bag`).

Two commonly used subcommands are:
1. `ros2 bag record` — captures messages from specified topics (or all topics with `-a`) into a bag file.
2. `ros2 bag play` — replays the recorded messages back into the ROS 2 graph in real-time or at a user-specified rate.

*(Acceptable: `ros2 bag info` as the third valid subcommand for ½ mark bonus.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*Mention the default storage format used by ros2 bag in ROS 2 Humble. What is the purpose of the `--clock` flag during recording?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The default storage format in ROS 2 Humble Hawksbill is **MCAP** (with `sqlite3` as a fallback option, selected via the `-s` flag).

The `--clock` flag enables the recorder to also publish a `/clock` topic containing simulation-time messages. This is **mandatory** when the recorded data is intended to be replayed in a simulation environment (e.g., Gazebo) that uses the `use_sim_time` parameter, because it synchronizes the playback time with the simulated world's clock.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture and working of the ros2 bag record** command. Describe the data flow from a publisher node to the bag file. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the complete **step-by-step procedure** to record the `/turtle1/cmd_vel` topic while a turtle is moving in `turtlesim`, and subsequently play it back. Include all necessary terminal commands. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks**

The `ros2 bag record` command operates via a dedicated **recorder node** that is dynamically instantiated by the tool. The architecture consists of three layers:

1. **Application Layer:** The publisher node (e.g., the velocity publisher running in Terminal 3) serializes `geometry_msgs/msg/Twist` messages and pushes them to the DDS middleware via the ROS 2 client library (rclcpp or rclpy).
2. **Middleware Layer (DDS):** The DDS implementation (e.g., Fast DDS) routes the message to all matching subscribers, including our recorder.
3. **Recorder Layer:** The recorder subscribes to the specified topic, applies a callback, and:
   - Stamps the message with header and receive timestamps.
   - Serializes the message using CDR.
   - Appends the byte stream to the active MCAP/SQLite file.
   - Updates the in-memory index for `info` queries.

The block diagram (already shown in Section 4) maps these interactions. **Data flow:** `Publisher → DDS Topic → Recorder Subscriber Callback → Serializer → Disk File`. **(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for description, 2 for data flow explanation.)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks**

```
# Terminal 1
source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash
ros2 run turtlesim turtlesim_node
```

```
# Terminal 2 — verify topics
source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash
ros2 topic list
```

```
# Terminal 2 — start recording
mkdir -p ~/bag_lab && cd ~/bag_lab
ros2 bag record /turtle1/cmd_vel -o cmd_vel_lab
```

```
# Terminal 3 — publish velocity
source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash
ros2 topic pub /turtle1/cmd_vel geometry_msgs/msg/Twist \
"{linear: {x: 2.0, y: 0.0, z: 0.0}, angular: {x: 0.0, y: 0.0, z: 1.0}}" --rate 10
```

**Wait 20 seconds → Press CTRL+C in Terminal 2 to stop recording.**

```
