$$\int_V \rho\, z^2\, dV = \rho \int_0^h z^2\, dz \int_0^{2\pi} d\phi \int_0^r \rho'\, d\rho' = \rho \cdot \frac{h^3}{3} \cdot 2\pi \cdot \frac{r^2}{2} = \frac{\rho \pi r^2 h^3}{3}$$

Add and substitute $\rho = m/(\pi r^2 h)$:

$$I_{xx} = \frac{\rho \pi r^2 h}{12} (3r^2 + 4h^2) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\,I_{xx} = I_{yy} = \frac{m}{12}(3r^2 + h^2)\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Solid Sphere Inertia Tensor

Mass $m$, radius $R$, density $\rho = m / \left( \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3 \right) = \dfrac{3m}{4\pi R^3}$. Use spherical coordinates $(r, \theta, \phi)$, $dV = r^2 \sin\theta\, dr\, d\theta\, d\phi$.

$$I_{zz} = \int_V \rho\, (x^2 + y^2)\, dV = \rho \int_0^{2\pi} d\phi \int_0^{\pi} \sin\theta\, d\theta \int_0^R r^2 \sin^2\theta \cdot r^2\, dr$$

Wait — careful: $x^2 + y^2 = r^2 \sin^2\theta$ in spherical coordinates. Hence:

$$I_{zz} = \rho \int_0^{2\pi} d\phi \int_0^{\pi} \sin^3\theta\, d\theta \int_0^R r^4\, dr$$

Evaluate each integral: $\int_0^{2\pi} d\phi = 2\pi$, $\int_0^{\pi} \sin^3\theta\, d\theta = 4/3$, $\int_0^R r^4\, dr = R^5/5$.

$$I_{zz} = \rho \cdot 2\pi \cdot \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{R^5}{5} = \frac{8 \pi \rho R^5}{15} = \frac{8 \pi R^5}{15} \cdot \frac{3m}{4\pi R^3} = \frac{2mR^2}{5}$$

By isotropy:

$$\boxed{\,I_{xx} = I_{yy} = I_{zz} = \frac{2}{5} m R^2\,}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Filling a URDF `<inertial>` Block

**Problem.** A link is a solid aluminium cylinder of radius $r = 0.05 \;\text{m}$, height $h = 0.5 \;\text{m}$. Aluminium density $\rho_{\text{Al}} = 2710 \;\text{kg/m}^3$. The centre of mass coincides with the link origin. Compute all six inertia values and write the URDF block.

**Step 1 — Mass.**

$$m = \rho_{\text{Al}} \cdot \pi r^2 h = 2710 \cdot \pi \cdot (0.05)^2 \cdot 0.5 = 2710 \cdot 3.1416 \cdot 0.0025 \cdot 0.5 \approx 10.64 \;\text{kg}$$

**Step 2 — Principal moments (cylinder formula).**

$$I_{xx} = I_{yy} = \frac{m}{12}(3r^2 + h^2) = \frac{10.64}{12}\bigl(3(0.0025) + 0.25\bigr) = 0.8867 \cdot 0.2575 \approx 0.2283 \;\text{kg·m}^2$$

$$I_{zz} = \tfrac{1}{2} m r^2 = 0.5 \cdot 10.64 \cdot 0.0025 \approx 0.0133 \;\text{kg·m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Off-diagonal products of inertia.** Zero (cylinder symmetric about its axis).

**Step 4 — Final URDF block.**

```xml
<inertial>
  <origin xyz="0 0 0" rpy="0 0 0"/>
  <mass value="10.64"/>
  <inertia ixx="0.2283" ixy="0"     ixz="0"
           iyy="0.2283" iyz="0"     izz="0.0133"/>
</inertial>
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Lab Mistake:** Students often write $I_{zz} = \tfrac{1}{2}mr^2$ but type a value meant for the *diameter* ($2r$) instead of the *radius*. This halves the spin-axis inertia and produces a violently unstable simulation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — URDF `<link>` Property Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Link Element]:::root --> B[Inertial Block]:::inert
    A --> C[Visual Block]:::vis
    A --> D[Collision Block]:::coll
    A --> E[Optional Other]:::misc

    B --> B1[origin xyz rpy]
    B --> B2[mass value kg]
    B --> B3[inertia tensor 6 components]

    C --> C1[origin xyz rpy]
    C --> C2[geometry primitive or mesh]
    C --> C3[material optional]

    D --> D1[origin xyz rpy]
    D --> D2[geometry simplified primitive]
    D --> D3[surface optional contact params]

    B3 --> B3a[ixx]
    B3 --> B3b[iyy]
    B3 --> B3c[izz]
    B3 --> B3d[ixy product]
    B3 --> B3e[ixz product]
    B3 --> B3f[iyz product]

    C2 --> C2a[box]
    C2 --> C2b[cylinder]
    C2 --> C2c[sphere]
    C2 --> C2d[mesh stl dae]

    D2 --> D2a[box capsule]
    D2 --> D2b[cylinder convex hull]
    D2 --> D2c[sphere bounding sphere]

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,color:#fff,stroke:#0a1f3d
    classDef inert fill:#2e7d32,color:#fff,stroke:#1b5e20
    classDef vis fill:#1565c0,color:#fff,stroke:#0d47a1
    classDef coll fill:#c62828,color:#fff,stroke:#8e0000
    classDef misc fill:#6a1b9a,color:#fff,stroke:#38006b
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Pipeline of the Lab Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step1: Author skeleton URDF]:::step --> P2[Step2: Add inertial mass inertia]
    P2 --> P3[Step3: Add collision primitives]
    P3 --> P4[Step4: Validate urdf parser]
    P4 --> P5[Step5: Launch RViz visualisation]
    P5 --> P6[Step6: Launch Gazebo physics test]
    P6 --> P7[Step7: Verify gravity drop of links]
    P7 --> P8[Step8: Tune inertia iteratively]:::done

    classDef step fill:#f9a825,color:#000,stroke:#f57f17
    classDef done fill:#2e7d32,color:#fff,stroke:#1b5e20
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Inertia Tensor Workflow (Box Example)

```mermaid
graph LR
    M[Identify Link Mass]:::a --> G[Identify Geometry]:::a
    G --> G1[Box]:::b
    G --> G2[Cylinder]:::b
    G --> G3[Sphere]:::b
    G1 --> F1[Apply Box Formula]:::c
    G2 --> F2[Apply Cylinder Formula]:::c
    G3 --> F3[Apply Sphere Formula]:::c
    F1 --> O[Compute COM offset d]:::d
    F2 --> O
    F3 --> O
    O --> S[Apply Parallel Axis Theorem]:::e
    S --> U[Fill URDF Inertia Block]:::f

    classDef a fill:#0277bd,color:#fff
    classDef b fill:#558b2f,color:#fff
    classDef c fill:#ef6c00,color:#fff
    classDef d fill:#6a1b9a,color:#fff
    classDef e fill:#c62828,color:#fff
    classDef f fill:#2e7d32,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Physical Schematic — Link-Frame vs Centre-of-Mass Frame

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph LF[Link Frame at joint origin]
        LABEL1[origin xyz rpy]
    end
    subgraph COM[Centre of Mass Frame]
        LABEL2[mass m]
        LABEL3[inertia tensor Icm]
    end
    LF -- offset d = dxyz --> COM
    COM -- rotation Rcm --> PRIN[Principal Axes]
    PRIN --> P1[I1 about axis 1]
    PRIN --> P2[I2 about axis 2]
    PRIN --> P3[I3 about axis 3]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mapping:** The above diagrams map directly to the verification steps the examiner expects during the **lab viva** — specifically: *"Draw the link reference frame, mark the COM offset, and identify the principal axes of the body."*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
Differentiate between the **`<visual>`** tag and the **`<collision>`** tag of a URDF link. Why is it recommended to use a *simplified primitive* for collision geometry?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The `<visual>` tag defines the *graphical appearance* of a link used by the renderer (RViz). The `<collision>` tag defines the *contact boundary* used by the physics engine to detect interpenetration. A simplified primitive (e.g. cylinder, box) is recommended for collision because the physics engine must run **broad-phase and narrow-phase collision checks at 1 kHz**; using a high-poly mesh would consume excessive CPU. **Visual** meshes can be highly detailed because rendering happens infrequently compared to collision checks. **(3 marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
State the **parallel-axis theorem** (Steiner's theorem). Why is it important when filling the `<inertial>` block of a URDF link?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The parallel-axis theorem states that the moment of inertia $I$ of a body about an arbitrary axis equals its moment of inertia about the parallel axis through the centre of mass $I_{cm}$ plus $m d^2$, where $d$ is the perpendicular distance between the two axes:

$$I = I_{cm} + m d^2$$

In URDF, the inertia tensor is expressed in the **link's reference frame** (joint origin), not the centre of mass. The `<origin xyz>` element within the `<inertial>` block shifts the tensor from the COM to the link frame. **Without applying the parallel-axis theorem, the inertia values will be incorrect and the simulation will be unstable.** **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]* — **Inertia Computation and URDF Authoring**

**(a)** A URDF link represents a uniform solid **rectangular box** of mass $m = 2.0 \;\text{kg}$ with side lengths $w = 0.4\;\text{m}$ (along $x$), $h = 0.2\;\text{m}$ (along $y$), and $d = 0.1\;\text{m}$ (along $z$). The geometric centre coincides with the link frame origin. **Derive** the six components of the inertia tensor about the link frame and write the complete `<inertial>` block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the same box is repositioned so that its centre of mass is now offset by $\mathbf{d} = (0.1, 0, 0.05)\;\text{m}$ from the link origin (no rotation), **recompute** the inertia tensor about the link frame using the parallel-axis theorem and write the updated `<inertial>` block. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Step-by-step:**

Using the **box formula** with $w = 0.4$, $h = 0.2$, $d = 0.1$, $m = 2.0$:

$$I_{xx} = \frac{m}{12}(h^2 + d^2) = \frac{2.0}{12}(0.04 + 0.01) = \frac{2.0 \cdot 0.05}{12} = 0.00833 \;\text{kg·m}^2$$

$$I_{yy} = \frac{m}{12}(w^2 + d^2) = \frac{2.0}{12}(0.16 + 0.01) = \frac{2.0 \cdot 0.17}{12} = 0.02833 \;\text{kg·m}^2$$

$$I_{zz} = \frac{m}{12}(w^2 + h^2) = \frac{2.0}{12}(0.16 + 0.04) = \frac{2.0 \cdot 0.20}{12} = 0.03333 \;\text{kg·m}^2$$

By symmetry, $I_{xy} = I_{yz} = I_{xz} = 0$.

```xml
<inertial>
  <origin xyz="0 0 0" rpy="0 0 0"/>
  <mass value="2.0"/>
  <inertia ixx="0.00833" ixy="0"        ixz="0"
           iyy="0.02833" iyz="0"        izz="0.03333"/>
</inertial>
```
**(7 marks — 3 for formula, 3 for computation, 1 for URDF block)**

**(b) Step-by-step using parallel-axis theorem with $\mathbf{d} = (0.1, 0, 0.05)$:**

The general shift formula (Steiner):

$$I_{ij}^{\text{new}} = I_{ij}^{\text{cm}} - m\,d_i d_j + m \Vert \mathbf{d} \Vert^2 \delta_{ij}$$

Equivalent scalar forms:

$$I_{xx}^{\text{new}} = I_{xx}^{\text{cm}} + m(d_y^2 + d_z^2) = 0.00833 + 2.0(0 + 0.0025) = 0.00833 + 0.005 = 0.01333$$

$$I_{yy}^{\text{new}} = I_{yy}^{\text{cm}} + m(d_x^2 + d_z^2) = 0.02833 + 2.0(0.01 + 0.0025) = 0.02833 + 0.025 = 0.05333$$

$$I_{zz}^{\text{new}} = I_{zz}^{\text{cm}} + m(d_x^2 + d_y^2) = 0.03333 + 2.0(0.01 + 0) = 0.03333 + 0.02 = 0.05333$$

$$I_{xy}^{\text{new}} = I_{xy}^{\text{cm}} - m\,d_x d_y = 0 - 2.0(0.1)(0) = 0$$

$$I_{xz}^{\text{new}} = I_{xz}^{\text{cm}} - m\,d_x d_z = 0 - 2.0(0.1)(0.05) = -0.01$$

$$I_{yz}^{\text{new}} = I_{yz}^{\text{cm}} - m\,d_y d_z = 0 - 0 = 0$$

Updated URDF:

```xml
<inertial>
  <origin xyz="0.1 0 0.05" rpy="0 0 0"/>
  <mass value="2.0"/>
  <inertia ixx="0.01333" ixy="0"      ixz="-0.01"
           iyy="0.05333" iyz="0"      izz="0.05333"/>
</inertial>
```
**(7 marks — 4 for parallel-axis application, 2 for numerical values, 1 for URDF block)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q-A):** Most students forget that the **`<origin>` block in `<inertial>` represents the COM location**, not the link origin. If the COM is at $(0.1, 0, 0.05)$, the `<origin>` must say so. Failing to update it while using the offset inertia tensor is a 2-mark deduction guaranteed.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]* — **Collision Modelling and Physics Verification**

**(a)** Explain, with a labelled block diagram, the **complete pipeline** of adding collision properties to a URDF link. Discuss why collision geometry is often a **simplified primitive** (e.g. capsule) rather than the high-resolution visual mesh. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** You are given a robotic gripper link with the following collision geometry:

> a cylinder of radius $r = 0.03 \;\text{m}$ and height $h = 0.10 \;\text{m}$ whose longitudinal axis is aligned with the $x$-axis of the link frame (NOT the default $z$-axis). The link mass is $m = 0.5 \;\text{kg}$. Compute the **principal moments of inertia** and write the collision and inertial blocks. **Gazebo uses the default $z$-axis as the cylinder symmetry axis** — explain how you would rotate the cylinder so that its symmetry axis aligns with the link $x$-axis. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Block-diagram answer:**

```
Author skeleton URDF
       |
       v
Identify each link's contact volume
       |
       v
Choose primitive (box | cylinder | sphere | mesh)
       |
       v
For each link add:
   <collision>
       <origin xyz="..." rpy="..."/>
       <geometry>...primitive...</geometry>
   </collision>
       |
       v
Validate with:  check_urdf <file.urdf>
       |
       v
Spawn in Gazebo; verify by attempting inter-link contact
```

**Why simplified primitive:**
- Visual mesh = high-poly (often 10 000–100 000 triangles) → consumed only for **rendering** at 30–60 Hz.
- Collision mesh must handle $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ pair-wise checks at physics step (1 kHz in Gazebo).
- GJK/EPA collision algorithms are $\mathcal{O}(n)$ for convex primitives but $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ for arbitrary meshes.
- A primitive **capsule** (cylinder + hemispherical caps) provides smooth rolling contacts at joints.
- Common industrial arm convention: collision cylinder of radius ~110% of the link's actual cross-section.

**(7 marks — 2 for diagram, 3 for reasoning, 2 for practical rationale)**

**(b) Step-by-step:**

**Step 1 — Mass:** $m = 0.5 \;\text{kg}$.

**Step 2 — Re-orient cylinder so its axis is the $x$-axis.** The URDF `<cylinder>` primitive is always defined with its symmetry axis along the **local $z$** of the collision frame. To rotate it so the symmetry axis becomes the link's $x$-axis, apply a 90° rotation about the $y$-axis:

$$\mathbf{R}_y(90°) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos 90° & 0 & \sin 90° \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin 90° & 0 & \cos 90° \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

This swaps the local $z$ (cylinder axis) with the link's $x$. In URDF roll-pitch-yaw, a rotation of 90° about $y$ corresponds to **rpy = "0 1.5708 0"**.

**Step 3 — Principal moments about the centre of mass, axis along $x$.** A solid cylinder has the *same* inertia tensor whether its axis is $x$, $y$ or $z$, by isotropy of the body itself. We just relabel:

$$I_{\parallel} = I_{xx} = \frac{1}{2} m r^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.5)(0.03)^2 = 0.5 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.0009 = 0.000225 \;\text{kg·m}^2$$

$$I_{\perp} = I_{yy} = I_{zz} = \frac{m}{12}(3r^2 + h^2) = \frac{0.5}{12}\bigl(3(0.0009) + 0.01\bigr) = \frac{0.5}{12}(0.0027 + 0.01) = \frac{0.5 \cdot 0.0127}{12} = 0.000529 \;\text{kg·m}^2$$

**Step 4 — URDF blocks:**

```xml
<collision>
  <origin xyz="0 0 0" rpy="0 1.5708 0"/>
  <geometry>
    <cylinder radius="0.03" length="0.10"/>
  </geometry>
</collision>

<inertial>
  <origin xyz="0 0 0" rpy="0 0 0"/>
  <mass value="0.5"/>
  <inertia ixx="0.000225" ixy="0"        ixz="0"
           iyy="0.000529" iyz="0"        izz="0.000529"/>
</inertial>
```

**(7 marks — 2 for axis rotation reasoning, 3 for inertia formulas, 2 for URDF block)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q-B):** The most common mark-loss in this question is leaving the `<cylinder>` un-rotated (default $z$-axis) but then writing $I_{xx} = mr^2/2$ (which is correct only *if* the cylinder axis IS $x$). Students must do **both**: rotate the primitive *and* re-label the inertia tensor. A 4-mark deduction is awarded for the inconsistent pair.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU PCRUL508 — Module 2 Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **URDF link = `<visual>` + `<collision>` + `<inertial>` + (optional `<sensor>`)**. All three primary blocks are mandatory for a *simulatable* model.
- **`<inertial>` block syntax** is fixed: `<origin>`, `<mass>`, `<inertia ixx ixy ixz iyy iyz izz>`.
- **The mass MUST be a positive scalar** in kilograms; a zero mass makes the rigid-body equations singular.
- **The inertia tensor must be positive-definite** — all three principal moments must be positive; if Gazebo throws a "moment of inertia is not positive definite" error, you've typed a negative number or used diameter instead of radius.
- **Primitive formulas to memorise for KTU exam:** box ($m/12$ pattern), cylinder ($mr^2/2$ about axis, $m(3r^2 + h^2)/12$ about diameter), sphere ($2mr^2/5$). These three cover 90% of KTU lab questions.
- **Off-diagonal products of inertia** ($I_{xy}, I_{yz}, I_{xz}$) are zero for *principal-axis-aligned* primitives. Use the parallel-axis theorem whenever the COM is offset from the link frame.
- **`<collision>` geometry should always be simpler than `<visual>` geometry** — the principle of "render richly, collide cheaply". Typical choice: capsule (cylinder + hemisphere caps) for robot links, box for grippers, sphere for wheels.
- **`<origin xyz rpy>` inside `<inertial>` denotes the COM location, not the link frame origin.**
- **Rotation convention in URDF** is RPY (roll-pitch-yaw) in **radians** — not degrees, not quaternion. To rotate a cylinder's symmetry axis from default $z$ to $x$, use rpy = "0 1.5708 0" (90° about $y$).
- **Validation tools to remember:** `check_urdf <file.urdf>` (parses the XML and prints the tree), `urdf_to_graphiz <file.urdf>` (visualises the link/joint tree as a PDF), `gazebo <file.urdf>` (full physics test).
- **Negative gravity test:** to verify inertial block is correct, set `<gravity>1</gravity>` disabled in Gazebo — the link must stay in place; if it drifts, your COM offset is wrong.
- **KTU Viva Favourite Question:** *"Why is collision geometry separate from visual geometry?"* — Answer: rendering and collision detection have different performance budgets; decoupling allows independent optimisation.
- **KTU Viva Favourite Question 2:** *"What happens if you omit the `<inertial>` block in a URDF link?"* — Answer: Gazebo assigns a default massless body, the link will not respond to gravity and may produce NaN in the dynamics integration, causing the simulator to crash.
- **KTU Viva Favourite Question 3:** *"If a cylinder is re-oriented by 90° about the $y$-axis, do the principal moments of inertia change?"* — Answer: No, the principal moments are properties of the *body*, not its frame orientation; only the *components* $(I_{xx}, I_{yy}, I_{zz})$ get re-labelled.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating a 3DOF Robotic Arm from Scratch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL508 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS) LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Creating a 3DOF Robotic Arm from Scratch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **3 Degrees-of-Freedom (3DOF) Robotic Arm** is a serial-chain manipulator whose end-effector pose (position + orientation) in 3D space is governed by **three independent actuated joints**. In the context of the KTU 2024 ROS Lab (PCRUL508), a 3DOF arm is modelled and simulated using the **Unified Robot Description Format (URDF)** — an XML specification native to ROS that describes a robot as a kinematic tree of rigid **links** connected by **joints**, with associated inertial, visual, and collision properties.

The three degrees of freedom, by convention for a planar/spherical educational arm, are:

- **Joint 1 (Base / Waist)** → Revolute joint rotating about the Z-axis (yaw).
- **Joint 2 (Shoulder)** → Revolute joint rotating about the Y-axis (pitch).
- **Joint 3 (Elbow)** → Revolute joint rotating about the Y-axis (pitch).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCRUL508 – Module 2):**
> *"Create a 3DOF robotic arm from scratch using URDF, visualize in RViz, and publish joint states using `joint_state_publisher` and `robot_state_publisher`."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Arm

Think of your own arm when you reach for a coffee mug on your desk:

| Human Body Part | Robot Equivalent | DOF Provided |
|---|---|---|
| **Shoulder swivel** (rotate torso) | Base revolute joint (yaw) | $\theta_1$ |
| **Shoulder pitch** (raise upper arm) | Shoulder revolute joint (pitch) | $\theta_2$ |
| **Elbow pitch** (bend forearm) | Elbow revolute joint (pitch) | $\theta_3$ |
| **Hand** (grip) | End-effector (passive) | — |

Your hand's **position** in space is fully determined by these three angles (your wrist stays fixed, palm down). A 3DOF arm makes the **same engineering compromise** — sufficient to reach any point in a 3D reachable workspace, but it cannot independently orient the gripper (which would require 6DOF).

### 1.3 Core Terminology Anchors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Link** — A rigid body segment of the robot (e.g., upper arm, forearm).
> - **Joint** — A constrained connection between two links (here: all **revolute**).
> - **Base Link** — The fixed, immovable root of the URDF tree (anchored to the world).
> - **End-Effector / Tool Link** — The terminal link representing the gripper or tool.
> - **TF (Transform) Tree** — The hierarchical pose graph ROS computes from the URDF.
> - **Workspace** — The volume of all points the end-effector can reach.

**Standard constant used in ROS:** Gravity vector default $\mathbf{g} = -9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ along world Z.
**Joint limit convention:** All angles in **radians**, all lengths in **metres**, all masses in **kilograms** (SI units — URDF strictly requires SI).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Kinematic Chain — Step-by-Step Logic

A serial 3DOF arm is described by a **rooted tree** of links. The logical construction order is:

1. **Anchor** the robot in the world via a fixed `base_link`.
2. **Attach** the first moving link to `base_link` through a revolute joint (Joint 1: yaw).
3. **Attach** the second link to the first through another revolute joint (Joint 2: shoulder pitch).
4. **Attach** the third link to the second (Joint 3: elbow pitch).
5. **Terminate** with a `gripper_link` rigidly fixed to the third link.

Each joint defines:
- An **axis of rotation** (unit vector in parent frame).
- An **origin** (pose offset of the child link frame in the parent frame).
- **Limits** (lower, upper, effort, velocity).

### 2.2 Degrees of Freedom — The Grübler–Kutzbach Criterion

For a planar/spherical serial chain with all revolute joints in 3D:

$$n_{DOF} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i - 6 \cdot (n - 1)$$

where $f_i$ is the DOF of joint $i$ (revolute $= 1$) and $n$ is the number of moving links. For a 3DOF arm: $n_{DOF} = 3(1) - 6(2) = -9$ — this formula applies to **closed/spatial mechanisms**. For a **simple open chain**, DOF = number of actuated revolute joints = **3** (the formula is informal in textbooks for serial arms).

### 2.3 Forward Kinematics — The Core Mathematics

The pose of the end-effector is the chain of homogeneous transformation matrices:

$$T_{0}^{3} = T_{0}^{1} \cdot T_{1}^{2} \cdot T_{2}^{3}$$

Each $T_{i}^{i+1}$ is a $4 \times 4$ matrix of the form:

$$T_{i}^{i+1} =
\begin{bmatrix}
R_{3 \times 3} & \mathbf{p}_{3 \times 1} \\
\mathbf{0}_{1 \times 3} & 1
\end{bmatrix}$$

where $R$ is the $3 \times 3$ rotation matrix and $\mathbf{p}$ is the $3 \times 1$ position vector of the child origin in the parent frame.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always use SI units. No exceptions — URDF parser will reject non-SI mass/length.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rotation about Z (yaw) by $\theta_1$ | $R_z(\theta_1)$ | rad | Base joint |
| 2 | Rotation about Y (pitch) by $\theta_2$ | $R_y(\theta_2)$ | rad | Shoulder |
| 3 | Rotation about Y (pitch) by $\theta_3$ | $R_y(\theta_3)$ | rad | Elbow |
| 4 | End-effector position | $\mathbf{p}_{ee} = T_{0}^{3} \cdot [0,0,0,1]^T$ | m | FK output |
| 5 | Workspace radius (max reach) | $r_{max} = L_1 + L_2 + L_3$ | m | Reach analysis |
| 6 | Workspace inner radius (singularity) | $r_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 - L_3 \vert$ | m | Dead zone |
| 7 | Reachable volume | $V = \tfrac{4}{3}\pi (r_{max}^3 - r_{min}^3)$ | m³ | Approx. (if spherical) |
| 8 | TF broadcast rate (default) | $30$ Hz | Hz | `robot_state_publisher` |
| 9 | RViz Fixed Frame | `base_link` | — | Visualization root |
| 10 | Joint state topic | `/joint_states` | — | SensorMsgs/JointState |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

A 3DOF educational arm is the **canonical first project** in ROS-based robotics because it forces the student to confront — at a manageable scale — every problem faced in industrial 6/7DOF arms: **URDF authorship, TF tree consistency, joint-state publishing, RViz debugging, FK validation, and (optionally) Gazebo simulation**. The same workflow scales to manipulators from Universal Robots (UR3/UR5/UR10), Franka Emika Panda, and KUKA arms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Forward Kinematics of a 3DOF Arm

Let the link lengths be $L_1, L_2, L_3$ (metres). The Denavit–Hartenberg (DH) parameter table is:

| Joint $i$ | $\theta_i$ (variable) | $d_i$ (offset) | $a_i$ (length) | $\alpha_i$ (twist) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_1$ | $0$ | $0$ | $\pi/2$ |
| 2 | $\theta_2$ | $0$ | $L_2$ | $0$ |
| 3 | $\theta_3$ | $0$ | $L_3$ | $0$ |

**Step 1 — Standard DH transformation matrix:**

$$
T_i^{i+1} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i \cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i \sin\alpha_i & a_i \cos\theta_i \\
\sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i \cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i \sin\alpha_i & a_i \sin\theta_i \\
0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Substituting Joint 1 ($\alpha_1 = \pi/2$):**

$$
T_{0}^{1} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_1 & 0 & \sin\theta_1 & 0 \\
\sin\theta_1 & 0 & -\cos\theta_1 & 0 \\
0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

*Explanation:* The twist $\alpha_1 = \pi/2$ rotates the X-axis of frame 1 to be vertical, allowing the second joint to act in a perpendicular plane.

**Step 3 — Substituting Joint 2 ($\alpha_2 = 0, a_2 = L_2$):**

$$
T_{1}^{2} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_2 & -\sin\theta_2 & 0 & L_2 \cos\theta_2 \\
\sin\theta_2 & \cos\theta_2 & 0 & L_2 \sin\theta_2 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

*Explanation:* Pure 2D planar rotation in the shoulder plane, translated by $L_2$ along the rotated X-axis.

**Step 4 — Substituting Joint 3 ($\alpha_3 = 0, a_3 = L_3$):**

$$
T_{2}^{3} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_3 & -\sin\theta_3 & 0 & L_3 \cos\theta_3 \\
\sin\theta_3 & \cos\theta_3 & 0 & L_3 \sin\theta_3 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Compose $T_{0}^{3} = T_{0}^{1} \cdot T_{1}^{2} \cdot T_{2}^{3}$:**

$$
T_{0}^{2} = T_{0}^{1} \cdot T_{1}^{2} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_1 \cos\theta_2 & -\cos\theta_1 \sin\theta_2 & \sin\theta_1 & L_2 \cos\theta_1 \cos\theta_2 \\
\sin\theta_1 \cos\theta_2 & -\sin\theta_1 \sin\theta_2 & -\cos\theta_1 & L_2 \sin\theta_1 \cos\theta_2 \\
\sin\theta_2 & \cos\theta_2 & 0 & L_2 \sin\theta_2 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 — Final end-effector position vector (dropping orientation):**

$$
\mathbf{p}_{ee} =
\begin{bmatrix}
x \\
y \\
z
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_1 (L_2 \cos\theta_2 + L_3 \cos(\theta_2 + \theta_3)) \\
\sin\theta_1 (L_2 \cos\theta_2 + L_3 \cos(\theta_2 + \theta_3)) \\
L_2 \sin\theta_2 + L_3 \sin(\theta_2 + \theta_3)
\end{bmatrix}
$$

*Explanation:* The X and Y coordinates are modulated by the base yaw $\theta_1$, while the Z coordinate is independent of $\theta_1$ — this is the geometric signature of a **spherical-coordinate-style 3DOF arm**.

### 3.2 Analytical Verification (Worked Numerical Example)

Take $L_1 = 0.1$ m (vertical base column), $L_2 = 1.0$ m, $L_3 = 0.8$ m.
Set $\theta_1 = 0, \, \theta_2 = \pi/2, \, \theta_3 = 0$ (arm stretched straight up, aligned along Z).

$$
x = \cos 0 \cdot (1.0 \cos(\pi/2) + 0.8 \cos(\pi/2 + 0)) = 0
$$

$$
y = \sin 0 \cdot (\ldots) = 0
$$

$$
z = 1.0 \sin(\pi/2) + 0.8 \sin(\pi/2) = 1.0 + 0.8 = 1.8 \text{ m}
$$

**Result:** End-effector at $(0, 0, 1.8)$ m — matches the geometric intuition of a vertically extended arm of total reach $1.0 + 0.8 = 1.8$ m. ✓

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — URDF Element Reference

| URDF Tag | Type | Mandatory? | Purpose | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `<robot name="...">` | Root | Yes | Names the robot | `"three_dof_arm"` |
| `<link name="...">` | Block | Yes | Defines a rigid body | `"upper_arm_link"` |
| `<inertial>` | Block | Yes (for Gazebo) | Mass + inertia tensor | $m = 1.0$ kg, $I_{xx} = 0.1$ |
| `<visual>` | Block | Yes | Mesh / primitive for rendering | `<box size="0.1 1 0.1"/>` |
| `<collision>` | Block | Yes | Mesh / primitive for physics | Same as visual (typical) |
| `<joint name="..." type="...">` | Block | Yes | Defines constraint | `type="revolute"` |
| `<parent link="..."/>` | Element | Yes | Tree parent | `"base_link"` |
| `<child link="..."/>` | Element | Yes | Tree child | `"shoulder_link"` |
| `<axis xyz="..."/>` | Element | Yes (for revolute) | Rotation axis | `xyz="0 1 0"` |
| `<limit lower="..." upper="..." effort="..." velocity="..."/>` | Element | Yes (revolute) | Safety limits | `-1.57 1.57 10 1` |
| `<origin xyz="..." rpy="..."/>` | Element | Common | Pose offset | `xyz="0 0 0.5"` |
| `<gazebo>` | Block | Optional | Gazebo-specific tuning | Material colour, plugin |

### 3.4 Launch File Mental Model

A typical launch file orchestrates four nodes:

- `joint_state_publisher` → publishes `/joint_states` from GUI sliders or URDF defaults.
- `robot_state_publisher` → consumes `/joint_states` + URDF, broadcasts TF.
- `rviz` → visualises TF tree + robot model.
- (Optional) `gazebo` → loads URDF into physics simulator.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Kinematic Tree of the 3DOF Arm

```mermaid
graph TD
    worldA[World Frame]:::env
    baseA[base_link]:::fixed
    columnA[base_column_link]:::link
    upperA[upper_arm_link]:::link
    foreA[forearm_link]:::link
    gripA[gripper_link]:::tool

    worldA --- baseA
    baseA -- "fixed_joint" --- columnA
    columnA -- "joint1 (revolute Z, theta1)" --- upperA
    upperA -- "joint2 (revolute Y, theta2)" --- foreA
    foreA -- "joint3 (revolute Y, theta3)" --- gripA

    classDef env fill:#2b2b2b,stroke:#888,color:#fff;
    classDef fixed fill:#5b8def,stroke:#000,color:#fff;
    classDef link fill:#f4b400,stroke:#000,color:#000;
    classDef tool fill:#34a853,stroke:#000,color:#fff;
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — ROS Node / Topic Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    GUI[Joint State Publisher GUI]:::node
    JSP[joint_state_publisher]:::node
    RSP[robot_state_publisher]:::node
    RVIZ[RViz Visualizer]:::node
    GZ[Gazebo Simulator]:::node

    GUI -- "slider values" --> JSP
    JSP -- "/joint_states" --> RSP
    RSP -- "TF transforms" --> RVIZ
    RSP -- "robot_description" --> GZ
    GZ -- "/joint_commands" --> RSP

    classDef node fill:#1a73e8,stroke:#000,color:#fff;
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Functional Workflow (Build → Simulate → Visualise)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Create ROS package] --> S2[Step 2: Author URDF XML]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Add inertial / visual / collision]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Define three revolute joints]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Write launch file]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: roslaunch and verify TF]
    S6 --> S7{Solves in RViz?}
    S7 -- No --> S8[Check joint axis and origin frames]
    S8 --> S2
    S7 -- Yes --> S9[Spawn in Gazebo]
    S9 --> S10[Send /joint_commands and validate motion]

    classDef step fill:#fff,stroke:#333,color:#000;
    classDef ok fill:#34a853,stroke:#000,color:#fff;
    class S10 ok;
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block — Workspace Geometry (Top View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    O((Origin base_link)):::center
    R1[Inner Dead Zone radius r_min]:::zone
    R2[Outer Reachable radius r_max]:::zone
    A[End Effector Sample Point]:::point

    O --- R1
    O --- R2
    O --- A

    classDef center fill:#000,stroke:#000,color:#fff;
    classDef zone fill:#fbbc04,stroke:#000,color:#000;
    classDef point fill:#ea4335,stroke:#000,color:#fff;
```

> [!NOTE]
> The annular region between $r_{min}$ and $r_{max}$ is the **reachable workspace**. Points inside $r_{min}$ (the singular collapsed-arm configuration) cannot be reached.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]** Define Degrees of Freedom (DOF) for a robotic manipulator. How many DOF does a 3DOF planar arm have, and what is its limitation?

**Model Answer:**
Degrees of Freedom (DOF) is the **number of independent parameters** required to fully specify the configuration (pose) of a robot's end-effector in 3D space. A 3DOF arm has three actuated revolute joints, so it has **3 DOF**. Its primary limitation is that it can **position** the end-effector anywhere in its reachable workspace but **cannot fully orient** it — for full 6D pose control, a 6DOF arm is required (3 for position + 3 for orientation). **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model]** What is the role of the `robot_state_publisher` node in a ROS-based robotic arm?

**Model Answer:**
The `robot_state_publisher` node **consumes the URDF** (robot description) and the live `/joint_states` topic published by `joint_state_publisher`, and **broadcasts the resulting 3D transforms** (TF) between every link of the robot at a fixed rate (default **30 Hz**). This TF tree allows RViz to visualise the arm, Gazebo to update its physics, and any downstream node (motion planning, IK solver) to query the current pose of any link via `tf2_ros`. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> When drawing the URDF tree, students frequently:
> 1. Forget to set **`<axis xyz>`** — RViz silently renders the arm as rigid.
> 2. Mix **radians and degrees** in `<limit>` tags — the parser accepts only radians.
> 3. Omit the **`safety_controller`** block for Gazebo — the arm may self-destruct in simulation.
> 4. Use **non-SI units** (e.g., mm for length) — URDF silently fails to load inertia.
> **Deduction pattern:** 1–2 marks per slip, capped at 50% of the sub-question.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — URDF Authoring and FK Validation

**(a)** Draw the kinematic tree and write the **complete URDF XML** for a 3DOF arm having:
- `base_link` (fixed root)
- `upper_arm_link` of length **1.0 m**, mass **1.0 kg**, box geometry $0.1 \times 1.0 \times 0.1$ m
- `forearm_link` of length **0.8 m**, mass **0.8 kg**, box geometry $0.08 \times 0.8 \times 0.08$ m
- `gripper_link` of mass **0.2 kg**
- Three revolute joints: `joint1` (Z-axis, base), `joint2` (Y-axis, shoulder), `joint3` (Y-axis, elbow) with limits $\pm \pi/2$ rad. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a):**

```xml
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<robot name="three_dof_arm">
  <!-- BASE LINK (fixed root) -->
  <link name="base_link">
    <visual>
      <geometry><box size="0.2 0.2 0.1"/></geometry>
      <material name="grey"><color rgba="0.5 0.5 0.5 1"/></material>
    </visual>
  </link>

  <!-- UPPER ARM LINK (L1 = 1.0 m) -->
  <link name="upper_arm_link">
    <inertial>
      <origin xyz="0 0 0.5" rpy="0 0 0"/>
      <mass value="1.0"/>
      <inertia ixx="0.0833" ixy="0" ixz="0" iyy="0.0833" iyz="0" izz="0.0017"/>
    </inertial>
    <visual>
      <origin xyz="0 0 0.5" rpy="0 0 0"/>
      <geometry><box size="0.1 0.1 1.0"/></geometry>
    </visual>
    <collision>
      <origin xyz="0 0 0.5" rpy="0 0 0"/>
      <geometry><box size="0.1 0.1 1.0"/></geometry>
    </collision>
  </link>

  <!-- FOREARM LINK (L2 = 0.8 m) -->
  <link name="forearm_link">
    <inertial>
      <origin xyz="0 0 0.4" rpy="0 0 0"/>
      <mass value="0.8"/>
      <inertia ixx="0.0427" ixy="0" ixz="0" iyy="0.0427" iyz="0" izz="0.0011"/>
    </inertial>
    <visual>
      <origin xyz="0 0 0.4" rpy="0 0 0"/>
      <geometry><box size="0.08 0.08 0.8"/></geometry>
    </visual>
    <collision>
      <origin xyz="0 0 0.4" rpy="0 0 0"/>
      <geometry><box size="0.08 0.08 0.8"/></geometry>
    </collision>
  </link>

  <!-- GRIPPER LINK -->
  <link name="gripper_link">
    <inertial>
      <mass value="0.2"/>
      <inertia ixx="0.001" ixy="0" ixz="0" iyy="0.001" iyz="0" izz="0.001"/>
    </inertial>
    <visual>
      <geometry><box size="0.05 0.15 0.05"/></geometry>
    </visual>
  </link>

  <!-- JOINT 1: Base yaw (revolute, Z-axis) -->
  <joint name="joint1" type="revolute">
    <parent link="base_link"/>
    <child link="upper_arm_link"/>
    <origin xyz="0 0 0.05" rpy="0 0 0"/>
    <axis xyz="0 0 1"/>
    <limit lower="-1.5708" upper="1.5708" effort="50" velocity="1.0"/>
  </joint>

  <!-- JOINT 2: Shoulder pitch (revolute, Y-axis) -->
  <joint name="joint2" type="revolute">
    <parent link="upper_arm_link"/>
    <child link="forearm_link"/>
    <origin xyz="0 0 1.0" rpy="0 0 0"/>
    <axis xyz="0 1 0"/>
    <limit lower="-1.5708" upper="1.5708" effort="50" velocity="1.0"/>
  </joint>

  <!-- JOINT 3: Elbow pitch (revolute, Y-axis) -->
  <joint name="joint3" type="revolute">
    <parent link="forearm_link"/>
    <child link="gripper_link"/>
    <origin xyz="0 0 0.8" rpy="0 0 0"/>
    <axis xyz="0 1 0"/>
    <limit lower="-1.5708" upper="1.5708" effort="50" velocity="1.0"/>
  </joint>
</robot>
```

**(b)** For the arm above, compute the end-effector position when $\theta_1 = 0, \theta_2 = \pi/2, \theta_3 = -\pi/2$. Explain the geometric configuration. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**

From the derivation in Section 3.1, the end-effector position is:

$$
\mathbf{p}_{ee} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_1 (L_2 \cos\theta_2 + L_3 \cos(\theta_2 + \theta_3)) \\
\sin\theta_1 (L_2 \cos\theta_2 + L_3 \cos(\theta_2 + \theta_3)) \\
L_2 \sin\theta_2 + L_3 \sin(\theta_2 + \theta_3)
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Substituting $\theta_1 = 0, \theta_2 = \pi/2, \theta_3 = -\pi/2$, $L_2 = 1.0$, $L_3 = 0.8$:

$$
x = \cos 0 \cdot (1.0 \cos(\pi/2) + 0.8 \cos(\pi/2 - \pi/2)) = 1.0 \cdot (0 + 0.8 \cdot 1) = 0.8
$$

$$
y = \sin 0 \cdot (\ldots) = 0
$$

$$
z = 1.0 \sin(\pi/2) + 0.8 \sin(\pi/2 - \pi/2) = 1.0 + 0.8 \cdot 0 = 1.0
$$

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{p}_{ee} = (0.8, \, 0, \, 1.0) \text{ m}}
$$

**Geometric interpretation:** The arm is rotated $90°$ at the shoulder (forearm pointing horizontally forward), and then the elbow folds back by $90°$, so the gripper sits **1.0 m above the base** and **0.8 m forward** — a classic "L-shaped" reaching pose. ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Launch File and RViz Visualisation

**(a)** Write the **complete ROS 2 launch file (Python)** that:
- Loads the URDF from a `urdf/` folder
- Starts `robot_state_publisher`
- Starts `joint_state_publisher_gui`
- Launches RViz with a preconfigured view. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a):**

```python
from launch import LaunchDescription
from launch_ros.actions import Node
from launch.substitutions import Command, PathJoinSubstitution
from launch_ros.substitutions import FindPackageShare

def generate_launch_description():
    urdf_path = PathJoinSubstitution([
        FindPackageShare("three_dof_arm"),
        "urdf",
        "three_dof_arm.urdf"
    ])

    rsp_node = Node(
        package="robot_state_publisher",
        executable="robot_state_publisher",
        parameters=[{"robot_description": Command(["cat ", urdf_path])}]
    )

    jsp_gui_node = Node(
        package="joint_state_publisher_gui",
        executable="joint_state_publisher_gui"
    )

    rviz_config = PathJoinSubstitution([
        FindPackageShare("three_dof_arm"),
        "rviz",
        "config.rviz"
    ])

    rviz_node = Node(
        package="rviz2",
        executable="rviz2",
        arguments=["-d", rviz_config],
        output="screen"
    )

    return LaunchDescription([rsp_node, jsp_gui_node, rviz_node])
```

**Marking distribution:** 2 marks for `robot_state_publisher` setup, 2 marks for `joint_state_publisher_gui`, 2 marks for RViz config wiring, 1 mark for clean imports/path substitution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how the **TF tree** is constructed from the URDF, and how RViz uses it to render the moving arm when you drag a joint slider. Include the role of the `<robot_description>` parameter. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**

1. **URDF Parsing** — When `robot_state_publisher` starts, ROS loads the URDF XML from the **`robot_description`** parameter (a string parameter on the parameter server). It parses the `<link>` and `<joint>` elements and builds an internal **KDL kinematic tree**. **(2 marks)**

2. **TF Tree Construction** — The publisher walks the tree from `base_link` downwards, computing the static parent-to-child transforms (from `<origin>` tags) once. These constitute the **fixed backbone** of the TF tree. **(1.5 marks)**

3. **Live Joint Updates** — Every time a `/joint_states` message arrives (published by `joint_state_publisher_gui` whenever a slider moves), the publisher recomputes the **dynamic transforms** for the three revolute joints by applying the rotation matrices $R_z(\theta_1), R_y(\theta_2), R_y(\theta_3)$ and chains them up the tree. **(1.5 marks)**

4. **RViz Visualisation** — RViz subscribes to the TF topic and queries the pose of every link. It then re-renders each `<visual>` mesh at its new world-frame position. The result is **real-time, lag-free** motion of the arm. **(1 mark)**

5. **Role of `<robot_description>`** — It is the **single source of truth** for the robot's geometry. Both `robot_state_publisher` and Gazebo's `spawn_entity.py` consume it; hence, any change to the URDF requires restarting these nodes to refresh the parameter. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise these before stepping into the exam hall:**

- ☐ A 3DOF arm has **3 actuated revolute joints**; it positions but does **not** orient.
- ☐ URDF must use **SI units** — radians, metres, kilograms — always.
- ☐ Every `<joint>` of type `revolute` **must** have an `<axis>` and a `<limit>` block.
- ☐ The TF tree is rooted at a **fixed link**, usually `base_link`; it cannot float.
- ☐ `joint_state_publisher` → publishes `/joint_states`; `robot_state_publisher` → broadcasts TF.
- ☐ Default TF broadcast rate = **30 Hz**; default RViz Fixed Frame = **`base_link`**.
- ☐ FK formula: $T_{0}^{3} = T_{0}^{1} \cdot T_{1}^{2} \cdot T_{2}^{3}$.
- ☐ Max reach = sum of link lengths; singularity radius = absolute difference.
- ☐ Xacro is the **macro-preprocessor** for URDF — use it for modularity, not for runtime.
- ☐ Gazebo requires a `<gazebo>` plugin for each joint to actuate — the URDF alone is inert.
- ☐ To verify FK: spawn the arm in RViz, set joint angles in GUI, observe end-effector pose, **and** cross-check with manual matrix multiplication.
- ☐ `urdf_tutorial` package is the **official KTU-recommended starting point** for this lab.
- ☐ The end-effector Z-coordinate is **independent of $\theta_1$** (base yaw) — a key exam trap.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Gazebo and its Integration with ROS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL508 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS) LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Gazebo and its Integration with ROS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gazebo** is an open-source, multi-robot **3D dynamic simulator** used in robotics research and education. It accurately simulates **rigid-body dynamics**, **sensor noise**, and **environmental interactions** in realistic scenarios, enabling developers to test algorithms on virtual robots before deploying them on physical hardware.

**Integration with ROS** refers to the seamless coupling between the Gazebo physics engine and the **Robot Operating System (ROS)** middleware, achieved through the `gazebo_ros_pkgs` meta-package. This integration allows sensor data generated in Gazebo to be published as ROS topics, control commands from ROS nodes to actuate simulated joints, and robot descriptions (**URDF/SDF**) to be loaded directly into the simulation environment.

The simulation step is governed by a fixed update rate, typically **1 kHz (1000 Hz)**, while the ROS communication layer operates over **TCP/IP** using **XML-RPC** for service calls and **TCPROS/UDPROS** for topic-based message passing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Analogy: A Flight Simulator for Robots**

Think of Gazebo as a **flight simulator**, but for ground, aerial, and underwater robots instead of airplanes.

- The **virtual world** is the rendered scene (rooms, terrain, obstacles).
- The **robot's body** is a digital twin — every joint, link, and sensor is mathematically modeled.
- **Physics laws** (gravity, friction, inertia) are continuously solved so the robot behaves like a real machine.
- **Virtual sensors** (cameras, LIDAR, IMU) generate data indistinguishable from real sensors, which ROS can consume as if it were physical hardware.

When you write a ROS node that subscribes to `/camera/image_raw`, it does not care whether the camera is a physical webcam attached via USB or a virtual camera rendered inside Gazebo. **This transparency is the core power of the ROS-Gazebo integration.**

## 1.3 Key Standards and Physical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry-Standard Defaults in Gazebo (KTU Reference)**
> - **Gravity vector:** $g = 9.80665 \ \text{m/s}^2$ (downward, $z$-axis)
> - **Default real-time factor (RTF):** $1.0$ (simulation matches wall-clock time)
> - **Max step size:** $\Delta t = 0.001 \ \text{s}$ (**1 ms** per physics tick)
> - **Recommended rendering rate:** 30–60 Hz
> - **Coordinate frame convention:** **REP-103** (x-forward, y-left, z-up for ground robots)

These parameters are **NOT arbitrary** — they are tuned to match real-world physics so that controllers designed in simulation transfer seamlessly to hardware (a concept called *sim-to-real transfer*).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of Gazebo

Gazebo's internal architecture is composed of **five primary subsystems**, each handling a distinct responsibility:

### 2.1.1 The Physics Engine (`ode`, `bullet`, `dart`, `simbody`)
- Solves the equations of motion for every rigid body in the scene.
- Uses **penalty-based** or **constraint-based** contact resolution.
- Applies Newton's second law and Euler/Lagrangian integration schemes.

### 2.1.2 The Rendering Engine (`OGRE 2.x`)
- Handles 3D graphics, lighting, shadows, and texture mapping.
- Operates independently from the physics loop to maximize throughput.

### 2.1.3 The Sensor Module
- Models ideal sensors and adds realistic noise (Gaussian, salt-pepper, drift).
- Generates output that mimics real hardware interfaces (e.g., camera image arrays, laser scans).

### 2.1.4 The Communication Layer (`gazebo_ros_pkgs`)
- Bridges Gazebo's internal **Gazebo Transport** (based on Protocol Buffers over ZeroMQ) with **ROS topics/services/actions**.

### 2.1.5 The Scene Description Parser
- Accepts **SDF (Simulation Description Format)** as the native format.
- Also parses **URDF** via the `spawn_model` tool for legacy compatibility.

## 2.2 The ROS-Gazebo Bridge (`gazebo_ros_pkgs`)

The integration is **NOT magic** — it is implemented as a set of ROS packages:

| Package | Function | Key Executable |
|---|---|---|
| `gazebo_ros` | Core launch files and C++ utilities | `gazebo_ros`, `spawn_model` |
| `gazebo_plugins` | Sensor and actuator plugins exposing ROS interfaces | `libgazebo_ros_camera.so` |
| `gazebo_msgs` | Custom message types (e.g., `LinkStates`, `ModelStates`) | — |
| `gazebo_ros_control` | Joint-position/velocity/effort controllers via `ros_control` | `controller_manager` |
| `gazebo_ros_pkgs` | Meta-package containing all of the above | — |

## 2.3 Communication Channels

ROS and Gazebo exchange data through **three primary channels**:

1. **Topics** — continuous, asynchronous data streams (e.g., `/laser_scan`, `/imu/data`).
2. **Services** — synchronous request/response RPCs (e.g., `/gazebo/spawn_urdf_model`).
3. **Parameters** — configuration values read from the ROS Parameter Server at launch time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This architecture is widely adopted in academic and industrial robotics. Companies like **Boston Dynamics**, **NVIDIA (Isaac Sim)**, and research labs worldwide use the sim-to-real paradigm to iterate safely, inexpensively, and rapidly before risking expensive hardware.

## 2.4 URDF vs SDF: The Format Decision

| Property | URDF (Unified Robot Description Format) | SDF (Simulation Description Format) |
|---|---|---|
| Native to | ROS / `robot_state_publisher` | Gazebo |
| Robot only? | Yes — single robot per file | No — full **world** (multi-robot, lights, ground plane) |
| Closed kinematic chains | Not supported | Supported |
| Sensor plugins | Defined via `<gazebo>` tags | Native `<sensor>` block |
| Versioning | None | Versioned (`sdf` root attribute) |
| Conversion | Cannot natively include worlds | Recommended for **complete simulation scenes** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When a question asks about *loading a robot into Gazebo*, examiners expect you to mention **URDF → spawned by `spawn_model`**, while *creating a complete simulation world* requires **SDF**.

## 2.5 The `ros_control` Loop

The integration between ROS controllers and Gazebo joints follows a strict **feedback loop**:

$$\text{Controller Command} \rightarrow \text{Controller Manager} \rightarrow \text{Transmission Interface} \rightarrow \text{Hardware Interface} \rightarrow \text{ Gazebo Joint}$$

The Gazebo plugin `gazebo_ros_control` then **reads back** the joint state and publishes it to `/joint_states` at a configurable rate (typically **100 Hz**).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivation, Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Study of Integration Methods

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix is critical for answering KTU comparison questions (frequently asked for **3 to 7 marks**).

| Method | Communication Pattern | Latency | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| `gazebo_ros` topic plugins | Publish/Subscribe (asynchronous) | Low (~1–5 ms) | Sensor data streaming |
| `gazebo_ros` services | Request/Response (synchronous) | Moderate | Spawning models, setting states |
| `gazebo_ros_control` | Real-time control loop | Very low (~1 ms) | Joint actuation, PID control |
| `ignition_ros2_control` (ROS 2) | Lifecycle-aware control | Very low | ROS 2 Humble and beyond |
| Direct `gz topic` (new Gazebo) | Native transport (ZeroMQ) | Lowest | Gazebo-native debugging |

## 3.2 Exhaustive Walkthrough: Launching a Robot in Gazebo with ROS

This is the **canonical experiment** aligned with Module Experiment 2 of PCRUL508. We will trace every algebraic and logical transition.

### Step 1: Verify Installation and Workspace Setup

The system must satisfy the dependency chain:

$$ \text{ROS Noetic/Melodic} \;\cap\; \text{Gazebo (matching version)} \;\cap\; \text{gazebo\_ros\_pkgs} $$

For ROS Noetic, the matching Gazebo version is **Gazebo 11**. We verify:

```bash
rosversion -d          # outputs: noetic
gazebo --version       # outputs: 11.x.x
```

**Logic:** Mismatched versions cause plugin loading errors such as `libgazebo_ros_camera.so: cannot open shared object file`. Always cross-check using the official ROS-Gazebo compatibility matrix.

### Step 2: Source the Workspace

```bash
source /opt/ros/noetic/setup.bash
source ~/catkin_ws/devel/setup.bash
```

**Logic:** This appends the workspace's `lib/` and `share/` directories to `$ROS_PACKAGE_PATH`, allowing Gazebo to locate the `gazebo_ros` launch files.

### Step 3: Launch Gazebo with an Empty World

The `roslaunch` command invokes the Gazebo server (physics) and client (rendering) as background processes:

```bash
roslaunch gazebo_ros empty_world.launch
```

**Under the hood**, this triggers the following process tree:

$$\text{roslaunch} \rightarrow \text{roscore (master)} \rightarrow \text{gzserver} \rightarrow \text{gzclient}$$

### Step 4: Spawn a URDF Model

The URDF (in **XML**) describes kinematic and dynamic properties. The spawn tool converts it to SDF internally:

```bash
rosrun gazebo_ros spawn_model -file ~/catkin_ws/src/my_robot/urdf/my_robot.urdf \
                              -urdf -model my_robot \
                              -x 0 -y 0 -z 0.5
```

**Logic of arguments:**
- `-urdf` flag tells `spawn_model` to parse the file as URDF.
- `-x, -y, -z` specify the initial pose offset (here, lifted **0.5 m** above ground to avoid initial penetration).
- The tool publishes to `/gazebo/spawn_urdf_model` service.

### Step 5: Verify Topics and Nodes

After spawning, the system publishes the topics:

```
/gazebo/model_states
/gazebo/link_states
/joint_states
/camera/image_raw
/laser/scan
/imu/data
```

**Logic:** Each `<gazebo>` plugin in the URDF declares a topic name. The Gazebo plugin subscribes internally to physics updates and republishes them on these ROS topics using `advertise()` and `publish()`.

### Step 6: Send Velocity Commands

For a mobile robot, control is typically issued via `cmd_vel`:

```bash
rostopic pub -1 /cmd_vel geometry_msgs/Twist \
             '{linear: {x: 0.5, y: 0.0, z: 0.0}, angular: {x: 0.0, y: 0.0, z: 0.3}}'
```

**The underlying physics update** for a differential drive robot is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2} \\
\omega &= \omega_z \\
v_{\text{left}} &= v - \frac{\omega \cdot L}{2} \\
v_{\text{right}} &= v + \frac{\omega \cdot L}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $L$ is the wheelbase (distance between left and right wheels). These wheel velocities are applied to the two drive joints, and the physics solver updates the robot's pose in the next time step.

## 3.3 Mathematical Model: Physics Update Step

Gazebo integrates the equations of motion using **semi-implicit Euler** by default:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{t+1} &= v_t + a_t \cdot \Delta t \\
p_{t+1} &= p_t + v_{t+1} \cdot \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $v_t$ = velocity at time $t$ (vector of size 6 for a 3D rigid body — 3 linear, 3 angular)
- $a_t$ = acceleration (sum of gravity, applied forces, contact forces)
- $p_t$ = pose (position + orientation quaternion)
- $\Delta t$ = simulation step size (default **0.001 s**)

The total **real-time factor (RTF)** is governed by:

$$
\text{RTF} = \frac{\Delta t_{\text{simulated}}}{\Delta t_{\text{wall clock}}}
$$

When $\text{RTF} = 1.0$, the simulation runs in real time. If computation is too slow, $\text{RTF} < 1.0$ (slowed-down simulation).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Gazebo High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph USERSPACE["USER SPACE LAYER"]
        U1[ROS Node 1]
        U2[ROS Node 2]
        U3[RViz Visualization]
    end

    subgraph MIDDLEWARE["ROS MIDDLEWARE LAYER"]
        M1[ROS Master]
        M2[Topic Bus]
        M3[Parameter Server]
        M4[Service Server]
    end

    subgraph BRIDGE["GAZEBO ROS BRIDGE LAYER"]
        B1[gazebo_ros_pkgs]
        B2[Plugin Loader]
        B3[ros_control Interface]
    end

    subgraph GAZEBOCORE["GAZEBO CORE LAYER"]
        G1[Physics Engine ODE]
        G2[Sensor Module]
        G3[Rendering Engine OGRE]
        G4[Scene Description SDF]
    end

    U1 --> M2
    U2 --> M2
    U3 --> M2
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> B1
    M3 --> B1
    M4 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> G1
    B2 --> G2
    B3 --> G1
    G4 --> G1
    G1 --> G2
    G2 --> B2
    G3 --> G2
```

**Interpretation:** Data flows downward from ROS nodes to the physics engine for actuation, and upward from sensors back to ROS topics.

## 4.2 ROS-Gazebo Integration Flow (Launch to Control)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1: Start roscore] --> B[Step 2: Launch Gazebo empty world]
    B --> C[Step 3: Publish robot description to robot_state_publisher]
    C --> D[Step 4: Spawn URDF via spawn_model service]
    D --> E[Step 5: Gazebo loads plugins and initializes sensors]
    E --> F[Step 6: Topics become active on ROS network]
    F --> G[Step 7: User nodes subscribe and publish]
    G --> H[Step 8: ros_control issues joint commands]
    H --> I[Step 9: Gazebo updates physics and sensors]
    I --> F
```

## 4.3 Sensor Data Pipeline Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSOR["SENSOR SIMULATION"]
        S1[Virtual Camera Plugin]
        S2[Virtual LIDAR Plugin]
        S3[Virtual IMU Plugin]
    end

    subgraph TRANSPORT["TRANSPORT LAYER"]
        T1[Camera Image Topic]
        T2[LaserScan Topic]
        T3[IMU Data Topic]
    end

    subgraph CONSUMER["CONSUMER NODES"]
        C1[Image Processing Node]
        C2[SLAM Node]
        C3[State Estimation Node]
    end

    S1 --> T1 --> C1
    S2 --> T2 --> C2
    S3 --> T3 --> C3
```

## 4.4 Component-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUTS["INPUTS"]
        I1[URDF or SDF File]
        I2[Launch File XML]
        I3[YAML Configuration]
    end

    subgraph PROCESSING["PROCESSING PIPELINE"]
        P1[Parser]
        P2[Model Loader]
        P3[Plugin Manager]
        P4[Physics Solver]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS["OUTPUTS"]
        O1[ROS Topics]
        O2[TF Transforms]
        O3[Sensor Data Streams]
        O4[Visual Rendering]
    end

    I1 --> P1 --> P2
    I2 --> P3
    I3 --> P3
    P2 --> P4
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> O1
    P4 --> O2
    P4 --> O3
    P4 --> O4
```
