### 4.2 Motion Planning Execution Sequence (Temporal Flow)

The following sequence diagram shows the **chronological message exchange** during a single `plan + execute` call.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Client
    participant MG as Move Group Node
    participant KP as Kinematics Plugin
    participant PL as OMPL Planner
    participant TP as Trajectory Processor
    participant CT as Joint Trajectory Controller
    participant R as Robot Gazebo

    U->>MG: MoveGroupAction Goal PoseStamped
    MG->>KP: Get IK Solver
    KP-->>MG: Joint Config q_goal
    MG->>PL: Plan request
    PL->>PL: RRTConnect Sampling
    PL-->>MG: RobotTrajectory N waypoints
    MG->>TP: Time Parameterization
    TP-->>MG: Smoothed JointTrajectory
    MG->>CT: ExecuteTrajectory Action
    CT->>R: JointTrajectory Command
    R-->>CT: Encoder Feedback 100Hz
    CT-->>MG: Execution Complete Result
    MG-->>U: Action Result Success
```

### 4.3 Configuration Space Obstacle Mapping (Conceptual)

This block diagram clarifies how Cartesian obstacles are abstracted into $\mathcal{C}$-space forbidden regions.

```mermaid

    D --> F[Valid Motion Plan]
    E --> G[Collision Detected]

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Cycle 2]**
> **Differentiate between Path Planning and Trajectory Planning in the context of ROS MoveIt!.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Path Planning (1 mark):** Path planning is a purely geometric process. It computes a sequence of collision-free configurations $\{q_0, q_1, ..., q_N\}$ from start to goal, with no consideration of time, velocity, or acceleration. Example: RRTConnect output.
- **Trajectory Planning (1 mark):** Trajectory planning takes the geometric path and assigns a time-stamp, velocity, and acceleration to each waypoint, producing a time-parameterized function $q(t)$. This ensures smooth, dynamically feasible motion. Example: Time-Optimal Trajectory Generation.
- **KTU distinction (1 mark):** Path planning answers *"where to move"*, while trajectory planning answers *"when and how fast to move"*. In MoveIt!, path planning is handled by OMPL, and trajectory planning is handled by the `TimeParameterization` stage.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
> **What is the role of the OMPL plugin in MoveIt! and name any two sampling-based algorithms it provides.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Role of OMPL (2 marks):** The **Open Motion Planning Library (OMPL)** is the default planning plugin in MoveIt!. It provides a unified interface to numerous sampling-based motion planning algorithms. OMPL operates on the robot's configuration space $\mathcal{C}$ and interfaces with the collision-checking module (FCL) to ensure every sampled configuration is collision-free.
- **Two algorithms (1 mark):** **RRT (Rapidly-exploring Random Tree)** and **RRTConnect** (Bidirectional RRT). Other valid answers: **PRM**, **KPIECE**, **LBKPIECE**, **SBL**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> ### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Regular Cycle 1]*

**Q.A (a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the MoveIt! motion planning framework in ROS. Identify the major nodes and their roles.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The MoveIt! architecture consists of **three primary layers** (3 marks for diagram + explanation):

1. **User Interface Layer (1 mark):** Consists of the **RViz Motion Planning Plugin** and C++/Python client APIs. Users can specify goal poses, visualize the planned path, and trigger execution.

2. **Move Group Node — Central Coordinator (3 marks):** This is the heart of MoveIt!. It integrates:
   - **Scene Monitor** — maintains the world representation using Octomap and collision objects.
   - **Kinematics Plugin** — solves FK/IK using KDL, Trac-IK, or IKFast.
   - **Planning Pipeline** — invokes OMPL planners (RRTConnect by default).
   - **Trajectory Processing** — applies time parameterization for smooth motion.

3. **Execution Layer (2 marks):** The `MoveItControllerManager` bridges to `ros_control`'s `joint_trajectory_controller`, which publishes `trajectory_msgs/JointTrajectory` at 100 Hz to the robot hardware (or Gazebo simulator). Feedback is received via `/joint_states` for closed-loop control.

The data flow is: **Sensors → Scene → Planner → Trajectory → Controller → Robot → Feedback**.

**Q.A (b) [7 Marks]** Derive the inverse kinematics iteration formula for a 6-DOF manipulator. Why is the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse used instead of the simple matrix inverse?

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Derivation (5 marks):**
Differentiating the forward kinematics $x = f_{FK}(q)$ with respect to time gives the velocity relation:

$$
\dot{x} = J(q) \dot{q}
$$

For a small step from $x_k$ toward $x_{goal}$, the error is $\Delta x = x_{goal} - x_k$. Inverting the relation:

$$
\Delta q = J^{-1}(q_k) \Delta x
$$

Since $J$ is generally **non-square** (for a 6-DOF arm, $J$ is $6 \times 6$ and square, but for redundant robots it is $m \times n$ with $m < n$), we use the **Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse** $J^{\dagger}$:

$$
q_{k+1} = q_k + \alpha \cdot J^{\dagger}(q_k) \cdot (x_{goal} - f_{FK}(q_k))
$$

where $\alpha \in (0, 1]$ is a step-size scalar for stability.

**Why pseudoinverse (2 marks):**
1. $J$ is often **singular** at certain configurations (singularities like wrist or elbow lock). The pseudoinverse gracefully handles this by providing a minimum-norm least-squares solution.
2. For **redundant manipulators** ($n > 6$), $J$ is non-square. Only the pseudoinverse can provide a valid joint-space solution.

---

> ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the RRTConnect algorithm. How does it improve upon the basic RRT algorithm? Mention the role of the step size $\epsilon$.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**RRT Algorithm Basics (2 marks):**
The basic RRT grows a single tree $\mathcal{T}$ from $q_{start}$. At each iteration:
- Sample $q_{rand} \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$.
- Find $q_{near} = \arg\min_{q \in \mathcal{T}} \vert\vert q - q_{rand} \vert\vert$.
- Extend by step $\epsilon$ toward $q_{rand}$.
- If the edge is collision-free, add $q_{new}$ to $\mathcal{T}$.

**RRTConnect Improvement (3 marks):**
RRTConnect grows **two trees simultaneously** — one from $q_{start}$ and one from $q_{goal}$. After every $k$ extensions, it attempts to **connect** the two trees. This bidirectional growth dramatically reduces the search space, achieving convergence in nearly half the time of vanilla RRT. It is the **default planner in MoveIt! tutorials** because of this speed advantage.

**Role of $\epsilon$ (2 marks):**
The step size $\epsilon$ controls the resolution of the tree:
- **Too small $\epsilon$:** Slow exploration, many iterations needed.
- **Too large $\epsilon$:** Risk of skipping narrow passages in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$, leading to planning failure.
- Typical industrial value: $\epsilon \approx 0.1$ rad for revolute joints.

**Q.B (b) [7 Marks]** Describe the role of `ros_control` and the `joint_trajectory_controller` in executing a planned trajectory. Why is a 100 Hz update rate important?

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**ros_control Architecture (3 marks):**
`ros_control` is the ROS hardware abstraction layer that decouples controllers from real robot hardware. It consists of:
- **Controller Manager:** Loads/unloads controllers.
- **Hardware Interface:** Communicates with motors (EtherCAT, CAN, serial).
- **Transmission Interface:** Maps actuator commands to joint commands.

**joint_trajectory_controller (2 marks):**
This controller accepts `trajectory_msgs/JointTrajectory` messages from MoveIt! and interpolates the waypoints using cubic/quintic splines. It samples the trajectory at the configured update rate and publishes position/velocity/effort commands to the hardware interface.

**Importance of 100 Hz (2 marks):**
A 100 Hz rate ($T_s = 10$ ms) ensures:
1. **Smooth tracking** — high-frequency correction of small tracking errors.
2. **Bounded latency** — actuator commands arrive before the next sensor feedback sample.
3. **PID stability** — the discrete-time PID has sufficient bandwidth to reject disturbances.

If the rate drops below ~30 Hz, oscillations and overshoot become visible in industrial arms; below 10 Hz, motion becomes jerky and unsafe.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing `move_group` with `ros_control`** — `move_group` plans; `ros_control` executes. Do not mix them.
> 2. **Forgetting units in formulas** — Always write $\epsilon$ in **radians** for revolute joints and **meters** for prismatic.
> 3. **Skipping the IK step in trajectory derivation** — A common 2-mark deduction in 14-mark questions.
> 4. **Not mentioning TF2** — The `tf2` system is critical for coordinate transforms between base_link, planning_frame, and end_effector.
> 5. **Writing RRT instead of RRTConnect** in MoveIt! viva — MoveIt! uses **RRTConnect** by default in tutorials, not RRT.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **MoveIt!** is the official motion planning framework for ROS, built on top of **OMPL** and **FCL**.
- [x] The **`move_group` node** is the central coordinator — it does not control motors, only plans.
- [x] **Path planning = geometry only**, **Trajectory planning = path + time parameterization**.
- [x] **RRTConnect** is the default sampling-based planner in MoveIt! — it is bidirectional.
- [x] **KDL** is the default kinematics solver; **Trac-IK** is faster; **IKFast** is analytical.
- [x] The **`joint_trajectory_controller`** runs at **100 Hz** ($f_c = 100$ Hz) for smooth closed-loop control.
- [x] Configuration space $\mathcal{C}$ partitions into $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ and \mathcal{C}_{obs}$ — the planner searches only in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$.
- [x] The **TF2 system** provides real-time coordinate transforms between `base_link`, `planning_frame`, and end-effector frames.
- [x] **Collision checking** is done by **FCL (Flexible Collision Library)** wrapped in MoveIt! as `collision_detection`.
- [x] **Octomap** provides 3D probabilistic occupancy grid for dynamic obstacle representation.
- [x] Standard launch command: `ros2 launch moveit2_tutorials demo.launch.py rviz_tutorial:=true`.
- [x] Trajectory messages use the type `trajectory_msgs/JointTrajectory` containing points, time_from_start, positions, velocities, accelerations.
- [x] For a **6-DOF arm**, $J$ is a $6 \times 6$ matrix; for redundant manipulators, the **pseudoinverse** $J^{\dagger} = (J^T J)^{-1} J^T$ is mandatory.
- [x] **Remember the KTU viva one-liner:** *"MoveIt! plans, ros_control executes, Gazebo simulates, and RViz visualizes."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of ROS Navigation Stack
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL508 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS) LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Overview of ROS Navigation Stack

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — ROS Navigation Stack**
> The **ROS Navigation Stack** is a collection of ROS packages (`move_base`, `costmap_2d`, `navfn`, `amcl`, `base_local_planner`, `dwa_local_planner`, `global_planner`, `robot_pose_ekf`, `map_server`, etc.) that collectively enable a mobile robot to autonomously plan and execute a collision-free trajectory from a current pose to a designated goal pose, given a static map, sensor data, and odometry.

The stack is built around the `move_base` meta-node, which orchestrates **Global Planning**, **Local Planning**, **Costmap Generation**, **Recovery Behavior Execution**, and **TF-based Sensor Fusion**. It is the *de-facto* standard framework in academic and industrial mobile robotics under ROS 1 (Noetic) and is ported to ROS 2 as **Nav2** in modern installations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Robot as an Autonomous Taxi"

Imagine you are in an **autonomous taxi** in an unfamiliar city:
- **You (the user)** enter a destination → this is the **Goal Pose** (`geometry_msgs/PoseStamped`).
- The **GPS + city map** gives a high-level route → this is the **Global Planner** (long-term, map-aware path).
- The **steering wheel and brake pedals** handle real-time obstacles (a child running across the road) → this is the **Local Planner** (short-term, reactive control).
- The **dashboard with proximity sensors** continuously maps the immediate surroundings → these are the **Costmaps** (obstacle grid layers).
- If the car gets stuck in a dead-end, the **driver executes a maneuver** (reverse, turn, abandon) → these are **Recovery Behaviors**.

The ROS Navigation Stack behaves **identically**: it takes a goal, consults a known map (global plan), and continuously re-plans locally using live laser/sensor data, falling back to recovery routines if progress stalls.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constraints (KTU Board Emphasis)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Constants / Defaults Used in KTU Lab Evaluation**
> - **Update Rate of Local Costmap:** **5 Hz** (typical: `controller_frequency = 5.0`)
> - **Global Planner Update Rate:** **1 Hz** (typical: `planner_frequency = 1.0`)
> - **Inflation Radius:** Default **0.55 m** around obstacles (`inflation_radius`)
> - **Robot Footprint:** Circle of **0.25 m** radius or polygon (`footprint`)
> - **Sensor Range Required:** LIDAR minimum **~5–10 m** for stable operation
> - **Coordinate Frame:** Goal published in `map` frame; odometry in `odom`; sensors in `base_link`/`base_laser`/`laser`
> - **Tolerance:** `xy_goal_tolerance = 0.20 m`, `yaw_goal_tolerance = 0.17 rad` (~10°)

### 1.4 Prerequisites Before You Can Run the Stack

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Holy Trinity" of Inputs Required by `move_base`:**
> 1. A **2D Occupancy Grid Map** (`.pgm` + `.yaml` published by `map_server`).
> 2. **TF Transforms** linking `map → odom → base_link → base_laser/sensors`.
> 3. **Sensor Streams** (`sensor_msgs/LaserScan` *or* `sensor_msgs/PointCloud2`) and **Odometry** (`nav_msgs/Odometry`).

If any one of these is missing, `move_base` will refuse to enter the `active` state and the terminal will print errors like `"Could not transform from base_laser to map"`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Decomposition of the `move_base` Node

The `move_base` node is a **hierarchical state machine** that interconnects five sub-systems. It subscribes to four critical topics and publishes velocity commands. Below is the structured logic flow.

- **Step 1 — Goal Reception:** Subscribes to `/move_base_simple/goal` (`geometry_msgs/PoseStamped`).
  - *Why:* To know *where* the robot must go. Goal is in the `map` frame.
- **Step 2 — Costmap Maintenance:** Maintains two layered costmaps (`local_costmap`, `global_costmap`).
  - *Why:* Translates raw sensor data and static map into a single grid where each cell holds a *cost* (0 = free, 254 = lethal, 255 = unknown).
- **Step 3 — Global Path Planning:** Calls the global planner (e.g., `navfn` or `global_planner`) to compute a path from start to goal.
  - *Why:* A coarse, *map-consistent* trajectory free of known obstacles.
- **Step 4 — Local Trajectory Generation:** The local planner (e.g., `base_local_planner` or `dwa_local_planner`) samples velocities, simulates trajectories, scores them, and picks the best.
  - *Why:* Real-time obstacle avoidance using live sensor data — this is *reactive control*.
- **Step 5 — Recovery Trigger:** If progress halts (no valid local trajectory), recovery behaviors fire in sequence: *clear costmaps → rotate 360° → clear costmaps again → abort*.
  - *Why:* To escape local minima (e.g., trapped in a U-shaped obstacle).
- **Step 6 — Velocity Publication:** Publishes `geometry_msgs/Twist` on `/cmd_vel` at `controller_frequency` Hz.
  - *Why:* The base controller (e.g., `diff_drive_controller`) consumes this to drive motors.

### 2.2 Costmap 2D — The Two-Layer Grid System

> [!NOTE]
> **Costmap Layers (added in order of priority):**
> 1. **Static Map Layer** — pre-loaded occupancy grid (`.pgm`).
> 2. **Obstacle Layer** — real-time sensor hits (laser/pointcloud).
> 3. **Inflation Layer** — exponentially decaying buffer around obstacles.
> 4. **Voxel Layer** *(optional)* — 3D obstacle tracking using point clouds.

Each cell's *cost* is computed by the **inflation layer** using the standard exponential decay formula.

### 2.3 The Dynamic Window Approach (DWA) — Local Planner Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The DWA, used by `dwa_local_planner`, restricts the search to a *reachable* velocity window within the robot's acceleration limits. The optimal velocity $(v_x^*, \omega_z^*)$ maximizes a multi-objective scoring function.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | Purpose |
|---|---------|----------------------|----------------------|---------|
| 1 | Inflation Layer Cost | $\text{cost}(d) = 255 \cdot e^{-W \cdot (d - r_{\text{inscribed}})}$ for $r_{\text{inscribed}} \le d \le r_{\text{inflation}}$ | $W = 10$ (decay weight) | Penalty grows as robot approaches obstacle |
| 2 | DWA Score | $S(v,\omega) = \alpha \cdot \text{heading}(v,\omega) + \beta \cdot \text{dist}(v,\omega) + \gamma \cdot \text{vel}(v,\omega)$ | $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ weights | Picks best trajectory |
| 3 | Reachable Velocity Window | $V_d = \{(v,\omega) \mid v \in [v_a - \dot v \cdot \Delta t,\; v_a + \dot v \cdot \Delta t]\}$ | $\Delta t = 0.5$ s | Limits search to physically reachable set |
| 4 | Admissible Velocity | $V_a = \{(v,\omega) \mid v \le \sqrt{2 \cdot \text{dist}(v,\omega) \cdot \dot v_{\text{brake}}\} \land \omega \le \sqrt{2 \cdot \text{dist}(v,\omega) \cdot \dot \omega_{\text{brake}}\}$ | — | Ensures robot can stop before collision |
| 5 | Sim Time | $T_{\text{sim}}$ | $1.7$ s (default) | Forward simulation horizon |
| 6 | Goal Tolerance | $\Delta_{xy}, \Delta_{\theta}$ | $0.20$ m, $0.17$ rad | Stopping criteria for goal |
| 7 | Costmap Resolution | $r_{\text{cm}}$ | $0.05$ m | Grid cell size (5 cm) |
| 8 | Update Frequency | $f_{\text{local}}, f_{\text{global}}$ | $5$ Hz, $1$ Hz | Plan/control refresh rate |
| 9 | Recovery Count | $N_{\text{recovery}}$ | $3$ (default `recovery_behavior_enabled`) | Number of retries before abort |
| 10 | Footprint Radius | $r_{\text{foot}}$ | $0.25$ m (typical) | Robot inscribed circle |

> **Engineering Utility:** The Navigation Stack is the *workhorse* behind warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva), hospital delivery bots, autonomous vacuum cleaners (Roomba descendants), and agricultural UGVs. Understanding the DWA scoring weights is essential when a robot oscillates or collides — re-tuning $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$ is the standard mitigation.

### 2.5 TF Tree — The Hidden Backbone

> [!CAUTION]
> If your **TF tree** is broken, the navigation stack will *silently* fail. You must publish at minimum: `map → odom → base_link → base_laser` (or `laser`, `base_scan`).

The `map → odom` transform is published by the **localization node** (e.g., `amcl` for known maps, or `slam_gmapping` for online mapping). The `odom → base_link` transform is published by the **odometry source** (wheel encoders, `robot_pose_ekf`, or `robot_localization` package).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Inflation Layer Cost Function

The inflation layer assigns a *cost* to every grid cell within the `inflation_radius`. The derivation proceeds from the requirement that the cost must be **continuous**, **monotonically decreasing** with distance, and **lethal at the inscribed radius**.

We start by defining the **inscribed radius** $r_{\text{inscribed}}$ — the radius of the smallest circle that fully contains the robot's footprint:

$$r_{\text{inscribed}} = \max_{(x,y) \in \text{footprint}} \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$$

For a circular robot of radius $r$, this simplifies to $r_{\text{inscribed}} = r$.

**Step 1 — Define the lethal boundary.** Any cell at distance $d < r_{\text{inscribed}}$ from an obstacle is lethal and is assigned a cost of $254$ (or marked as obstacle):

$$\text{cost}(d) = 254, \quad d < r_{\text{inscribed}}$$

**Step 2 — Define the inflation boundary.** Cells at distance $d \ge r_{\text{inflation}}$ are unaffected and retain the cost of the static map:

$$\text{cost}(d) = \text{static\_cost}, \quad d \ge r_{\text{inflation}}$$

**Step 3 — Exponential decay in the inflation region.** Between $r_{\text{inscribed}}$ and $r_{\text{inflation}}$, the cost decays exponentially from $254$ to $0$ as $d$ increases:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{cost}(d) &= 255 \cdot e^{-W \cdot (d - r_{\text{inscribed}})} \quad \text{for} \; r_{\text{inscribed}} \le d \le r_{\text{inflation}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Here, $W$ is the *cost scaling factor*. The value $255$ is chosen because ROS costmap uses unsigned 8-bit integers (range $0$–$255$) for the cost field.

**Step 4 — Solve for the decay weight $W$.** We impose the boundary condition that at $d = r_{\text{inflation}}$, the cost should be approximately $0$ (i.e., the user wants the inflation to *just* reach the outer boundary):

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= 255 \cdot e^{-W \cdot (r_{\text{inflation}} - r_{\text{inscribed}})} \\
\Rightarrow \quad e^{-W \cdot \Delta r} &= \frac{0}{255} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

In practice, this cannot be satisfied exactly with a finite $W$, so the convention is to set $W$ to a fixed default of **$10$** when the parameter is not specified. This is a **design heuristic**, not a derived value.

**Step 5 — Numerical example.** Let $r_{\text{inscribed}} = 0.25$ m, $r_{\text{inflation}} = 0.55$ m, $W = 10$. Compute the cost at $d = 0.35$ m (a point $0.10$ m outside the inscribed radius):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{cost}(0.35) &= 255 \cdot e^{-10 \cdot (0.35 - 0.25)} \\
&= 255 \cdot e^{-10 \cdot 0.10} \\
&= 255 \cdot e^{-1.0} \\
&= 255 \cdot 0.3679 \\
&\approx 93.81
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, a cell $10$ cm outside the robot's footprint carries a cost of $\approx 94$, which is treated as a *soft* obstacle that the local planner will avoid. **This exponential decay is what gives the local planner a smooth gradient to follow**, rather than a binary wall.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Navigation Stack Configuration Parameters

Below is a step-by-step decision matrix for a student configuring the navigation stack in `move_base.launch` and the corresponding `*.yaml` files.

| Step | Decision Point | File to Edit | Parameter | Recommended Value (TurtleBot 2) | Effect if Wrong |
|------|---------------|--------------|-----------|---------------------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Robot base type | `costmap_common_params.yaml` | `robot_base_frame` | `base_footprint` | TF errors |
| 2 | Robot footprint | `costmap_common_params.yaml` | `footprint` | `[[-0.205, -0.205], [0.205, -0.205], [0.205, 0.205], [-0.205, 0.205]]` | Collisions |
| 3 | Inflation radius | `costmap_common_params.yaml` | `inflation_radius` | `0.55` | Planner gets stuck |
| 4 | Local planner type | `move_base.launch` | `base_local_planner` | `TrajectoryPlannerROS` or `DWAPlannerROS` | No motion |
| 5 | Global planner type | `move_base.launch` | `base_global_planner` | `GlobalPlanner` or `NavfnROS` | Path not generated |
| 6 | Map server | `map_server.launch` | `map` arg | `$(find my_pkg)/maps/room.yaml` | Goal rejected |
| 7 | Goal tolerance | `dwa_local_planner_params.yaml` | `xy_goal_tolerance` | `0.20` | Robot never reaches goal |
| 8 | Forward sim time | `dwa_local_planner_params.yaml` | `sim_time` | `1.7` | Overshoots / gets stuck |
| 9 | Recovery count | `move_base.launch` | `recovery_behaviors` | `[{name: conservative_reset}, {name: rotate_recovery}, {name: aggressive_reset}]` | Spins forever |
| 10 | TF timeout | `costmap_common_params.yaml` | `transform_tolerance` | `0.5` | Costmap "stale" warnings |

### 3.3 Verification of DWA Velocity Window

The Dynamic Window Approach restricts the search to velocities the robot can *actually reach* in one control cycle. We verify this analytically.

**Given:** $v_a = 0.0$ m/s (current linear velocity), $\dot v = 0.5$ m/s², $\Delta t = 0.5$ s.

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{\min}^{\text{reachable}} &= v_a - \dot v \cdot \Delta t = 0.0 - 0.5 \cdot 0.5 = -0.25 \text{ m/s} \\
v_{\max}^{\text{reachable}} &= v_a + \dot v \cdot \Delta t = 0.0 + 0.5 \cdot 0.5 = +0.25 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, the *reachable* linear velocity window is $[-0.25, +0.25]$ m/s in a single timestep. The DWA further filters this to the **admissible set** (velocities that allow safe stopping) and the **dynamic window** itself.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** If a student can show the DWA computation for a specific velocity pair $(v, \omega)$ and explain *why* it is rejected or accepted, they have demonstrated deep understanding — this is a high-value 7-mark answer in KTU ESE.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Navigation Stack Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram shows how the **five sub-systems** of `move_base` interact with the **sensors**, **map**, and **robot base**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User / RViz Goal Tool] -->|geometry_msgs PoseStamped| B[move_base Node]
    C[Map Server: map_server] -->|nav_msgs OccupancyGrid| B
    D[Localization: amcl or gmapping] -->|TF map to odom| B
    E[Odometry Source: wheel encoders / EKF] -->|nav_msgs Odometry + TF odom to base_link| B
    F[Laser Scanner: LIDAR] -->|sensor_msgs LaserScan| B
    G[Point Cloud Camera optional] -->|sensor_msgs PointCloud2| B
    B --> H[Global Costmap: 2D layered grid]
    B --> I[Local Costmap: rolling window]
    H --> J[Global Planner: navfn or global_planner]
    I --> K[Local Planner: DWA or TrajectoryPlanner]
    J -->|nav_msgs Path| B
    K -->|trajectory scores| B
    B --> L[Recovery Behaviors: rotate reset clear]
    L --> B
    B -->|geometry_msgs Twist cmd_vel| M[Robot Base Controller]
    M --> N[Mobile Robot Wheels]
    N -->|encoder ticks| E
```

**Reading the diagram:** The `move_base` node (B) is the *central hub*. All inputs converge on it; it orchestrates costmap updates (H, I), planning (J, K), and recovery (L), and outputs velocity commands (M).

### 4.2 TF Tree Required by the Navigation Stack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[map] -->|published by amcl or gmapping| B[odom]
    B -->|published by odometry source| C[base_link]
    C --> D[base_laser or laser]
    C --> E[base_footprint]
    C --> F[wheel_left_link]
    C --> G[wheel_right_link]
    C --> H[camera_link optional]
    C --> I[imu_link optional]
```

**Critical transform:** `map → odom` (slowly corrected by localization). `odom → base_link` (fast, from wheel encoders). The robot pose in the `map` frame is what global planning uses.

### 4.3 Costmap Layer Stack — How Obstacles Become Costs

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1: Static Map Layer] --> L2[Layer 2: Obstacle Layer]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Inflation Layer]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Voxel Layer optional]
    L4 --> OUT[Final Costmap Output for Planner]
    S1[OccupancyGrid .pgm] --> L1
    S2[LaserScan] --> L2
    S3[PointCloud2] --> L4
    F1[footprint] --> L3
    P1[inflation_radius 0.55 m] --> L3
    P2[decay weight W 10] --> L3
```

### 4.4 Recovery Behavior State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Planning
    Planning --> Executing: Valid path + trajectory
    Executing --> Planning: Reached intermediate waypoint
    Executing --> Recovery1: Stuck or invalid trajectory
    Recovery1 --> Planning: Costmap cleared
    Recovery1 --> Recovery2: Still stuck
    Recovery2 --> Planning: 360 degree rotation done
    Recovery2 --> Recovery3: Still stuck
    Recovery3 --> Planning: Aggressive reset
    Recovery3 --> Aborted: After 3 failed cycles
    Aborted --> [*]
```

This is the **recovery state machine** built into `move_base`. The default sequence is: *clear costmaps → rotate 360° → clear costmaps → abort*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** What is the role of the `move_base` node in the ROS Navigation Stack? List any two planners that can be plugged into it. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The `move_base` node is the central coordinator of the ROS Navigation Stack. It receives a goal pose in the `map` frame, maintains global and local costmaps, invokes a global planner to compute a static obstacle-free path, and a local planner to generate real-time velocity commands (`/cmd_vel`) while avoiding dynamic obstacles. It also handles recovery behaviors if the robot gets stuck.

Two pluggable planners are: **(1) Global Planners:** `navfn`, `global_planner` (A*) **(2) Local Planners:** `base_local_planner` (Trajectory Rollout), `dwa_local_planner` (DWA).

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between a global costmap and a local costmap. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Global Costmap | Local Costmap |
|--------|---------------|---------------|
| Coverage | Entire static map (`.pgm`) | Rolling window around the robot |
| Update Rate | Lower (1 Hz) | Higher (5 Hz) |
| Source | Static map + sensor data | Sensor data + footprint |
| Purpose | Long-term path planning | Short-term reactive control |
| Inflation Radius | Larger (e.g., 0.55 m) | Smaller (e.g., 0.30 m) |
| Frame | `map` frame | `odom` frame (typically) |

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Question A (14 marks):** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the ROS Navigation Stack. Discuss the roles of global planner, local planner, costmaps, and recovery behaviors. List the TF transforms required.

**Model Solution (Part (a) — 7 marks — Block Diagram & Architecture):**
The ROS Navigation Stack is organized around the `move_base` node, which integrates five subsystems:
1. **Goal Reception** — via `/move_base_simple/goal`.
2. **Global Costmap** — layered grid covering the entire map.
3. **Global Planner** (`navfn`/`global_planner`) — produces `nav_msgs/Path` from start to goal using Dijkstra or A* algorithm.
4. **Local Costmap** — rolling window updated from live LIDAR data.
5. **Local Planner** (`dwa_local_planner`) — uses Dynamic Window Approach to compute velocity commands at 5 Hz.
6. **Recovery Behaviors** — triggered if no valid trajectory is found.

**Model Solution (Part (b) — 7 marks — TF & Inputs Required):**
TF transforms required: `map → odom → base_link → base_laser`. The `map → odom` transform is published by `amcl` (known map) or `gmapping` (online). The `odom → base_link` is published by the odometry source. The `base_link → base_laser` is static, published by the URDF. Required inputs: occupancy grid map, laser scans, odometry, and goal pose.

> **Question B (14 marks — Alternative Choice):** Explain the Dynamic Window Approach (DWA) used by the local planner. Derive the scoring function and the reachable velocity window. Mention its advantages and limitations.

**Model Solution (Part (a) — 7 marks — Concept & Derivation):**
The Dynamic Window Approach (DWA) restricts the velocity search space to those velocities the robot can *actually reach* in one timestep, given its acceleration limits. The reachable set $V_d$ is defined as:

$$V_d = \{(v,\omega) \mid v \in [v_a - \dot v \Delta t,\; v_a + \dot v \Delta t] \;\land\; \omega \in [\omega_a - \dot \omega \Delta t,\; \omega_a + \dot \omega \Delta t]\}$$

The admissible set $V_a$ further restricts to velocities that allow safe stopping. The scoring function combines three weighted terms:

$$S(v,\omega) = \alpha \cdot \text{heading}(v,\omega) + \beta \cdot \text{dist}(v,\omega) + \gamma \cdot \text{vel}(v,\omega)$$

where `heading` measures alignment with the global path, `dist` is clearance to obstacles, and `vel` rewards forward velocity.

**Model Solution (Part (b) — 7 marks — Advantages & Limitations):**
**Advantages:** Real-time (5 Hz), low computational cost, reactive to dynamic obstacles, smooth trajectories.
**Limitations:** Suffers from the *kinodynamic* constraint, can get trapped in U-shaped obstacles (solved by recovery behaviors), does not perform global re-planning within local control, and can fail in highly dynamic multi-agent scenarios.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Missing the TF tree explanation** — students often forget to list `map → odom → base_link → base_laser`. This is worth **2–3 marks** in any 14-mark question.
> 2. **Confusing global and local planner roles** — global = static map; local = live sensor data. Drawing the wrong connection loses marks.
> 3. **Forgetting units** in the inflation formula (write **meters** explicitly). Examiners deduct **0.5 marks** per missing unit.
> 4. **Not drawing the block diagram** — in 14-mark questions, the diagram itself carries **3–4 marks**. Always draw it, even if rough.
> 5. **Stating the DWA formula without explaining each term** — list `heading`, `dist`, `vel` explicitly with their physical meaning.
> 6. **Confusing ROS 1 and ROS 2 terminology** — the lab course uses ROS 1 (Noetic). Nav2 is ROS 2 and should not be substituted unless the question specifies it.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Bullet List — "Read This Before the Exam"**
> - **Central Node:** `move_base` orchestrates everything.
> - **Three Required Inputs:** Occupancy Grid Map + TF Tree + Sensor/Odom Streams.
> - **TF Tree:** `map → odom → base_link → base_laser`. The `map → odom` link is *corrective* (from localization); `odom → base_link` is *continuous* (from encoders).
> - **Two Costmaps:** Global (whole map, slow update) + Local (rolling window, fast update).
> - **Costmap Layers (in order):** Static Map → Obstacle → Inflation → Voxel.
> - **Inflation Cost Formula:** $\text{cost}(d) = 255 \cdot e^{-W(d - r_{\text{inscribed}})}$ for $r_{\text{inscribed}} \le d \le r_{\text{inflation}}$; default $W = 10$, $r_{\text{inscribed}} = 0.25$ m, $r_{\text{inflation}} = 0.55$ m.
> - **Global Planners:** `navfn` (Dijkstra/NavFn), `global_planner` (A*, Dijkstra variants).
> - **Local Planners:** `base_local_planner` (Trajectory Rollout), `dwa_local_planner` (Dynamic Window Approach).
> - **DWA Velocity Window:** $V_d$ = reachable, $V_a$ = admissible, $V_s$ = sampling; intersection gives final search space.
> - **DWA Score:** $S = \alpha \cdot \text{heading} + \beta \cdot \text{dist} + \gamma \cdot \text{vel}$. Default weights: $\alpha = 1.0$, $\beta = 0.2$, $\gamma = 0.2$ (configurable).
> - **Recovery Behaviors (default sequence):** clear costmaps → rotate 360° → clear costmaps → aggressive reset → abort.
> - **Controller Frequency:** 5 Hz (typical). Planner Frequency: 1 Hz.
> - **Goal Tolerance:** $\Delta_{xy} = 0.20$ m, $\Delta_{\theta} = 0.17$ rad.
> - **Output Topic:** `/cmd_vel` (`geometry_msgs/Twist`).
> - **Key Launch Files:** `move_base.launch`, `amcl.launch`, `map_server.launch`, `robot.launch` (URDF + TF).
> - **Key Config Files:** `costmap_common_params.yaml`, `local_costmap_params.yaml`, `global_costmap_params.yaml`, `dwa_local_planner_params.yaml`, `global_planner_params.yaml`, `amcl.launch` (parameters).
> - **Common Errors to Debug:** (i) "Could not transform from base_laser to map" → fix TF tree; (ii) "Costmap2DROS transform timeout" → increase `transform_tolerance`; (iii) Robot oscillates → reduce DWA `sim_time` or increase $\beta$ (obstacle weight); (iv) Robot gets stuck in U-shape → increase `clearing_rotation_allowed` or tune recovery.
> - **ROS 2 Equivalent:** Nav2 stack uses `bt_navigator`, `nav2_planner`, `nav2_controller`, `nav2_recoveries` (Behavior Trees replace the rigid state machine).
> - **Standard Lab Packages:** `turtlebot_navigation`, `turtlebot_gazebo`, `amcl`, `map_server`, `move_base` — all are first-party packages in `ros-noetic-desktop-full`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Localization and Mapping with SLAM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL508 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS) LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Localization and Mapping with SLAM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)** is a fundamental computational problem in autonomous robotics that seeks to construct a spatial map of an unknown environment while concurrently estimating the robot's pose (position and orientation) within that very map being built. Formally, SLAM solves the *chicken-and-egg* problem where:

- The robot needs a map to localize itself.
- The robot needs its pose to build the map.

Mathematically, SLAM estimates the joint posterior probability density over both the robot trajectory $x_{1:t}$ and the map $m$ given all sensor measurements $z_{1:t}$ and control inputs $u_{1:t}$:

$$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

This is the **SLAM posterior** — the central object computed by every SLAM algorithm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCRUL508 — Experiment 2):** Students must demonstrate SLAM using ROS, with packages such as `gmapping`, `hector_slam`, or `cartographer`, on either a simulated (Gazebo) or physical mobile robot platform. Key deliverables include building an occupancy grid map, observing the localization quality via RViz, and tuning core parameters (`map_resolution`, `linearUpdate`, `angularUpdate`, `minimumScore`).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **wearing a blindfold in a dark, unfamiliar house** and are handed a flashlight and a notebook.

- **Mapping (notebook):** Every time your flashlight hits a wall, you sketch that wall's rough position in your notebook. Step by step, a map of the house is being drawn.
- **Localization (sense of position):** To draw the next wall correctly, you must remember *where you are standing and which direction you are facing* in the house. But to know that, you need the partial map you just drew.
- **SLAM (the whole act):** You simultaneously update the notebook (map) and your mental awareness of your position (pose), using only the flashlight readings (sensor measurements) and your sense of steps taken (odometry).

The flashlight readings are noisy (sensors aren't perfect), and your step count drifts (odometry accumulates error) — yet you converge to a usable map and pose. **That is SLAM.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why SLAM is Hard (The Core Difficulty):**
> - **Sensor noise:** Every measurement has uncertainty.
> - **Odometry drift:** Wheel encoders accumulate error over time.
> - **Data association:** Recognizing that two sensor readings came from the *same* physical landmark.
> - **Computational complexity:** Maintaining a posterior over a growing map and trajectory is exponential in the naive case.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Map resolution | $r_{map}$ | **0.05 m** (5 cm) per cell | Granularity of occupancy grid |
| Linear update distance | $d_{lin}$ | **0.5 m** | Process scan after robot moves this far |
| Angular update | $\theta_{ang}$ | **0.2 rad** (~11.5°) | Process scan after robot rotates this much |
| Particle count (AMCL) | $N_{part}$ | **500–5000** | Number of pose hypotheses |
| Laser max range | $R_{max}$ | **30 m** | Maximum usable LiDAR measurement |
| ICP fitness threshold | $\epsilon_{ICP}$ | **0.2 m** | Scan-matching convergence threshold |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of the SLAM Problem

The joint SLAM posterior is intractable directly, so it is decomposed via the **Bayes filter** into two alternating steps:

**Step 1 — Motion Update (Prediction):** Propagating the prior pose belief through the kinematic/odometry model.

**Step 2 — Measurement Update (Correction):** Refining the predicted pose using the incoming sensor observation and the current map estimate.

**Step 3 — Map Augmentation:** Adding the new sensor sweep (or scan) to the map using the corrected pose.

### 2.2 Why and How — The Logic of Each Step

- **Why predict first?** The robot *acts* (moves) before it *senses* (takes a new scan). Motion is described by a probabilistic transition model $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ where $u_t$ is the odometry/control input.
- **Why correct second?** Sensor measurements carry independent information about the environment. By matching the predicted scan to the current map, we get a likelihood term $p(z_t \mid x_t, m_{t-1})$ that pushes the pose estimate toward observations consistent with the map.
- **Why update the map last?** Only after we have a *good* pose estimate should we fuse the new scan into the map, otherwise measurement noise gets baked into the geometry.

### 2.3 Mathematical Formulation

**Bayes Filter for SLAM (Continuous Form):**

$$\text{Bel}(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m_{t-1}) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, \text{Bel}(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

where $\eta$ is a normalization constant ensuring the posterior integrates to 1.

**Discrete Grid Map Update (Occupancy Grid):**

Each cell $m_i$ holds the log-odds occupancy value:

$$l(m_i \mid z_{1:t}) = l(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}) + l(m_i \mid z_t) - l_0$$

where $l_0 = \log\left(\frac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)}\right) = 0$ assuming a prior probability of 0.5.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Name | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | SLAM Posterior | $x_t$: pose, $m$: map | probability |
| 2 | $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ | Measurement Model | $z_t$: scan | probability |
| 3 | $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ | Motion Model | $u_t$: control | probability |
| 4 | $l(m_i) = \log\frac{p(m_i)}{1-p(m_i)}$ | Log-Odds | $p(m_i) \in [0,1]$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | $l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + \text{inv\_sensor}(z_t, x_t, m_i)$ | Occupancy Update | log-odds sum | dimensionless |
| 6 | $K_t = P_{t \vert t-1} H^T (H P_{t \vert t-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$ | Kalman Gain (EKF-SLAM) | $P$: cov., $H$: Jacob., $R$: meas. noise | mixed |
| 7 | $w_t^{[i]} = p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)$ | Particle Weight (FastSLAM) | $w$: weight | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\hat{x}_t = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_t^{[i]} x_t^{[i]}$ | Pose Estimate (PF) | $N$: particles | m, rad |
| 9 | $\epsilon = \sum_{j=1}^{M} \Vert z_{src}^{(j)} - z_{ref}^{(c_j)} \Vert^2$ | ICP Cost | $M$: point count | $m^2$ |
| 10 | $\text{Fitness} = \frac{\epsilon}{M}$ | ICP Fitness Score | — | $m^2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder on Notation:** Throughout these notes, vertical bars `|` for conditional probability (e.g., $p(a \mid b)$) are correctly typeset. In markdown tables, do **not** use raw `| |`; use the LaTeX `|x|` or `\vert x \vert` form only inside math blocks.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Self-Driving Cars):** Google/Waymo, Tesla FSD — multi-sensor SLAM with LiDAR + cameras.
- **Warehouse Robotics (AMR):** Amazon Kiva robots, Fetch Robotics — use 2D SLAM (`gmapping`) for navigation.
- **Underwater Exploration:** AUVs use sonar-based SLAM because GPS is unavailable.
- **AR/VR Headsets (Apple Vision Pro, Meta Quest 3):** Inside-out SLAM tracks the device in 3D space.
- **Domestic Vacuum Robots (Roomba, Xiaomi):** Low-cost 2D SLAM with single-beam LiDAR or depth camera.
- **Disaster Response Robots:** Search-and-rescue robots in collapsed buildings (DARPA Subterranean Challenge).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Bayes' Rule to the Occupancy Grid Log-Odds Update

**Goal:** Derive the standard recursive update equation used in `gmapping` and `cartographer` for occupancy grid maps.

**Step 1 — Bayes' Rule Applied to a Single Cell:**

We want the posterior probability that cell $m_i$ is occupied given all measurements up to time $t$:

$$p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \, p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t})}$$

**Step 2 — Introduce the Inverse Sensor Model:**

Define the *inverse sensor model* $p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)$, which returns the probability that cell $m_i$ is occupied given a single measurement $z_t$ and the current pose $x_t$. Substituting:

$$p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) \, p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(\neg m_i) \, p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1}) \, \frac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}}$$

This is a standard manipulation of Bayes' rule under the assumption of static environment (the measurement is conditionally independent given the cell state).

**Step 3 — Take the Log-Odds of Both Sides:**

Let $l_t(m_i) = \log\frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}$. Then:

$$l_t(m_i) = \log\frac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)} - \log\frac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)} + l_{t-1}(m_i)$$

**Step 4 — Identify Terms:**

Define $l_0 = \log\frac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)} = 0$ (since prior occupancy is 0.5). Then:

$$l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + \text{inv\_sensor}(z_t, x_t, m_i) - l_0$$

$$\boxed{l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + \text{inv\_sensor}(z_t, x_t, m_i)}$$

**Step 5 — Thresholding to Recover Probability:**

The final occupancy probability of cell $m_i$ is recovered by:

$$p(m_i) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{\,l_t(m_i)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Log-Odds?** The update becomes a simple *addition* instead of a multiplication, which is numerically stable (avoids underflow for long trajectories) and computationally cheap. This is why every production-grade 2D SLAM stack in ROS uses this representation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) SLAM Linearization

**Goal:** Show how EKF-SLAM linearizes the nonlinear motion and measurement models.

**Step 1 — Define the Augmented State Vector:**

$$\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} x_r \\ x_{m_1} \\ x_{m_2} \\ \vdots \\ x_{m_N} \end{bmatrix}$$

where $x_r = [x, y, \theta]^T$ is the robot pose and $x_{m_j} = [x_j, y_j]^T$ are landmark positions.

**Step 2 — Define Nonlinear Models:**

Motion: $x_r(t) = f(x_r(t-1), u_t) + w_t$ where $w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$.

Measurement: $z_t = h(X_t) + v_t$ where $v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$.

**Step 3 — Compute Jacobians at Current Estimate:**

$$F_X = \left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial X}\right|_{\hat{X}_{t-1}}, \quad H_t = \left.\frac{\partial h}{\partial X}\right|_{\hat{X}_{t \vert t-1}}$$

**Step 4 — Prediction Step:**

$$\hat{X}_{t \vert t-1} = f(\hat{X}_{t-1}, u_t)$$

$$P_{t \vert t-1} = F_X P_{t-1} F_X^T + Q_t$$

**Step 5 — Update Step (Compute Kalman Gain):**

$$K_t = P_{t \vert t-1} H_t^T (H_t P_{t \vert t-1} H_t^T + R_t)^{-1}$$

**Step 6 — Apply Correction:**

$$\hat{X}_t = \hat{X}_{t \vert t-1} + K_t(z_t - h(\hat{X}_{t \vert t-1}))$$

$$P_t = (I - K_t H_t) P_{t \vert t-1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Complexity Bottleneck:** EKF-SLAM maintains a state vector of size $(3 + 2N)$ where $N$ is the number of landmarks. The covariance matrix $P$ is $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$, and each update is $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$. This is why EKF-SLAM scales poorly beyond a few hundred landmarks.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: SLAM Algorithm Families

| Algorithm | Core Idea | Computational Cost | Robustness to Nonlinearity | Landmark Limit | ROS Package | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **EKF-SLAM** | Single Gaussian over (pose, map) | $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ | Low (linearization errors) | ~ few hundred | Custom / MRPT | Small maps, education |
| **FastSLAM (Particle Filter)** | Rao-Blackwellized: particles × EKF per landmark | $\mathcal{O}(M \log N)$ | High | Thousands | (Custom) | Unknown data association |
| **Graph-SLAM (Pose Graph)** | Optimize a graph of pose constraints | $\mathcal{O}(K^3)$ for $K$ nodes | Very High | Unlimited | `cartographer` | Large-scale 2D/3D |
| **Visual SLAM (ORB-SLAM3)** | Features + bundle adjustment | $\mathcal{O}(F^2)$ per frame | High | Unlimited | `ORB_SLAM3` (ROS 2) | Camera-only |
| **2D LiDAR Scan Matching (gmapping)** | Particle filter + scan-match proposal | $\mathcal{O}(M \cdot N_{part})$ | High | Grid only | `gmapping` | 2D indoor maps |

Where:
- $N$ = number of landmarks
- $M$ = number of particles
- $K$ = number of pose nodes in the graph
- $F$ = number of ORB features per frame

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: The SLAM Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Sensors: LiDAR / Camera / IMU / Wheel Odom] --> B[Sensor Preprocessing]
    B --> C[ROS Topic Publishers]
    C --> D[/scan /odom /imu/]
    D --> E[SLAM Core Node gmapping / cartographer]
    E --> F[Particle Filter or Scan Matcher]
    F --> G[Occupancy Grid Map Update]
    G --> H[Map Server Static Map Topic]
    H --> I[RViz Visualization]
    E --> J[AMCL Localization Node]
    J --> K[/amcl_pose Topic]
    K --> L[Navigation Stack move_base]
    L --> M[Velocity Commands to Robot Base]
    M --> A
    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style E fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style G fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style I fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style L fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The flow is *closed-loop*. Velocity commands close the loop by moving the robot, which produces new sensor data, which feeds back into the SLAM node. This is why SLAM is a **recursive Bayesian estimation** problem.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: SLAM Node Internal Architecture (`gmapping`)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[LaserScan Input] --> B[Scan Preprocessor]
    B --> C[Odometry Predict]
    C --> D[Particle Filter Proposal Distribution]
    D --> E[Scan Matching Per Particle ICP]
    E --> F[Weight Update via Map Likelihood]
    F --> G[Resampling if Neff below threshold]
    G --> H[Best Particle Pose Selection]
    H --> I[Occupancy Grid Update]
    I --> J[Map Topic Output]
    H --> K[Pose Topic Output]
    style D fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style E fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style I fill:#d62728,color:#fff
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: ROS Topics Exchanged During a SLAM Session

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant R as Robot Drivers
    participant G as gmapping Node
    participant M as map_server
    participant V as RViz
    R->>G: /scan (sensor_msgs/LaserScan)
    R->>G: /tf (odom to base_footprint)
    G->>G: Process scan + odom
    G->>V: /map (nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid)
    G->>V: /tf (map to odom)
    V->>V: Render map + robot pose
    V-->>R: Operator drives robot via teleop
    Note over G,M: Save: rosrun map_server map_saver -f mymap
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram: Robot SLAM Lifecycle States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Mapping: Launch gmapping
    Mapping --> Localizing: Save map and switch to AMCL
    Localizing --> Relocalizing: Kidnapped robot scenario
    Relocalizing --> Localizing: Pose recovered
    Localizing --> Idle: Shutdown
    Mapping --> Idle: Shutdown
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a State Diagram?** SLAM is not a single algorithm — it is a *family* of algorithms used at different robot lifecycle stages. Mapping is performed once to build the map; localization (AMCL) is used *every time* the robot is powered on to find its pose in the saved map.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model] (3 Marks)**
*Define SLAM. State any two major challenges in implementing SLAM for a mobile robot.*

**Model Answer:**

**Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)** is the computational problem of constructing a map of an unknown environment while simultaneously estimating the robot's pose within that map, formally expressed as estimating the joint posterior $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.

**Two major challenges:**

1. **Data Association:** Correctly matching newly observed landmarks to previously mapped ones despite sensor noise and perceptual aliasing (two distinct places looking identical).
2. **Computational Complexity:** Maintaining a consistent estimate of an ever-growing map; the joint posterior is exponential in the number of landmarks in the naive formulation.

*(A third valid answer: Odometry drift / sensor noise / loop closure detection.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model] (3 Marks)**
*What is the role of the AMCL package in ROS? How is it different from `gmapping`?*

**Model Answer:**

**AMCL (Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization)** is a ROS package that performs *2D localization of a robot in an existing map* using a particle filter. It does **not** build a new map; it estimates the robot's pose $(x, y, \theta)$ within a pre-loaded static map.

**Difference from `gmapping`:**

| Aspect | `gmapping` | `AMCL` |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Builds a map | Localizes within a known map |
| Map output | Yes (publishes `/map`) | No (consumes `/map`) |
| Algorithm | Rao-Blackwellized particle filter with scan-matching | Particle filter only |
| Use case | First-time exploration | Every subsequent mission |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** Derive the log-odds recursive update equation for an occupancy grid map used in 2D LiDAR SLAM. Clearly state all variables and assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of the `gmapping` ROS package, listing the input/output topics and key parameters. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Log-Odds Update (7 Marks):**

- State the SLAM posterior and the goal of estimating per-cell occupancy $p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$. **(1 Mark)**
- Apply Bayes' rule to express the posterior in terms of the prior and the likelihood from the current measurement $z_t$. **(1 Mark)**
- Define the inverse sensor model $p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)$ and state that the cell is updated only if the laser beam passes through or terminates in it. **(1 Mark)**
- Transform both sides into log-odds form: $l_t(m_i) = \log\frac{p}{1-p}$. **(1 Mark)**
- Identify the constant prior term $l_0 = 0$ for $p(m_i) = 0.5$. **(1 Mark)**
- Arrive at the final recursive update: $l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + \text{inv\_sensor}(z_t, x_t, m_i)$. **(1 Mark)**
- State the recovery equation $p(m_i) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{l_t(m_i)}}$ and explain the saturation bounds ($\pm l_{\max}$) to clamp the log-odds and prevent runaway accumulation. **(1 Mark)**

*(Full derivation is shown in Section 3.1 of these notes.)*

---

**(b) `gmapping` Functional Architecture (7 Marks):**

- **Inputs:** `/scan` (sensor_msgs/LaserScan), `/tf` (for `odom`→`base_footprint` transform). **(1 Mark)**
- **Outputs:** `/map` (nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid), `/tf` (for `map`→`odom` transform), `~entropy` (diagnostic). **(1 Mark)**
- **Internal stages** (refer to Section 4.2 diagram):
  - Odometry-based motion prediction. **(1 Mark)**
  - Particle filter proposal distribution. **(1 Mark)**
  - Per-particle scan matching (ICP-style). **(1 Mark)**
  - Weight update + adaptive resampling. **(1 Mark)**
  - Occupancy grid integration. **(1 Mark)**
- **Key tunable parameters:** `particles` (e.g., 30), `map_resolution` (0.05 m), `linearUpdate` (0.5 m), `angularUpdate` (0.2 rad), `minimumScore` (50), `loopSearchMaximumDistance`, `xmin/ymin/xmax/ymax` (map bounds). **(Awarded within the 1-Mark allocation above.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** Compare EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM, and Graph-SLAM with respect to algorithmic approach, computational complexity, and practical scalability. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the standard procedure to perform SLAM in ROS using the `gmapping` package on a simulated TurtleBot3 in Gazebo. Include the steps to save the generated map using `map_server`. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 Marks):**

- Present the table from Section 3.3 of these notes. **(3 Marks for the table itself)**
- **EKF-SLAM:** Single EKF over the augmented (pose + landmarks) state vector. Quadratic cost $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ in number of landmarks. Brittle to nonlinearities and data association failures. **(1 Mark)**
- **FastSLAM:** Particle filter where each particle carries $N$ independent low-dimensional EKFs (one per landmark). Effective cost $\mathcal{O}(M \log N)$. Better data association but resampling degeneracy. **(1 Mark)**
- **Graph-SLAM:** Models the SLAM problem as a sparse graph of pose constraints and optimizes it (e.g., with g2o, GTSAM). Cost $\mathcal{O}(K^3)$ where $K$ is pose count, but the sparsity keeps it tractable. Scales to city-scale maps (Cartographer). **(1 Mark)**
- Conclude with the recommendation: For KTU lab, use `gmapping` (FastSLAM family) for 2D indoor; use `cartographer` (Graph-SLAM) for larger or 3D environments. **(1 Mark)**

---

**(b) Procedure to Perform SLAM with TurtleBot3 in Gazebo (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Workspace Setup:** Create a catkin workspace, clone the `turtlebot3`, `turtlebot3_simulations`, `turtlebot3_slam`, and `slam_gmapping` packages into `src/`. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 2 — Launch Gazebo Simulation:**

```bash
export TURTLEBOT3_MODEL=burger
roslaunch turtlebot3_gazebo turtlebot3_world.launch
```

**(1 Mark)**

**Step 3 — Launch the SLAM Node:**

```bash
roslaunch turtlebot3_slam turtlebot3_slam.launch slam_methods:=gmapping
```

**(1 Mark)**

**Step 4 — Launch RViz for Visualization:**

```bash
rosrun rviz rviz -d $(rospack find turtlebot3_slam)/rviz/turtlebot3_slam.rviz
```

Add displays: `Map`, `RobotModel`, `TF`, `LaserScan`. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 5 — Teleoperate the Robot to Explore:**

```bash
roslaunch turtlebot3_teleop turtlebot3_teleop_key.launch
```

Drive slowly through the environment to build the map. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 6 — Save the Map:**

```bash
rosrun map_server map_saver -f ~/maps/turtlebot3_world
```

This produces two files: `turtlebot3_world.pgm` and `turtlebot3_world.yaml`. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 7 — (Optional) Switch to AMCL for Localization:** Load the saved map with `map_server` and launch `amcl.launch` for long-term use without rebuilding the map. **(1 Mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing AMCL with SLAM:** Students often credit AMCL as "performing SLAM." AMCL is a *localization-only* algorithm — it does **not** build maps. Valued at zero marks for any "map-building" question.
> 2. **Forgetting to publish `odom`→`base_footprint` TF:** `gmapping` will silently fail or produce a corrupted map if the robot state publisher is not running.
> 3. **Drifting without loop closure:** In `gmapping`, setting `loopSearchMaximumDistance = 0` disables loop closure, which is the *primary* mechanism for correcting accumulated odometry error.
> 4. **Confusing EKF-SLAM with EKF localization:** EKF localization uses a *fixed-size* state vector (pose only); EKF-SLAM uses a *growing* state vector (pose + landmarks) with quadratic covariance.
> 5. **Forgetting to set `map_update_interval` or `map_publish_interval`:** This causes the `/map` topic to flood, choking the network.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SLAM is the joint estimation of pose and map**, not a single algorithm but a *family* (EKF, FastSLAM, Graph-SLAM, Visual SLAM).
- The central mathematical object is the **SLAM posterior** $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.
- The **occupancy grid log-odds** representation turns map updates into fast, numerically stable additions: $l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + \text{inv\_sensor}(z_t, x_t, m_i)$.
- **EKF-SLAM** has $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ cost; **FastSLAM** has $\mathcal{O}(M \log N)$; **Graph-SLAM** exploits sparsity for very large maps.
- **ROS ecosystem:** `gmapping` (2D, FastSLAM family), `cartographer` (2D/3D, Graph-SLAM), `hector_slam` (odometry-free, scan matching only), `amcl` (localization only), `slam_toolbox` (lifelong mapping + localization).
- **Mandatory `/tf` tree:** `map` → `odom` → `base_footprint` → `laser/base_scan`. Missing transforms break SLAM silently.
- **Key parameters to tune:** `particles`, `map_resolution`, `linearUpdate`, `angularUpdate`, `minimumScore`, `loopSearchMaximumDistance`, `map_update_interval`.
- **Map save command:** `rosrun map_server map_saver -f <map_name>` produces `.pgm` + `.yaml` files.
- **AMCL ≠ SLAM:** AMCL is for localization within a pre-built map; do not claim it as a SLAM algorithm.
- **Loop closure** is what makes long-trajectory SLAM drift-free; it is the cornerstone of modern graph-based systems.
- **Sensor choice matters:** 2D LiDAR is the standard for KTU lab; depth cameras add richer 3D data at the cost of compute; wheel odometry alone is insufficient.
- **Standard exam shortcut:** If asked "Why is SLAM hard?", always answer with: (i) chicken-and-egg coupling, (ii) data association ambiguity, (iii) computational cost of joint posterior, (iv) perceptual aliasing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Path Planning and Obstacle Avoidance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL508 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS) LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Path Planning and Obstacle Avoidance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Path Planning** in the context of the Robot Operating System (ROS) is formally defined as the computational process of determining a continuous, collision-free trajectory for a mobile robot from an initial configuration (start pose) to a goal configuration (target pose) within a known, partially known, or unknown environment. In the ROS navigation stack (`move_base` framework), this task is decomposed into two hierarchical subtasks:

1. **Global Path Planning**: Computes a holistic route across a static, pre-loaded map using algorithms such as **A\***, **Dijkstra**, or **Carrot Planner**.
2. **Local Path Planning (Obstacle Avoidance)**: Performs real-time reactive trajectory generation using sensor feedback (laser scans, depth images) via algorithms like **Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)** or **Timed Elastic Band (TEB)**.

**Obstacle Avoidance** is the reactive sub-problem where the local planner continuously modulates the robot's linear and angular velocities $(v, \omega)$ to evade dynamic and unmodeled obstacles that are absent from the static global costmap.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course outcome (CO) for this experiment typically maps to **CO2: Implement path planning and obstacle avoidance algorithms using the ROS Navigation Stack on a simulated/real differential drive robot.** Students must demonstrate competency in launching `move_base`, configuring costmaps, and interpreting `nav_msgs/Path` topics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car from Kochi to Trivandrum using Google Maps:

- **Global Path Planner** is like Google Maps itself — it knows the entire highway network in advance and computes the **optimal long-distance route** (e.g., via NH66).
- **Local Planner / Obstacle Avoidance** is like your eyes, brain, and hands on the steering wheel — it reacts to **sudden, unexpected obstacles** like a fallen tree, a pothole, or a jaywalker, and swerves locally while still trying to follow Google's long-term suggestion.
- **Costmap** is the road map's data layer that paints "no-go zones" (construction sites, school zones) in red, "risky zones" in yellow, and "free space" in green.
- **TF Tree (Transform)** is the GPS and compass network that constantly tells the car *"you are here"* and *"the map is oriented that way"*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics** in ROS Navigation:
> - Robot footprint: typically **0.4 m × 0.4 m** for a TurtleBot3 Burger
> - Update rate of `move_base`: **20 Hz** (default)
> - Inflation radius for obstacles: **0.55 m** (default for TurtleBot3)
> - Controller frequency: **20 Hz**

### 1.3 The ROS Navigation Stack — Top-Level View

The ROS Navigation Stack is a **2D planar navigation** framework whose canonical architecture is:

$$\text{Perception (Sensors)} \rightarrow \text{Costmap (2D Grid)} \rightarrow \text{Global Planner} \rightarrow \text{Local Planner} \rightarrow \text{Velocity Commands} \rightarrow \text{Robot Base}$$

> [!TIP]
> A useful mental model: the **global planner is a *strategist*** (long-term thinker) and the **local planner is a *tactician*** (immediate, reactive decision-maker). They operate in a tight feedback loop at 20 Hz.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of the Problem

The monolithic path-planning problem is decomposed in the `move_base` node into the following logical pipeline:

1. **Sensor Data Ingestion**
   - Subscribes to `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` (LiDAR) and `sensor_msgs/PointCloud2` (Depth Camera).
   - Transform sensor data into the `odom` or `map` frame using the **TF tree**.

2. **Costmap Construction (`costmap_2d` package)**
   - Maintains three layers: **Static Map Layer**, **Obstacle Layer**, **Inflation Layer**.
   - Each cell holds an integer in the range $[0, 254]$ where $0$ = free space, $254$ = inscribed (lethal) obstacle, and $253$ = possibly circumscribed.

3. **Global Planning (`navfn` / `global_planner`)**
   - Receives a goal in `geometry_msgs/PoseStamped`.
   - Returns a `nav_msgs/Path` (a sequence of `PoseStamped` waypoints).
   - Default algorithm: **A\*** with the `grid_path` post-smoother.

4. **Local Planning (`dwa_local_planner` / `teb_local_planner`)**
   - Receives the global plan + current sensor scan.
   - Samples reachable velocities in the **Dynamic Window**.
   - Scores each trajectory using a weighted cost function.
   - Publishes `geometry_msgs/Twist` $(v_x, v_y, \omega_z)$ to `cmd_vel`.

5. **Recovery Behaviors**
   - If the local planner fails, behaviors like `rotate_recovery` and `clear_costmap_recovery` are triggered.

### 2.2 The A* Heuristic Function — Formal Definition

A\* is an **admissible, best-first search** algorithm. For a node $n$, it maintains the function:

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$

where:

- $g(n)$ = the actual cost from the start node to $n$ (known exactly).
- $h(n)$ = the heuristic estimate of the cost from $n$ to the goal (an estimate).
- $f(n)$ = the total estimated cost of the cheapest solution passing through $n$.

**Admissibility condition** (guarantees optimality):

$$h(n) \leq h^*(n) \quad \forall n$$

where $h^*(n)$ is the true minimum cost. The most common admissible heuristic on a 2D grid is the **Euclidean distance**:

$$h(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_{\text{goal}})^2 + (y_n - y_{\text{goal}})^2}$$

Alternative: **Manhattan distance** (faster, less accurate):

$$h(n) = \vert x_n - x_{\text{goal}} \vert + \vert y_n - y_{\text{goal}} \vert$$

### 2.3 The Dynamic Window Approach (DWA) — Cost Function

DWA samples velocities $(v, \omega)$ within a **reachable window** bounded by the robot's acceleration limits. For each sampled velocity, it forward-simulates a trajectory of horizon $T$ (typically 2–4 s) and scores it via:

$$C(\text{traj}) = \alpha \cdot C_{\text{heading}} + \beta \cdot C_{\text{dist}} + \gamma \cdot C_{\text{velocity}}$$

where:

- $C_{\text{heading}}$ = alignment of the trajectory's terminal heading with the goal.
- $C_{\text{dist}}$ = clearance to the nearest obstacle along the trajectory.
- $C_{\text{velocity}}$ = forward progress (rewards high $v$).
- $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ = non-negative tuning weights.

**Velocity Reachable Window (Dynamic Window)**:

$$V_d = \left\{ (v, \omega) \;\middle|\; v \in [v_a - \dot{v} \Delta t,\; v_a + \dot{v} \Delta t] \;\wedge\; \omega \in [\omega_a - \dot{\omega} \Delta t,\; \omega_a + \dot{\omega} \Delta t] \right\}$$

where subscript $a$ denotes the current actual velocity, $\dot{v}$ and $\dot{\omega}$ are acceleration bounds, and $\Delta t$ is the control period.

### 2.4 Configuration Space (C-Space) Concept

The **Configuration Space** $\mathcal{C}$ is the set of all possible robot configurations. The **C-space obstacle** $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ is the set of configurations in which the robot collides with a workspace obstacle $\mathcal{WO}$. The free space is:

$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$$

The path planning problem reduces to finding a continuous map:

$$\tau: [0, 1] \rightarrow \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}, \quad \tau(0) = q_{\text{start}}, \quad \tau(1) = q_{\text{goal}}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Term** | **Meaning** | **Equation / Value** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $f(n)$ | A* total estimated cost | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | dimensionless (or cells) |
| $g(n)$ | Cost from start to $n$ | accumulated path length | meters |
| $h(n)$ | Heuristic estimate to goal | Euclidean / Manhattan | meters |
| $C(\text{traj})$ | DWA trajectory cost | $\alpha C_h + \beta C_d + \gamma C_v$ | dimensionless |
| $V_d$ | DWA reachable velocity set | bounded by $\dot{v}, \dot{\omega}$ | m/s, rad/s |
| $T$ | DWA simulation horizon | 2 – 4 s (default 3.0) | seconds |
| $R_{\text{infl}}$ | Costmap inflation radius | $r_{\text{robot}} + r_{\text{safety}}$ | meters |
| $\rho_{\text{min}}$ | Minimum obstacle distance | tunable, default 0.30 m | meters |
| $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ | Collision-free configurations | $\mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ | set |
| $q$ | Robot configuration | $(x, y, \theta)$ for 2D planar | (m, m, rad) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The ROS Navigation Stack is the de-facto standard for autonomous mobile robots in warehouses (Amazon Kiva), restaurants (Servi robots), hospitals, and agricultural robotics. Understanding its tuning parameters is essential for robotics engineers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Heuristic Admissibility for A* on a 2D Grid

**Statement:** *The Euclidean distance is an admissible heuristic for A* on a 2D occupancy grid where the robot is a single point and movement cost equals geometric distance.*

**Proof (Constructive):**

**Step 1 — Define the optimal cost from node $n$ to goal $g$.**
Let $h^*(n)$ denote the true minimum cost. Since the robot is treated as a point in a 2D Euclidean plane, the shortest collision-free path in an obstacle-free plane is, in fact, the straight line segment from $n$ to $g$.

$$h^*(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_g)^2 + (y_n - y_g)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Define the heuristic function.**
The Euclidean distance is a direct computation in continuous space:

$$h(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_g)^2 + (y_n - y_g)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Establish the inequality.**
Comparing the two expressions, we have:

$$\begin{aligned}
h(n) &= \sqrt{(x_n - x_g)^2 + (y_n - y_g)^2} \\
h^*(n) &= \sqrt{(x_n - x_g)^2 + (y_n - y_g)^2} \quad \text{(if path is straight)} \\
h^*(n) &\geq \sqrt{(x_n - x_g)^2 + (y_n - y_g)^2} \quad \text{(in general, path ≥ straight line)}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore, by the triangle inequality in $\mathbb{R}^2$:

$$h(n) \leq h^*(n) \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.** Because the heuristic never overestimates, A* is guaranteed to terminate with an **optimal path** (Theorem of Hart, Nilsson & Raphael, 1968).

### 3.2 Derivation: DWA Trajectory Sampling Constraints

**Statement:** *The Dynamic Window restricts the search space to velocities that can be reached within one control period $\Delta t$.*

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Maximum velocity reachable in time $\Delta t$ from current $v_a$.**

By Newton's first-order kinematic integration:

$$v_{\text{max reachable}} = v_a + \dot{v}_{\text{max}} \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 2 — Minimum velocity reachable in time $\Delta t$.**

$$v_{\text{min reachable}} = v_a - \dot{v}_{\text{max}} \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 3 — Velocity window.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_d &= \{ v \in [v_a - \dot{v}_{\text{max}} \Delta t,\; v_a + \dot{v}_{\text{max}} \Delta t] \\
     &\quad \cap\; v \in [v_{\text{min}},\; v_{\text{max}}] \\
     &\quad \cap\; v \in [0,\; \sqrt{2 \cdot d_{\text{obs}} \cdot \dot{v}_{\text{max}}}]\, \}
\end{aligned}$$

The third constraint is the **admissibility under braking** — the velocity must be low enough that the robot can come to a complete stop before reaching the nearest obstacle at distance $d_{\text{obs}}$.

**Step 4 — Solve for the braking-limited velocity.**
Setting deceleration distance equal to $d_{\text{obs}}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{obs}} &= \frac{v_{\text{brake}}^2}{2 \dot{v}_{\text{max}}} \\
v_{\text{brake}} &= \sqrt{2 \cdot d_{\text{obs}} \cdot \dot{v}_{\text{max}}}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Costmap Inflation Radius

**Statement:** *The inflation layer marks a buffer zone around every obstacle to keep the robot's footprint at a safe clearance.*

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Robot footprint inscribed circle radius.**
For a circular approximation of a rectangular robot of size $L \times W$:

$$r_{\text{inscribed}} = \frac{1}{2} \min(L, W)$$

**Step 2 — Safety buffer.**
A user-defined safety distance $r_{\text{safety}}$ is added:

$$r_{\text{safety}} = \text{user parameter (default 0.10 m)}$$

**Step 3 — Total inflation radius.**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{infl}} &= r_{\text{inscribed}} + r_{\text{safety}} \\
                &= \frac{\min(L, W)}{2} + r_{\text{safety}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example for TurtleBot3 Burger ($L = 0.138$ m, $W = 0.178$ m, $r_{\text{safety}} = 0.10$ m):**

$$R_{\text{infl}} = \frac{\min(0.138,\, 0.178)}{2} + 0.10 = \frac{0.138}{2} + 0.10 = 0.069 + 0.10 = 0.169 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> ROS default inflation for TurtleBot3 is actually **0.55 m** to provide a generous corridor that helps the local planner succeed even in narrow passages.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Planners

| **Algorithm** | **Type** | **Optimality** | **Computational Complexity** | **Best Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dijkstra | Uninformed BFS | Optimal (in $g$) | $O(V^2)$ or $O(E + V \log V)$ | Small static maps |
| A* | Heuristic search | Optimal (with admissible $h$) | $O(E)$ in best case | Static maps, fast goals |
| DWA | Reactive sampling | Locally optimal | $O(N_v \cdot N_\omega \cdot T \cdot \Delta t)$ | Dynamic obstacles |
| TEB | Trajectory optimization | Locally optimal | Iterative (SLSQP solver) | Holonomic robots |
| RRT | Sampling-based | Probabilistically complete | $O(n \log n)$ | High-dimensional C-space |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ROS Navigation Stack — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["SENSORS\nLaserScan PointCloud2 Odometry"] --> nodeB["TF2\nTRANSFORM SERVER"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["COSTMAP 2D\nStatic Map Layer\nObstacle Layer\nInflation Layer"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["GLOBAL PLANNER\nnavfn or global_planner\nA star algorithm"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["LOCAL PLANNER\ndwa_local_planner or teb"]
    nodeF["GOAL POSE\nPoseStamped"] --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeG["MOVE BASE\nCORE NODE\n20 Hz loop"]
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH["CMD VEL\nTwist messages"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["ROBOT BASE\ndiff_drive_controller"]
    nodeI --> nodeA
    nodeJ["RECOVERY\nBEHAVIORS"] -.-> nodeG
    nodeG -.-> nodeJ
```

### 4.2 Hierarchical Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Start: Receive Goal Pose"] --> nodeB{"Is global plan\nvalid?"}
    nodeB -- "Yes" --> nodeC["Run DWA\nlocal planner"]
    nodeB -- "No" --> nodeD["Trigger recovery:\nclear costmap"]
    nodeD --> nodeE{"Recovery\nsucceeded?"}
    nodeE -- "Yes" --> nodeC
    nodeE -- "No" --> nodeF["ABORT:\npublish empty Twist"]
    nodeC --> nodeG{"Goal reached\nwithin tolerance?"}
    nodeG -- "No" --> nodeC
    nodeG -- "Yes" --> nodeH["STOP and\npublish success"]
```

### 4.3 Coordinate Transform Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["MAP\nStatic global frame"] --> nodeB["ODOM\nAccurate short term\ndrifts over time"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["BASE FOOTPRINT\nRobot center"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["BASE LINK\nRobot chassis"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["LASER FRAME\nSensor origin"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["WHEEL LEFT\nWheel joint"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["WHEEL RIGHT\nWheel joint"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The TF tree is **strictly hierarchical** in ROS. A correct transform from `map` to `base_link` typically requires two hops: `map` → `odom` (from AMCL localization) and `odom` → `base_link` (from wheel odometry). If the TF chain is broken, `move_base` will not function.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**  
Differentiate between **global path planning** and **local path planning** in the ROS Navigation Stack. Name the default algorithms used in `move_base` for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Global Path Planning | Local Path Planning |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Compute a holistic route from start to goal | Generate immediate, reactive velocity commands |
| **Input** | Static map + goal pose | Global plan + live sensor data |
| **Output** | `nav_msgs/Path` (waypoint sequence) | `geometry_msgs/Twist` $(v, \omega)$ |
| **Update rate** | Low (~1 Hz, on demand) | High (20 Hz) |
| **Default algorithm in ROS** | A* (`navfn` / `global_planner`) | DWA (`dwa_local_planner`) |
| **Handles dynamic obstacles** | No | Yes |

*(Award 1 mark for the table headers, 1 mark for the two algorithms, 1 mark for the distinction in input/output.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**  
What is a **costmap** in ROS? List its three primary layers and briefly state the purpose of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **costmap** is a 2D occupancy grid used by the navigation stack to represent the environment, where each cell holds an integer in $[0, 254]$ indicating traversability. The three primary layers are:

1. **Static Map Layer** — Loads the pre-built map from the `map_server` (PGM/YAML). Mark walls, furniture, and immovable structures. (1 mark)
2. **Obstacle Layer** — Ingests live sensor data (LiDAR/PointCloud) and marks newly detected obstacles that were not in the static map. (1 mark)
3. **Inflation Layer** — Adds a "buffer zone" of decaying cost around every obstacle to ensure the robot maintains a safety distance. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model – 14 marks]**

**(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, describe the architecture of the **ROS Navigation Stack** and explain the role of the `move_base` node. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)** in detail, including the velocity sampling window, the trajectory evaluation cost function, and how obstacle avoidance is achieved. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```
